Important clues were recent residence in an endemic area with consumption of unwashed produce, cholestatic liver tests with eosinophilia, ultrasound appearance of a tubular moving structure within the common bile duct, and direct extraction of a motile worm at ERCP. These features identify Ascaris lumbricoides as the causative parasite.

hints.text: A 37-year-old male shepherd presents to the clinic with a 5-day history of a lesion on the dorsum of his right hand. He reports the lesion began as a small itchy papule, progressed to a vesicle and now is an ulcer ~2.5 cm in diameter with a central black, necrotic eschar. Marked non-pitting swelling surrounds the lesion, extending to the mid-forearm; he has low-grade fever (38.1 °C) and tender axillary lymphadenopathy but no purulent drainage.

Past medical history notable only for well-controlled hypertension. He is not immunosuppressed and takes no glucocorticoids. He received routine childhood vaccines but no recent travel. He reports the lesion has not responded to a 3-day course of oral amoxicillin prescribed at a local clinic; systemic symptoms have been minimal until the current swelling.

WBC 12.6 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance; C-reactive protein 58 mg/L. Gram stain of material from the lesion shows large, Gram‑positive, rectangular rods arranged singly and in short chains. Motility testing in the laboratory is negative. After 24–48 hours on blood agar the organism grows as large, non-haemolytic, grey colonies with a slightly mucoid appearance.

Soft-tissue ultrasound of the hand and forearm demonstrates marked subcutaneous oedema with intact fascial planes and no discrete abscess or gas in the tissues. Plain radiograph of the hand shows soft-tissue swelling without bone involvement.

On directed questioning he reports that two weeks before onset several of his sheep died suddenly on the farm; in the week prior to the lesion he had a superficial cut while shearing and handled raw fleeces without gloves.

explanation: The combination of a painless black eschar with extensive non-pitting oedema, large Gram‑positive non-motile rods on direct stain, non-haemolytic mucoid colonies, and recent handling of dead livestock is most consistent with infection by Bacillus anthracis. Confirmation is by culture and PCR for the organism; management includes prompt intravenous antibiotics with agents active against the organism and consultation regarding antitoxin and public-health measures, and wound care without unnecessary debridement unless indicated.

hints.text: A 52-year-old male postal-sorting facility worker is admitted with 48 hours of fever, severe chest pain, progressive dyspnoea, and a dry cough. On arrival he is febrile to 39.4 °C, tachycardic, hypotensive (BP 88/54 mmHg), and tachypnoeic with oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Physical exam shows jugular venous fullness and bibasilar crackles; there is no rash and no obvious skin lesions.

He is a current smoker (30 pack‑year) with well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Over the previous week he had a prodrome of malaise and low-grade fever treated at home with paracetamol. He reports no recent travel, no known sick contacts, and was not given antibiotics prior to arrival. He works daily in a sorting area that processes international parcels.

Initial labs: WBC 22.1 × 10^9/L, lactate 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 125 μmol/L. Blood cultures flagged positive within 18 hours; Gram stain from the blood culture bottle shows large, Gram‑positive rods with a box-like appearance. Colony morphology on blood agar after subculture: large, non‑haemolytic, domed colonies with a sticky texture. Routine motility testing demonstrates no motility.

Chest radiograph shows a widened mediastinum and bilateral pleural effusions without focal lobar consolidation. Contrast-enhanced CT chest demonstrates prominent mediastinal lymphadenopathy with stranding consistent with haemorrhagic mediastinitis and moderate bilateral pleural effusions.

Further occupational inquiry reveals that three days before symptom onset he opened a suspicious-seeming envelope that released a fine powder; several colleagues who handled the same batch of mail reported similar prodromal symptoms in the prior week. The facility processes imported animal-product shipments intermittently.

explanation: The acute febrile respiratory illness with mediastinal widening, haemorrhagic mediastinitis on CT, large Gram‑positive non-motile rods in blood culture, and a history of handling a powder-containing parcel point to Bacillus anthracis causing inhalational disease. Diagnosis is supported by culture and PCR; management requires prompt broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy with agents effective against this organism, aggressive supportive care including pleural drainage when indicated, and notification of public-health authorities for exposure tracing and consideration of antitoxin therapy.

hints.text: A 24-year-old laboratory technician presents to urgent care with abrupt onset of profuse vomiting and diffuse abdominal cramping that began about two hours after a staff cafeteria lunch. Over the next four hours he has had more than ten episodes of non-bloody emesis, is light-headed when standing, tachycardic to 110/min and has dry mucous membranes.

He is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, has no recent travel and has not received antibiotics. Two coworkers who ate the same meal reported similar isolated vomiting without diarrhoea. There is no jaundice, no bloody stools and no prior gastrointestinal disease.

Basic chemistry shows Na 135 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, Cl 88 mmol/L, HCO3− 34 mmol/L, creatinine 120 µmol/L. Haemoglobin 145 g/L, white cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L. Stool microscopy shows no fecal leukocytes. A sample of the patient’s vomitus and the leftover food were submitted for microbiological testing.

Abdominal radiograph and point-of-care ultrasound are unremarkable: no free intraperitoneal air, no biliary dilatation and normal gas pattern. There is no evidence on imaging to suggest a surgical abdomen.

The patient reports reheated fried rice from the staff canteen that had been held at ambient temperature for several hours before reheating. Public-health laboratory culture of the leftover rice grew abundant, beta-haemolytic, motile, spore-forming Gram-positive rods; the laboratory also reported recovery of a heat-stable, low–molecular-weight vomiting-inducing toxin in the food sample. The short incubation interval and these findings were communicated to the clinic.

explanation: This presentation—multiple rapid-onset vomiting after reheated rice that had been held at room temperature, isolation of abundant motile, spore-forming Gram-positive rods producing a heat-stable emetic toxin—points to Bacillus cereus food poisoning (emetic type). Supportive care and rehydration are the usual initial management.

hints.text: A 62-year-old man develops fever to 39.2°C with rigors, tachycardia and hypotension 48 hours after starting lipid-containing total parenteral nutrition through a newly placed right internal jugular central venous catheter following bowel resection. The catheter exit site appears clean; he requires intravenous fluids but no vasopressors initially.

He has type 2 diabetes (well controlled on oral therapy) and is postoperative day 3 after elective hemicolectomy complicated by prolonged ileus. Broad-spectrum antibiotics had been stopped 24 hours earlier. He has no prosthetic cardiac valves and no history of intravenous drug use.

Blood cultures drawn from the central line and peripherally flagged positive within 10–12 hours and revealed large, motile, spore-forming Gram-positive rods showing beta-haemolysis. White cell count is 18.2 × 10^9/L and CRP is 210 mg/L. Preliminary susceptibility testing showed resistance to several beta-lactam antibiotics.

Contrast CT of chest and abdomen demonstrates no intra-thoracic or intra-abdominal collection. Transthoracic echocardiography shows no valvular vegetation. There is no radiographic source of sepsis identified.

The central line was removed and empirical vancomycin started. The patient defervesced and haemodynamics stabilised within 36–48 hours; repeat blood cultures were sterile after 48 hours. Culture of the catheter tip yielded the same motile, spore-forming Gram-positive rods that had been recovered from the blood.

explanation: A central-line–associated bloodstream infection with rapid growth of motile, spore-forming, beta-haemolytic Gram-positive rods that cleared only after catheter removal and glycopeptide therapy is characteristic of Bacillus cereus causing catheter-related sepsis in patients receiving parenteral nutrition. Removal of the infected device and targeted antimicrobial therapy are key to cure.

hints.text: A 24-year-old female day-care worker presents with a 3-week history of paroxysmal cough. Episodes occur predominantly at night and end with forceful retching and occasional vomiting; parents report a high-pitched inspiratory gasp after severe coughing spells. She is afebrile, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and auscultation shows mild expiratory wheeze without focal crackles.

She received routine childhood immunisations but has not had an adult booster. No recent antibiotic use. Several infants in her room developed prolonged cough over the past month; two were kept home because of worsening symptoms. She has no known chronic lung disease and is a non-smoker.
Complete blood count shows WBC 12.4 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 8.2 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 5 mg/L. Multiplex viral PCR on a nasopharyngeal swab is negative. Routine sputum culture is unrevealing.
Chest radiograph demonstrates no lobar consolidation; there is mild peribronchial cuffing and hyperinflation, but no focal air‑space opacity to suggest bacterial pneumonia.

Public-health review of the day-care identified a cluster of prolonged cough illnesses among infants and staff. Nasopharyngeal testing from two infants and the patient returned positive for the same fastidious, aerobic Gram‑negative coccobacillus by PCR; local health services began macrolide prophylaxis for close contacts.

explanation: Diagnosis: Bordetella pertussis infection. The subacute paroxysmal cough with post‑tussive vomiting and inspiratory gasps in an adult working in a day-care, lymphocytosis on CBC, a compatible chest radiograph, and PCR confirmation from nasopharyngeal specimens in an outbreak among infants and staff support the diagnosis. Macrolide prophylaxis for close contacts and booster immunisation are key control measures.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents with 48 hours of progressive right-sided facial weakness: inability to close the right eye, drooping of the mouth, and hyperacusis. He also reports ten days of fatigue and migratory arthralgias without rash or vesicles.

He returned three weeks ago from a week-long camping trip in a temperate, wooded region where he slept in a tent and noted several insect bites. He removed one small tick from his calf but did not seek care. No significant past medical history and he is otherwise immunocompetent.

Peripheral blood tests: WBC 6.4 × 10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L. Lumbar puncture shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with 160 × 10^6/L cells, protein 1.2 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.4 mmol/L); CSF Gram stain and routine cultures negative. Initial serum antibody testing was non-reactive.
Contrast MRI of the brain shows enhancement of the intracanalicular and proximal extracranial segment of the right facial nerve and subtle meningeal enhancement, without parenchymal lesions.

He was started on intravenous ceftriaxone; by day 7 there was marked improvement in eye closure and oral competence, with near-complete recovery at six weeks. Convalescent serology performed four weeks after presentation converted to high-titre IgG on immunoblot.

explanation: Acute cranial neuropathy with preceding systemic symptoms, documented tick exposure, CSF lymphocytic meningitis, MRI facial nerve enhancement, neurologic improvement with appropriate antibiotic therapy and later seroconversion point to Borrelia burgdorferi infection (manifesting as early disseminated neurological involvement of Lyme borreliosis).

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to urgent care with 48 hours of cramping lower abdominal pain and diarrhoea that began watery and became streaked with blood. He reports fever to 39.0 °C, three episodes of non-bilious vomiting, and lightheadedness on standing. On examination he is tachycardic to 110/min, temperature 38.7 °C, and has diffuse abdominal tenderness greatest in the right lower quadrant without peritoneal signs.

Previously healthy, no regular medications, no recent antibiotics and no travel outside the country. He works as a barista. He returned three days ago from a weekend with friends and says two nights before symptom onset he ate a rotisserie chicken from a late-night takeaway. No prior inflammatory bowel disease in him or family.

Blood tests: leucocyte count 14.0 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, CRP 72 mg/L, creatinine 60 μmol/L. Stool microscopy shows numerous neutrophils and positive fecal occult blood; routine stool ova and parasite panel pending.

Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound targeted to the right lower quadrant demonstrates a thickened terminal ileum that is non-compressible; the appendix visualised and measuring normal calibre without surrounding free fluid. Clinicians favoured ileocolitis over acute appendicitis.

On detailed questioning he recalls handling and carving the rotisserie chicken at home and rinsing the cutting board with cold water only. Symptoms began about 48 hours after that meal. The combination of febrile, often bloody ileocolitis beginning ~48 hours after poultry exposure pointed to a bacterial enteritis linked to poultry.

explanation: Presentation of febrile, crampy diarrhoea progressing to bloody stools within 48 hours of eating undercooked/handled poultry, neutrophilic stool and ileocecal wall thickening are most consistent with Campylobacter jejuni enteritis. Diagnosis is supported by stool culture showing a thermophilic, motile, oxidase-positive Gram-negative organism; macrolide therapy is the usual treatment when antibiotics are indicated.

hints.text: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric clinic with 36 hours of fever, diffuse abdominal cramps and frequent loose stools that have become frankly bloody. She has passed six loose stools in the previous 24 hours, has reduced oral intake, and appears tired on examination. Temperature is 38.5 °C with diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding.

Developmentally normal, immunisations up to date, no chronic medical conditions. Family returned yesterday from a weekend at a small dairy farm where she was given unpasteurised cow's milk and had close contact with calves. Her 4-year-old brother developed mild diarrhoea that did not require medical attention.

Plain abdominal radiograph obtained in the emergency department shows no dilated bowel loops, no air–fluid levels and no radiographic signs of intussusception or obstruction. Clinical assessment triggered intravenous rehydration.

Parents confirm raw farm milk ingestion occurred roughly 48 hours before symptom onset and that she handled animals with intermittent handwashing. The temporal relationship between raw milk consumption, farm-animal contact and rapid-onset bloody diarrhoea focused testing on bacterial zoonotic causes linked to dairy and farm exposure.

Stool culture returned positive for a motile, oxidase-positive Gram-negative organism that grew best at 42 °C; the laboratory reported susceptibility to macrolide antibiotics. Peripheral blood: leucocyte count 12.4 × 10^9/L, CRP 110 mg/L.

explanation: Rapid-onset, often bloody diarrhoea after raw milk and farm animal exposure with isolation of a thermophilic, motile, oxidase-positive Gram-negative bacillus that is macrolide sensitive fits Campylobacter jejuni. Supportive care and macrolide therapy when indicated are the typical management steps; epidemiology here points to a dairy-associated zoonotic transmission.

hints.text: A 22-year-old woman presents to an urban sexual health clinic with a 2-week history of increasing mucopurulent vaginal discharge, dysuria, and light intermenstrual spotting. She reports mild suprapubic discomfort but no high fever or severe abdominal pain. On pelvic examination the cervix is erythematous with friability and a small amount of purulent discharge; bimanual exam elicits cervical motion tenderness.

She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and seeking contraception. She reports a single new male partner about 6 weeks ago and inconsistent condom use. No prior sexually transmitted infections, no intrauterine device placement, and no recent instrumentation.

Endocervical swab microscopy shows numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes; Gram stain demonstrates no gram-negative intracellular diplococci. First-void urine and endocervical nucleic acid amplification testing are positive for organism-specific nucleic acid; nucleic acid testing for Neisseria gonorrhoeae is negative. White blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 18 mg/L.

Transvaginal ultrasound demonstrates no tubo-ovarian abscess and normal-appearing ovaries, but a small amount of cul-de-sac free fluid and mild endometrial thickening consistent with pelvic inflammatory change.

She received a single 1 g oral dose of azithromycin and her partner was notified and treated empirically. Symptoms substantially improved within 5 days and repeat nucleic acid testing at 3 weeks was negative.

explanation: The presentation of young woman with mucopurulent cervicitis, cervical friability and cervical motion tenderness, a positive nucleic acid amplification test from endocervical/first-void urine with gonorrhoea testing negative, and clinical cure after single-dose macrolide therapy are most consistent with infection by Chlamydia trachomatis, a common cause of urogenital cervicitis and mild pelvic inflammatory disease.

hints.text: A 19-year-old university student presents to the emergency department with 3 days of progressive right testicular pain radiating to the groin, associated with dysuria and a thin yellow urethral discharge. He had low-grade fever to 38.0 °C. On exam the right hemiscrotum is swollen and tender with an exquisitely tender epididymis; cremasteric reflex is intact and there is no high-riding testis.

He is otherwise healthy, reports insertive unprotected intercourse with a new female partner 2 weeks earlier, and has no history of urinary tract instrumentation. No mumps exposures, no recent travel, and no prior sexually transmitted infections.

Urine microscopy demonstrates >10 leukocytes per high-power field and culture shows no growth after 48 hours. Gram stain of urethral discharge reveals polymorphonuclear leukocytes but no intracellular gram-negative diplococci. First-void urine nucleic acid amplification testing is positive for organism-specific nucleic acid. Serum white blood cell count 11.6 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 32 mg/L.
Scrotal Doppler ultrasound shows an enlarged, hyperemic right epididymis with preserved testicular blood flow and no sonographic evidence of torsion or abscess.

He was started on doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10 days with analgesia; pain and discharge improved markedly within 48–72 hours. He and his partner were treated empirically and advised to avoid sexual activity until therapy was completed and symptoms resolved.

explanation: A young sexually active man with acute epididymitis, urethral discharge, a positive nucleic acid amplification test from first-void urine with no bacterial growth on routine culture and rapid improvement with doxycycline is most consistent with Chlamydia trachomatis infection, a frequent cause of nongonococcal urethritis and epididymitis in this age group.

hints.text: An 78-year-old woman is brought from a convalescent ward with three days of profuse watery diarrhoea (8–12 stools per 24 h), crampy lower abdominal pain and new-onset confusion. Temperature 38.2°C, pulse 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg; abdomen diffusely tender without peritonism.

She has type 2 diabetes and stage 3 chronic kidney disease. Two weeks earlier she completed a seven-day course of intravenous ceftriaxone for hospital-acquired pneumonia and was discharged to the ward five days before the diarrhoea began. She is taking a proton‑pump inhibitor for reflux.

Blood tests: white cell count 22.3 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 110 g/L, creatinine 210 µmol/L (baseline 110 µmol/L), albumin 28 g/L, CRP 146 mg/L. A stool specimen is sent for microbiology; basic microscopy shows numerous leucocytes.
Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen shows diffuse thickening of the colon wall, most pronounced in the ascending and transverse colon, with pericolonic fat stranding but no free air or portal venous gas.

Empiric oral therapy directed at toxin-mediated colitis was started; the diarrhoea improved markedly within 48 hours. Subsequent stool testing returned positive for a toxigenic strain by nucleic acid amplification and endoscopic inspection demonstrated raised yellow‑white plaques on the colonic mucosa.

explanation: Clinical picture is hospital-associated, post-antibiotic watery diarrhoea with leukocytosis, acute kidney injury and CT changes of colitis. Rapid response to oral therapy targeted at toxin-mediated colitis plus positive NAAT and pseudomembranous colitis on endoscopy confirm infection with Clostridioides difficile.

hints.text: A 44-year-old woman presents to urgent care with five days of watery diarrhoea (6–10 stools daily), lower abdominal cramping and low-grade fever. She reports lightheadedness on standing and has reduced urine output. No recent travel; stools are non-bloody.
She is otherwise healthy apart from gastro-oesophageal reflux disease treated with a proton‑pump inhibitor. Ten days ago she received a five-day course of oral clindamycin after a dental extraction.

Blood tests: white cell count 15.6 × 10^9/L, creatinine 95 µmol/L (no known baseline elevation), albumin 32 g/L, CRP 78 mg/L. Stool sample tested at presentation is positive for glutamate dehydrogenase and for toxin by enzyme immunoassay.

She works as a receptionist in an outpatient clinic; a cluster of diarrhoeal illness among clinic staff was reported in the week before her symptoms began. No recent antibiotic use in household contacts.

She was started on an oral, non-absorbed antibacterial agent recommended for toxin-mediated colitis; stools became formed and frequency fell to one–two per day within 72 hours. A confirmatory nucleic acid amplification test for toxin genes was subsequently positive.

explanation: Recent clindamycin exposure, PPI use and a clinic outbreak with the described syndrome point to an antibiotic‑associated, toxin-mediated colitis. Positive stool GDH and toxin EIA plus NAAT confirmation and rapid clinical improvement on targeted oral therapy establish the diagnosis of Clostridioides difficile infection.

hints.text: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department 18 hours after a family dinner with progressive diplopia, slurred speech, dysphagia and dry mouth. He reports increasing difficulty breathing and generalized weakness; on exam he is afebrile, alert, has bilateral ptosis and impaired extraocular movements, symmetric facial weakness, diminished gag reflex, and decreased deep-tendon reflexes but intact sensation.

He has well-controlled hypertension, takes an ACE inhibitor, and has no recent travel. Family reports he ate several spoonfuls of home-canned vegetables prepared in an improvised water-bath canner earlier that week. No one else in the household has required care beyond mild transient nausea.

Routine blood tests are unremarkable (WBC 7.4 × 10^9/L, Na+ 139 mmol/L, K+ 4.2 mmol/L). Lumbar puncture shows normal cell count and protein. Repetitive nerve stimulation demonstrates low-amplitude compound motor action potentials that increase with rapid (20–50 Hz) stimulation and with brief voluntary exercise.
Non-contrast CT brain is normal. Chest radiograph shows small-volume lungs with bibasilar subsegmental atelectasis; no focal consolidation to suggest pneumonia.

Leftover home-canned vegetables were submitted for testing: anaerobic culture grew a spore-forming Gram-positive bacillus and a mouse bioassay detected a heat-labile neurotoxin that prevents presynaptic acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction; the patient received antitoxin and respiratory support.

explanation: Findings—acute, symmetric, descending cranial nerve palsies with bulbar weakness after ingestion of improperly home-canned food, EMG showing facilitation with high-frequency stimulation, and isolation of an anaerobic spore-forming Gram-positive bacillus with a presynaptic neurotoxin—are diagnostic of infection with Clostridium botulinum (foodborne botulism).

hints.text: A 34-year-old male farm worker presented 10 hours after his left thigh was crushed beneath a silage harvester. He described severe, rapidly worsening pain out of proportion to inspection. On exam the left thigh was tense, mottled, markedly swollen with palpable crepitus; temperature 38.5°C, heart rate 118/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg.

He is otherwise healthy, does not have diabetes, and is a current smoker. The wound was heavily contaminated with soil and decaying plant material; he delayed coming to hospital because of travel from the barn and had taken over-the-counter ibuprofen.

Initial bloods: WBC 18.4 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 96 g/L, platelets 220 × 10^9/L, CRP 210 mg/L, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, CK 3 200 U/L and LDH 980 U/L. Peripheral smear shows evidence of acute haemolysis. Blood cultures were obtained and sent.

Plain radiograph and CT of the left thigh demonstrated extensive subcutaneous and intramuscular gas tracking along fascial planes without fracture. There was loss of normal muscle bulk and no focal collection amenable to simple drainage.

Urgent surgical exploration revealed pale, non-contractile muscle with abundant gas bubbles and a fetid brown exudate but little purulence. Gram stain of wound fluid showed large, Gram-positive rectangular rods. Anaerobic culture grew rapidly with a wide, double-zone haemolysis on blood agar and lecithinase activity was noted on egg-yolk medium; the team began high-dose penicillin and serial debridement.

explanation: This presentation and operative findings are most consistent with invasive myonecrosis caused by Clostridium perfringens. Rapid onset after contaminated traumatic injury, severe pain with crepitus, evidence of haemolysis, intramuscular gas on imaging, and characteristic large Gram-positive rods with rapid growth and double-zone haemolysis point to this pathogen. Management requires urgent surgical debridement and high-dose penicillin; adjuncts may include clindamycin and hyperbaric oxygen in selected cases.

hints.text: A 55-year-old woman presented with sudden onset of colicky abdominal pain and profuse watery diarrhoea that began 10 hours after attending a family buffet. She reported 12 watery stools in the first 18 hours, lightheadedness on standing, no vomiting or bloody stools, and was afebrile with pulse 102/min and blood pressure 110/70 mmHg.

Several other guests developed similar brief diarrhoeal illness. The meal included a large tray of roast beef that had been carved and left at room temperature for several hours before reheating. Her past medical history includes stage 3 chronic kidney disease and hypertension; she is not immunosuppressed.

Bloods: WBC 8.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L, Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, creatinine 150 µmol/L (baseline 120 µmol/L). Stool microscopy showed no fecal leukocytes; routine bacterial cultures for common enteric pathogens returned negative. A stool specimen was sent for anaerobic culture and enterotoxin testing.

She received oral rehydration and potassium replacement; antibiotics were withheld. Symptoms diminished substantially within 24 hours and resolved completely by 36 hours; her creatinine trended back toward baseline. Other affected guests followed a similarly brief, self-limited course.

Public health testing of a leftover roast beef showed heavy growth of large, spore-forming Gram-positive bacilli on anaerobic culture. Both the food specimen and the patient's stool were positive by immunoassay for an enterotoxin produced during sporulation, implicating contamination of cooked meat kept at ambient temperature.

explanation: The short incubation after a meat dish left at room temperature, abrupt watery diarrhoea without fever or fecal leukocytes, rapid resolution with supportive care, and detection of a sporulation-associated enterotoxin in both food and stool indicate foodborne illness due to Clostridium perfringens. Management is supportive (rehydration, electrolyte correction); prevention involves proper cooling and reheating of cooked meats and prompt refrigeration.

hints.text: A 24-year-old male landscaper presents to the emergency unit with 72 hours of progressive jaw tightness and difficulty opening his mouth. He reports painful, brief spasms of the face and neck triggered by loud noises and by being touched; he is otherwise alert, afebrile, and complaining of severe muscle pain between spasms.

He completed only a partial childhood immunization series and has not received any adult boosters; he has no chronic medical conditions and no prior neurologic disease. Symptoms began gradually after several days of mild stiffness and progressed to frequent, painful spasms that limit oral intake.

Full blood count: white blood cell count 12.1 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 8 mg/L. Serum creatine kinase 3 600 U/L. Blood cultures at 48 hours remain without growth; an aerobic wound swab (sent on presentation) grew mixed skin flora.

Non-contrast CT of the head shows no intracranial acute abnormality. Chest radiograph is clear. Plain radiograph of the left foot demonstrates a small puncture-track soft-tissue lucency without retained radiopaque foreign body.

On directed questioning the patient recalls stepping on a heavily soiled nail while barefoot 9 days before onset; the puncture wound was not professionally cleaned and he has not received any post-exposure prophylaxis.

explanation: The combination of trismus, stimulus-triggered painful spasms, recent contaminated puncture wound, and absent adult booster is characteristic of infection by Clostridium tetani. Diagnosis is clinical; laboratory and imaging are nondiagnostic. Management centers on wound care, administration of human tetanus immunoglobulin for passive neutralization, antimicrobial therapy (eg, metronidazole), and control of muscle spasms with sedatives and supportive care, plus active immunization once stable.

hints.text: A 10-day-old male neonate is brought from a rural home-birth clinic with 48 hours of poor feeding, increased generalized rigidity and intermittent spasms that are provoked by handling. The infant has a tight jaw and difficulty latching; cry is strong between spasms and there is no fever.

The birth was at home with a midwife; the mother received no antenatal vaccination against the relevant peripartum pathogens and reports use of an unsterile instrument to cut the cord. The baby passed meconium and had no perinatal complications otherwise.
Capillary glucose 3.2 mmol/L. White blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 4 mg/L. Serum creatine kinase 4 800 U/L. Blood cultures are pending and currently show no growth at 24 hours.
Cranial ultrasound performed to evaluate for intracranial infection or haemorrhage is normal. Chest radiograph shows clear lung fields without consolidation to suggest aspiration.

On inspection the umbilical stump is inflamed and coated with darkened material the mother describes as a local herbal dressing; symptoms began approximately 5–7 days after the cord was cut and treated in this way, and no maternal immunization was documented.

explanation: A neonate with poor feeding, trismus, stimulus‑induced spasms beginning days after unsterile cord care and with an unimmunized mother points to infection by Clostridium tetani via the umbilical stump. Diagnosis is clinical; management requires supportive care, wound/care-site decontamination, administration of human tetanus immunoglobulin for passive neutralization, appropriate antimicrobials, and vaccination of the mother and, when appropriate, active immunization of the infant.

hints.text: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after 48 hours of progressive sore throat, high fever (measured at home 38.7 °C), increasing drooling and muffled voice. On examination she is febrile, tachypnoeic (respiratory rate 30/min) with inspiratory stridor; inspection of the oropharynx shows a thick, grey, adherent membrane covering both tonsils and the posterior pharynx with bilateral tender, firm cervical lymphadenopathy.

She attends a crowded daycare centre. Parents report she missed the preschool booster dose of triple vaccine and had no documented booster in the past 2 years. No prior significant medical problems and no recent antibiotics. Household includes a newborn sibling.

Blood: white cell count 18.6 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance; C-reactive protein 112 mg/L. A throat swab Gram stain shows pleomorphic, club-shaped Gram-positive bacillary forms; special staining performed in the laboratory highlights metachromatic intracellular granules.

Lateral neck radiograph obtained because of increasing work of breathing shows narrowing of the oropharyngeal air column with thickening of the prevertebral soft tissues and subglottic narrowing compared with previous films.

Because of airway compromise the patient received urgent airway support and a single dose of equine-derived antitoxin directed against the circulating exotoxin, followed by IV benzylpenicillin; over the next 24 hours fever fell and respiratory distress and drooling improved as portions of the membrane detached. Subsequent culture from the pharyngeal swab produced grey–black colonies on tellurite-containing medium and immunodiffusion testing demonstrated toxin production.

explanation: This presentation—an un- or under-immunised child with an adherent grey pharyngeal membrane, inspiratory stridor, pleomorphic club-shaped Gram-positive bacilli on Gram stain, growth of black colonies on tellurite medium and demonstration of an exotoxin—supports infection by Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Management requires prompt administration of antitoxin to neutralise circulating toxin, appropriate antibiotic therapy (for example penicillin or erythromycin) and isolation/chemoprophylaxis of close contacts; monitor for cardiac and neurological complications.

hints.text: A 32-year-old man presents to a travel clinic with a non-healing ulcer on his right lower leg of 3 weeks’ duration. The lesion began after a minor thermal burn while travelling; it has a central grey adherent membrane, surrounding erythema and oedema, tender regional lymphadenopathy and a low-grade fever.

He returned from a six-week trip to coastal rural areas in Southeast Asia 4 weeks ago, where he did agricultural work walking barefoot. He reports having missed routine adult boosters and says he has not had a tetanus/diphtheria booster for many years. He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes treated with metformin.

Blood tests: white cell count 12.4 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 28 mg/L. Wound swab Gram stain reveals pleomorphic Gram-positive bacillary organisms; no obvious polymicrobial growth on initial culture plates at 24 hours.

Soft-tissue ultrasound of the lower leg shows a superficial subcutaneous collection without evidence of deep abscess or underlying cortical bone involvement; plain radiograph of the tibia and fibula shows no periosteal reaction.

Public-health follow-up revealed several household contacts developed sore throats within 10 days of his return. The regional laboratory reported that the wound isolate produced grey–black colonies on potassium tellurite medium and an in vitro immunodiffusion assay showed production of an exotoxin; the lesion improved on oral erythromycin with local wound care.

explanation: A chronic membranous skin ulcer in a returned traveller with missed booster immunisation, pleomorphic Gram-positive bacilli on microscopy and growth of tellurite-positive colonies with demonstration of exotoxin is consistent with cutaneous infection due to Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Treatment includes appropriate antibiotics (for example macrolide or penicillin), local wound care, assessment for toxin-mediated systemic effects, and public-health measures including contact tracing and consideration of antitoxin if systemic toxin effects are suspected.

hints.text: A 3-year-old girl is brought to urgent care with 48 hours of fever to 38.9°C, increasing irritability and refusal to eat. Her parent reports drooling and mouth pain. On examination she is alert but fussy, with multiple shallow ulcers on the lateral tongue and buccal mucosa and 2–4 mm clear vesicles on the palms and the plantar surface of both feet; mucous membranes are erythematous and she has mild tachycardia consistent with dehydration from poor oral intake.

Healthy term infant with routine childhood immunisations. No recent travel. She attends a local daycare; several other children developed fever and sore throat over the past week. No prior episodes of similar rash and no known chronic medical conditions.

Blood: WBC 9.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 4.6 × 10^9/L, CRP 7 mg/L. Throat swab PCR targeted at common viral causes of pediatric febrile vesicular illness detected a small, non-enveloped single-stranded RNA virus; bacterial cultures from throat and lesion swabs were negative.

Close clinical photograph of the hands and oral cavity shows multiple shallow, round vesicles on erythematous bases concentrated on the palmar surfaces and interdigital webs, and small oval shallow ulcers on the lateral tongue; no pustulation or deep necrosis is evident.

Public-health follow-up identified a cluster of similar febrile, vesicular illnesses at the child’s daycare over the past week, and stool samples from two symptomatic classmates were positive by the same PCR assay for the same small RNA virus; the cluster onset occurred during late summer.

explanation: Presentation of fever, painful oral ulcers and vesicular lesions on palms and soles in a toddler with a daycare cluster in summer is most consistent with hand–foot–and–mouth disease caused by Coxsackievirus (an enterovirus). Laboratory confirmation commonly uses PCR from throat, stool or lesion swabs; management is supportive (hydration, analgesia) and public-health measures for the daycare setting.

hints.text: A 24-year-old hotel maintenance worker presents with 10 days of watery diarrhoea, 6–10 loose, nonbloody stools per day, crampy lower abdominal pain and 3 kg weight loss. He reports dry mouth and lightheadedness on standing. On examination he is afebrile, heart rate 98/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, mucous membranes are dry and abdominal exam shows mild diffuse tenderness without peritonism.

He is otherwise healthy, not on regular medication, and denied recent antibiotic use. Two weeks before presentation he supervised draining and cleaning of a hotel outdoor pool with visible biofilm and intermittent closure for maintenance; several colleagues developed brief gastroenteritis soon afterwards. He has not travelled internationally and has no known immunodeficiency.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen performed because of persistent symptoms shows mild, continuous small-bowel wall thickening and mesenteric fat stranding limited to the proximal jejunum, without obstruction or abscess.

Occupational exposure included handling contaminated pool filters and accidental splashes to the face during the maintenance work; two coworkers had shorter self-limited diarrhoeal illnesses after the same task, and the hotel reported temporary loss of automated filtration for several days prior to the cluster.

Stool testing returned: culture for enteric bacteria negative, viral antigen panel negative, but modified Ziehl–Neelsen stain showed 4–6 µm ovoid acid-fast oocysts; a stool immunoassay for a coccidian protozoan was positive.

explanation: The combination of prolonged watery diarrhoea after recreational/occupational exposure to inadequately filtered pool water and identification of 4–6 µm acid-fast oocysts on stool microscopy is diagnostic for infection with Cryptosporidium species. CT findings of small-bowel wall thickening can reflect enteritis. In immunocompetent hosts illness is often self-limited but can be treated with nitazoxanide when indicated and public-health measures (pool disinfection, repair of filtration) are important to prevent outbreaks.

hints.text: A 3-year-old girl is brought from daycare with a 2-week history of profuse watery diarrhoea: up to 8 stools daily, poor oral intake, and 800 g weight loss. She is listless, has dry lips and reduced skin turgor, and perianal skin irritation from frequent stools. There has been no vomiting or visible blood.

She attends a local daycare centre where three other children have had similar prolonged watery diarrhoea. She has no recent travel history and was not immunocompromised; immunisations are current. Her GP initially prescribed oral rehydration and a short course of amoxicillin for an ear infection two days before the diarrhoea worsened.

On admission serum potassium 3.1 mmol/L, HCO3− 18 mmol/L, sodium 136 mmol/L; complete blood count WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L. Stool bacterial culture and routine viral antigen testing were negative; a specimen was sent for ova and parasite examination.
Abdominal ultrasound performed to exclude intussusception was normal apart from nonobstructing bowel gas; no focal fluid collection, mass or intussusception identified.

Because of the prolonged community outbreak and dehydration she was started on oral nitazoxanide after fluid and electrolyte repletion. Her stool frequency fell by 50% within 48 hours and resolved over the following week; the laboratory later reported modified acid-fast staining of the original stool showing multiple ovoid 4–6 µm oocysts, confirming the causative coccidian protozoan.

explanation: A toddler from a daycare with protracted watery diarrhoea, negative bacterial and viral tests, and stool modified acid-fast oocysts indicates infection with Cryptosporidium species. The clinical response to nitazoxanide in an immunocompetent child with supportive care is consistent with recommended therapy, and recognition of daycare clusters prompts infection control measures to limit spread.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to primary care with three weeks of low-grade fever, profound fatigue, sore throat and night sweats. She reports progressively worsening lethargy that forced her to miss university classes. On exam she has posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and a palpable spleen edge 3 cm below the costal margin; oropharynx shows mild erythema without exudate.

She works in a day‑care centre and cares for infants and toddlers. No recent travel. She is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and reports a new romantic partner within the past two months. She received routine childhood vaccinations and has no history of infectious mononucleosis.

Full blood count: WBC 12.4 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 6.8 × 10^9/L and 18% atypical lymphocytes. Liver tests: ALT 210 U/L, AST 160 U/L, bilirubin normal. Heterophile antibody test (Monospot) performed twice is negative. Epstein–Barr virus serology shows past exposure pattern without IgM. C-reactive protein mildly elevated at 18 mg/L.
Abdominal ultrasound confirms splenomegaly with craniocaudal length 14.5 cm and no focal lesions. Chest radiograph normal.

Convalescent serology two weeks later demonstrates new IgM to a ubiquitous herpesvirus with rising IgG; quantitative PCR detects viral DNA in whole blood at 2.1 × 10^4 copies/mL. The patient’s symptoms gradually resolve over six weeks with supportive care and activity restriction.

explanation: Presentation is a mononucleosis-like syndrome with splenomegaly, heterophile-negative result, lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes, exposure to young children, and seroconversion with detectable viral DNA. These features point to Cytomegalovirus infection.

hints.text: A 54-year-old man, three months after deceased-donor renal transplantation, is admitted with fever, odynophagia and anorexia for five days. He reports difficulty swallowing solids and liquids and has lost 3 kg. On exam he is febrile 38.3°C, mildly tachycardic, with white plaques absent on oral mucosa; abdominal exam shows a well-healed transplant incision and no rebound tenderness.

Immunosuppression includes tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil and low-dose prednisone. He received standard perioperative prophylaxis but was CMV serostatus of donor and recipient was unknown at discharge. No recent travel; no known exposures to tuberculosis or endemic fungi.

Laboratory tests show WBC 2.2 × 10^9/L with neutropenia 0.9 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 98 g/L, platelets 120 × 10^9/L. Liver enzymes mildly elevated (ALT 78 U/L). Plasma quantitative PCR demonstrates viral DNA at 4.7 × 10^5 copies/mL. Blood cultures are sterile.

Upper endoscopy is performed because of odynophagia and shows several large, linear, shallow ulcers in the mid and distal oesophagus. Biopsy of the ulcer edge is obtained and sent for histopathology and immunostaining.

Intravenous antiviral therapy with ganciclovir is started empirically. Over 10 days the patient’s fever subsides, odynophagia improves, and plasma viral DNA falls to 1.8 × 10^3 copies/mL. Histopathology of the biopsy later reports enlarged cells with prominent intranuclear inclusions and positive immunostain for a herpesvirus antigen.

explanation: An immunosuppressed solid-organ transplant recipient with oesophageal ulceration, high viral load, characteristic intranuclear inclusion pathology and rapid response to ganciclovir is consistent with end-organ disease caused by Cytomegalovirus.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the travel clinic with 72 hours of high fever, severe myalgias and arthralgias, intense retro-orbital headache and a new diffuse maculopapular rash. He reports one episode of mild epistaxis this morning. On exam: T 39.4°C, pulse 110/min, blood pressure 120/78 mmHg, few petechial spots on the forearms and a positive tourniquet sign.

He returned 5 days ago from a 2-week stay in an urban area of Southeast Asia where he stayed in budget accommodation and recalled multiple daytime mosquito bites despite using topical repellent intermittently. No chronic medical problems, not taking anticoagulants; he took ibuprofen for pain in the first 48 hours.

Acute blood tests: leukocyte count 2.1 × 10^9/L, platelet count 68 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 150 g/L, haematocrit 46%. ALT 132 U/L, AST 178 U/L. C-reactive protein 8 mg/L. Prothrombin time within reference range.

Bedside abdominal ultrasound shows a diffusely thickened gallbladder wall (6 mm) and a small right-sided pleural effusion; no free intraperitoneal fluid otherwise. Chest radiograph without focal consolidation.

Local public health notices described increased reports of an Aedes-transmitted febrile illness in the neighbourhood he visited. Acute-phase serum was positive on a nonstructural protein 1 antigen assay used for diagnosis of this arboviral febrile illness; RT-PCR on the same specimen detected viral RNA consistent with a single infecting serotype.

explanation: Presentation: recent travel to an Aedes-endemic urban area, high fever with severe myalgia/retro-orbital pain, rash, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, haemoconcentration and gallbladder wall oedema indicate plasma leakage—findings typical for infection with Dengue virus. Confirmation provided by positive NS1 antigen assay and RT-PCR on acute serum.

hints.text: A 36-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks' gestation presents with 5 days of high fever, severe frontal headache, diffuse bone pain and progressive gum bleeding and epistaxis. She feels lightheaded when standing. On exam she is febrile 38.9°C, tachycardic 115/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, with widespread petechiae and a blanching erythematous rash over the trunk.

She lives in a coastal city where a cluster of febrile illnesses has been reported locally. No recent travel outside the region. Past medical history unremarkable, no prior transfusions, not on antiplatelet or anticoagulant medications. She reports taking a single dose of naproxen for pain before presentation.

Admission bloods: platelet count 30 × 10^9/L, leukocyte count 1.8 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 128 g/L, haematocrit 48% (elevated from a prior antenatal haematocrit of 36% at 20 weeks), AST 220 U/L, ALT 180 U/L. Urine dip moderately concentrated; fibrinogen mildly reduced.

Obstetric ultrasound: viable fetus with normal heart rate. Focused maternal ultrasound shows small free pelvic fluid and bilateral small pleural effusions consistent with early plasma leakage; no intra-abdominal bleeding identified.

With careful isotonic fluid resuscitation and withholding of nonsteroidal agents, her hemodynamics stabilised and bleeding diminished over 24–48 hours. Acute-phase serum later demonstrated seroconversion with new IgM to the implicated arbovirus, and RT-PCR on the admission specimen detected viral RNA consistent with the infecting serotype.

explanation: Clinical picture: febrile illness in an endemic urban area with thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, rising haematocrit, transaminitis and imaging evidence of plasma leakage during pregnancy points to severe presentation of Dengue virus infection. Diagnosis supported by rising acute-phase IgM and RT-PCR detection of viral RNA.

hints.text: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of persistent cough, right-sided pleuritic chest pain and two brief episodes of blood-tinged sputum. He is afebrile, has reduced breath sounds at the right lower lung field and mild dyspnoea on exertion.

He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes and a remote appendectomy. Three years earlier an abdominal ultrasound performed for unrelated pain documented a 3-cm asymptomatic cyst in the right hepatic lobe that was not further investigated.

Blood count reveals eosinophilia of 1.2 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 28 mg/L. Sputum microscopy is negative for acid-fast bacilli. Serologic testing shows IgG reactivity against larval cestode antigens.

He reports lifelong work on a sheep farm where household dogs are present; dogs are frequently fed uncooked offal after slaughter and he often helps in the slaughtering process and skinning of animals.

Chest radiograph shows a round, well-demarcated 7-cm opacity in the right lower zone. CT of the thorax demonstrates a thin-walled cystic lesion containing internal daughter cysts and a floating laminated membrane with an air–fluid level within the cyst.

explanation: A solitary pulmonary cyst with daughter cysts and an internal floating membrane (the 'water-lily' appearance), eosinophilia, prior hepatic cyst and high-risk exposure to dogs fed raw offal points to Echinococcus granulosus infection with pulmonary involvement. Management options include antiparasitic therapy with albendazole and definitive removal of intact cysts by surgery or carefully controlled percutaneous techniques when appropriate; care must be taken to avoid cyst rupture, which can cause anaphylaxis and secondary dissemination. Public health measures focus on preventing transmission between livestock and dogs and public education around handling of offal.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with 8 days of progressively bloody diarrhoea and crampy lower abdominal pain. She reports 10–12 liquid stools per day with mucus and occasional visible blood, low-grade fevers and tenesmus. On examination she is mildly tachycardic and has focal tenderness in the left lower quadrant without peritonism.

She returned 10 days ago from a six-week volunteer placement in rural Bangladesh. She ate local salads and drank untreated well and river water frequently. She has no chronic illnesses, has not taken antibiotics recently and is HIV-negative by rapid test performed at presentation.

Stool microscopy demonstrates motile protozoan trophozoites; faecal occult blood is positive. C-reactive protein is 56 mg/L and white blood cell count is 9.8 × 10^9/L with a slight neutrophil predominance.

Flexible sigmoidoscopy shows multiple discrete, shallow ulcers with surrounding oedema in the sigmoid colon. Biopsy from an ulcer edge contains rounded trophozoite organisms adherent to mucosa; occasional erythrophagocytosis is seen within these organisms.

She was started on a nitroimidazole agent; within 72 hours fever and diarrhoea had largely resolved. A separate oral luminal cysticidal agent was administered afterwards and stool microscopy at two weeks was negative.

explanation: The combination of recent travel to an endemic area with ingestion of untreated water, bloody diarrhoea, motile trophozoites on stool microscopy that contain ingested red cells, flask-shaped mucosal ulcers on endoscopy and rapid clinical response to a nitroimidazole followed by a luminal cysticidal agent is diagnostic of Entamoeba histolytica infection.

hints.text: A 49-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 10 days of progressive right upper-quadrant pain, fevers to 38.9 °C and poor appetite; he reports a 5-kg weight loss over three weeks. He denies diarrhoea. On exam he is febrile with RUQ tenderness and a palpable tender liver edge.

He emigrated from Guatemala two months ago. His medical history is otherwise notable only for diet-controlled diabetes; there is no history of biliary disease, abdominal surgery or indwelling catheters. He is not known to be immunosuppressed.

White blood cell count is 14.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 142 mg/L. Liver panel shows alkaline phosphatase 320 U/L and alanine aminotransferase 78 U/L. Two sets of blood cultures drawn before antibiotic therapy remain without growth at 48 hours.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identifies a solitary 8-cm hypodense lesion in the posterior right hepatic lobe with peripheral rim enhancement. Percutaneous aspiration yields thick brown, odourless material; Gram stain shows no organisms and routine bacterial cultures are sterile.

On re-questioning he recalls a self-limited episode of watery, mildly bloody diarrhoea while visiting relatives in rural Guatemala about six weeks before presentation and acknowledges frequent consumption of unboiled river water during that trip.

explanation: A solitary right-lobe liver abscess with sterile aspiration material in a patient who had recent travel to an endemic area and a preceding diarrhoeal illness points to Entamoeba histolytica. The sterile, thick brown aspirate and history of untreated water exposure are characteristic for an amebic hepatic abscess rather than pyogenic infection.

hints.text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic with a 2-week history of intense perianal itching that is most severe at night and has disrupted sleep; mother reports increasing daytime irritability and excoriations around the anus. He attends a local daycare and has otherwise normal growth and development; no fever or gastrointestinal bleeding.

Family history: his 4-year-old sister began complaining of similar perianal itch several days after him. No recent travel, no known foodborne exposures, no chronic medical conditions, and no recent antibiotic courses. Over-the-counter topical hydrocortisone gave only minimal temporary relief.

An abdominal ultrasound requested because of intermittent periumbilical discomfort showed normal bowel caliber, no free fluid, and a non-enlarged appendix; no structural explanation for the localized perianal symptoms was identified.

Mother volunteers that on two mornings she noticed thin white thread-like structures clinging to the child’s perianal skin and to his pajamas; several other children at the daycare had reported nightly rectal itch to staff over the same interval.

A morning perianal adhesive-tape specimen obtained before bathing showed numerous flattened ovoid eggs with a slightly asymmetrical shape on light microscopy; a few slender, motile adult worms were observed on the sample.

explanation: The clinical picture—nocturnal perianal pruritus in a preschool-aged child with household and daycare clustering and a positive morning adhesive-tape test demonstrating characteristic eggs and occasional adult worms—indicates infection with Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm). Management includes oral anthelmintic therapy (for example, mebendazole, albendazole, or pyrantel pamoate), simultaneous treatment of household contacts, strict hand hygiene and laundering of bedding/clothing, and a follow-up dose or repeat therapy per local guidance to interrupt reinfection.

hints.text: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the district clinic with a 2-day history of watery diarrhoea and poor oral intake. Mother notes up to 12 watery stools per day, marked thirst, and two episodes of emesis. There is no blood in the stools and no fever. On exam the child is sleepy between feeds, has sunken eyes, delayed capillary refill and a weak peripheral pulse.

The family lives in a rural village with intermittent municipal water; a day before illness several neighbours reported similar loose stools after a village festival where stored homemade rice salad was served. The child has received routine immunisations but no recent antibiotics and has no known chronic illnesses.

Abdominal radiograph obtained to exclude bowel obstruction is unremarkable; there is no air–fluid level pattern suggestive of obstruction and no dilated loops. Imaging did not identify any localized intra-abdominal process to suggest invasive enteritis.

He was treated promptly with oral rehydration solution and zinc supplementation per local guidelines; after two ORS boluses his mental status improved and stool frequency decreased. Because several household members remained symptomatic, a single oral course of azithromycin was given to the child, with further clinical improvement over the next 48 hours.

Point-of-care stool test negative for occult blood; stool culture grows a non-invasive lactose-fermenting Gram-negative bacillus. A laboratory PCR panel returned positive for a gene encoding a plasmid-borne heat-labile enterotoxin and negative for rotavirus, norovirus and common invasive bacterial pathogens.

explanation: Acute profuse watery diarrhoea in a young child after exposure to a common-source food, clinical improvement with rehydration and macrolide, and detection of plasmid-borne enterotoxin genes indicate enterotoxin-mediated diarrhoeal infection — Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents with 7 days of high fever to 39.5°C, severe sore throat, profound fatigue and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. He reports difficulty swallowing and decreased oral intake. On exam there are bilateral tonsillar exudates and a palpable spleen tip.

No chronic medical conditions and no regular medications. On day 4 of the illness he was seen in primary care and given a 5‑day course of oral amoxicillin for presumed streptococcal pharyngitis; he completed two days of that prescription prior to re-presentation.
WBC 11.6 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 6.5 × 10^9/L and 22% atypical lymphocytes. ALT 98 IU/L. Rapid streptococcal antigen test negative. Heterophile (Monospot) test performed on day 7 returned negative.
Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound demonstrates splenomegaly with a longitudinal length of 15.6 cm and no focal abnormality.

Forty-eight hours after starting amoxicillin he developed a diffuse pruritic maculopapular eruption involving trunk and limbs; the rash persisted several days after stopping the drug and improved with symptomatic skin care and antihistamine.

explanation: The combination of sore throat, lymphadenopathy, atypical lymphocytosis and splenomegaly points to infectious mononucleosis. A negative heterophile test early in illness is possible. Development of a generalized maculopapular rash after an aminopenicillin in this clinical context is a well-known reaction that strongly suggests Epstein‑Barr virus infection. Treatment is supportive; avoid contact sports while the spleen is enlarged.

hints.text: A 27-year-old woman presents to primary care with 48 hours of dysuria, urinary frequency and suprapubic discomfort. She reports one feverish episode at home (measured 38.2 °C) and mild nausea but no flank pain or vomiting. On examination she is alert, pulse 96/min, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg; suprapubic tenderness is present without costovertebral angle tenderness.

She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, no diabetes, and had a urinary tract infection last year treated successfully. She started using a diaphragm with spermicide three weeks ago after change of partner; no recent antibiotic exposure or instrumentation.

Urine dipstick: positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Urine microscopy: 42 × 10^6/L leukocytes and occasional bacteriuria. Midstream urine culture later grew >10^5 CFU/mL colonies on MacConkey agar that were lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative and indole-positive.
Point-of-care renal ultrasound obtained because of fever showed normal-sized kidneys without hydronephrosis and no perinephric collection.
She reports onset of urinary symptoms 12–24 hours after sexual intercourse with a new partner; she had used the diaphragm the previous night and notes new vaginal irritation in the same interval.

explanation: Presentation, positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase, and culture of lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative, indole-positive gram-negative bacilli are most consistent with a common uropathogen from the Enterobacterales group—specifically Escherichia coli. Risk factors (recent diaphragm/spermicide use and recent intercourse) further support an ascending urinary tract infection by this organism.

hints.text: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with 24 hours of crampy abdominal pain and the sudden onset of bloody diarrhoea. He has had four bloody stools in the past 12 hours, severe abdominal cramping, and minimal vomiting. Temperature is 37.8 °C, heart rate 110/min; he appears mildly dehydrated.
Previously well child with no chronic medical problems, no recent antibiotics, and no international travel. Immunisations up to date. No sick contacts at school.

Initial labs: white cell count 9.6 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 124 g/L, platelets 280 × 10^9/L, creatinine 38 µmol/L. Stool microscopy shows numerous fecal leukocytes. Stool culture yielded gram‑negative bacilli that grew on MacConkey agar; an enzyme immunoassay detected a protein toxin that inhibits protein synthesis in cultured Vero cells.
Abdominal ultrasound performed to exclude intussusception shows segmental thickening of the descending colon wall with preserved peristalsis and no free fluid.
Parents report the child ate a hamburger at a local fair 48 hours before symptom onset; other family members who ate the same food remained well.

explanation: Bloody diarrhoea after undercooked ground beef, stool leukocytes, and detection of a Shiga-like (verotoxin) activity in a lactose-fermenting gram‑negative bacillus are characteristic of enterohaemorrhagic strains of Escherichia coli (for example O157:H7 and related serotypes). Management focuses on supportive care and careful monitoring for haemolytic–uraemic syndrome; antibiotics are generally avoided for toxin-producing strains.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 2-week history of watery, foul-smelling diarrhoea and post-prandial bloating. She notes four to six loose, greasy stools per day, crampy periumbilical pain but no visible blood or high fever, and an unintentional 4 kg weight loss. On exam she is afebrile, mildly cachectic, with soft, non-tender abdomen and hyperactive bowel sounds.

She returned 3 weeks ago from a 3-week backpacking trip in the Himalayas. She reports multiple days of drinking water from mountain streams and using rustic hostels with shared toilets. No recent antibiotics, no chronic medical conditions, no immunosuppressive medications.

Complete blood count: haemoglobin 125 g/L, WBC 6.2 × 10^9/L. CRP 3 mg/L. Stool culture negative for enteric bacteria; stool microscopy shows no faecal leukocytes and a qualitative faecal fat test is markedly positive.
Abdominal ultrasound and non-contrast CT performed to exclude inflammatory or obstructive causes are both unremarkable, without bowel-wall thickening, lymphadenopathy, or biliary pathology.

Two other hikers who drank from the same unfiltered streams developed similar prolonged, greasy, foul-smelling diarrhoea without fever; symptoms in this patient improved substantially after a single course of nitroimidazole therapy prescribed empirically by a travel clinic physician.

explanation: Prolonged, non-bloody, foul-smelling fatty diarrhoea after drinking unfiltered freshwater while backpacking, absence of fever and faecal leukocytes, marked faecal fat, and clinical response to nitroimidazole are most consistent with an intestinal protozoan infection that causes malabsorption after freshwater exposure.

hints.text: A 3-year-old boy is brought by his mother with 10 days of bulky, pale, foul-smelling loose stools occurring several times per day and poor appetite. Parents note he has not gained weight as expected and is more irritable; there is no high fever or bloody diarrhoea. On exam he is afebrile, mildly pale, with a soft, mildly distended abdomen and otherwise normal cardiorespiratory exam.

He recently started at a new daycare 2 weeks ago; two other children at the centre have similar prolonged loose stools. He has no chronic medical problems, has not received recent antibiotics, and has normal developmental milestones for his age.

CBC: haemoglobin 115 g/L, WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L. CRP <5 mg/L. Stool bacterial culture negative; stool exam shows increased fat and no faecal leukocytes. A point-of-care stool antigen test performed at the clinic returns positive for a single intestinal protozoan antigen.
Plain abdominal radiograph obtained to evaluate persistent symptoms shows no obstruction or bowel dilatation; abdominal ultrasound is normal without intussusception or masses.

Child was treated with a 5-day course of oral nitroimidazole; diarrhoea frequency decreased markedly within 48 hours and appetite improved. Repeat stool antigen testing four weeks after treatment is negative and weight trajectory has normalized.

explanation: A young child in daycare with prolonged, greasy, foul-smelling, non-bloody diarrhoea, absence of inflammatory markers, positive stool antigen for a protozoan, and rapid clinical and microbiologic response to nitroimidazole therapy point to an enteric protozoal infection acquired by faecal–oral transmission at daycare.

hints.text: A 68-year-old man with COPD presents with 3 days of fever, pleuritic right‑sided chest pain, and increasing productive cough with green sputum. He had an influenza‑like illness 10 days earlier that briefly improved then worsened. He is an active smoker and uses an inhaled corticosteroid.

No known immunodeficiency. Lives with adult family; his toddler granddaughter had purulent conjunctivitis and an ear infection two weeks ago. He received seasonal influenza vaccine last season; no recent travel or hospitalisation.

On arrival: temperature 38.6°C, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 91% on room air. Blood tests: WBC 15.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 12.6 × 10^9/L, CRP 120 mg/L. Sputum Gram stain shows many neutrophils and small pleomorphic Gram‑negative coccobacilli; blood cultures pending.
Chest radiograph demonstrates a dense lobar consolidation of the right lower lobe with air bronchograms and no pleural effusion.

Empiric intravenous ceftriaxone plus oral azithromycin was given; he defervesced and oxygen requirement fell within 48 hours. Sputum culture later grew a fastidious small coccobacillus on chocolate agar but not on plain blood agar unless plated adjacent to Staphylococcus aureus, supporting identification of a mucosal respiratory Gram‑negative coccobacillus.

explanation: Post‑influenza secondary bacterial pneumonia in an older smoker with COPD, sputum Gram stain showing small pleomorphic Gram‑negative coccobacilli, and the organism’s growth only on chocolate agar or via the satellite phenomenon indicate Haemophilus influenzae as the pathogen.

hints.text: A 28-year-old male software engineer presents with 6 months of intermittent epigastric burning pain, worse at night and often relieved by meals. Last week he noticed dark, tarry stools and one episode of lightheadedness on standing. He takes occasional over-the-counter aspirin for tension headaches and drinks coffee daily.

He was born and raised in a crowded household in Southeast Asia and emigrated at age 12. No chronic medical conditions, no regular nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug prescriptions, no prior endoscopies, and no recent courses of antibiotics. No alcohol excess and no known liver disease.
Haemoglobin 104 g/L, MCV 74 fL, white cell count 6.4 × 10^9/L, ferritin 8 µg/L. Two separate stool guaiac tests were positive. Serum C-reactive protein 2 mg/L.

Esophagogastroduodenoscopy demonstrates a single 15 mm anterior duodenal ulcer with a clean base and surrounding mucosal erythema; multiple gastric and duodenal biopsy specimens were obtained at the procedure.

A rapid urease test on a gastric biopsy turned positive within minutes. He was treated with a proton-pump inhibitor plus two antibiotics for 14 days; at 8-week follow-up his epigastric pain had resolved and a urea breath test was negative.

explanation: Clinical picture of chronic dyspepsia with a duodenal ulcer, positive rapid urease test on gastric biopsy, and symptom resolution with PPI-based eradication therapy is diagnostic of infection with Helicobacter pylori.

hints.text: A 22-year-old university student presents to urgent care with 4 days of fever to 38.9°C, severe throat pain and difficulty swallowing. He has drooling and has eaten very little; on inspection there are numerous painful multiple small ulcers on the anterior gingiva and buccal mucosa and erythematous, swollen lips. He has tender bilateral submandibular lymphadenopathy and appears mildly dehydrated.

No chronic illnesses and not on medications. He reports no prior episodes like this. Two weeks earlier he kissed a new partner who had a crusted vesicle at the lip margin; he recalls the partner’s lesion appeared shortly before they met. No recent travel, no sexual symptoms elsewhere.

Full blood count: white cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L with a normal neutrophil predominance; C-reactive protein 24 mg/L. A scraping from an oral lesion stained on-site showed epithelial cells with multinucleation and nuclear changes consistent with a viral cytopathic effect.

Close oral-photograph by the clinician demonstrates multiple small, shallow ulcers clustered on the attached gingiva and inner lower lip mucosa with surrounding erythema and a yellowish fibrinous base; no single large necrotic lesion.

Empiric oral antiviral therapy was started; within 48 hours his fever resolved, oral pain decreased and ulcers began to crust. A PCR assay of a lesion swab later returned positive for an enveloped, linear double-stranded DNA virus that produced the multinucleated epithelial cells seen on scraping.

explanation: Presentation and timeline indicate a primary mucocutaneous infection acquired from recent direct contact, with painful anterior oral ulcers, cervical adenopathy, viral cytopathic effect on smear, and rapid response to an antiherpetic agent. These features are most consistent with infection by Herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1).

hints.text: A 45-year-old woman presents with 3 days of progressive left eye pain, photophobia, tearing and blurred vision. She notes foreign-body sensation and worsening pain with bright light. Visual acuity in the affected eye has fallen from baseline; there is unilateral ciliary injection but no systemic fever.

She is otherwise healthy, uses soft contact lenses intermittently and admits to wearing them overnight the night before symptom onset. She also reports recurrent painful vesicular lesions at the left lip margin during prior febrile illnesses, last episode several months ago. No diabetes or immunosuppression.

Slit-lamp examination with fluorescein staining reveals a branching, linear epithelial defect on the corneal surface with terminal bulb-like endings; there is minimal stromal infiltration and no hypopyon.
Corneal scrapings were obtained. Light microscopy showed multinucleated epithelial cells; bacterial cultures remained sterile. PCR of the corneal swab detected an enveloped double-stranded DNA virus.

Topical antiviral therapy was initiated promptly; within 48–72 hours pain and photophobia improved and the epithelial defect began to epithelialize. No response was seen to initial empirical antibacterial drops prior to antiviral initiation.

explanation: Unilateral dendritic corneal ulcer on fluorescein staining, prior mucocutaneous vesicles at the lip margin, multinucleated epithelial cells on scrapings, PCR detection of an enveloped dsDNA virus, and rapid response to topical antiviral therapy identify Herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1) as the most likely pathogen causing herpetic keratitis.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency clinic with 72 hours of severe vulvar pain, burning dysuria and a fever of 38.2 °C. Examination shows multiple small, clustered clear vesicles on an erythematous base over the labia majora and introitus with bilateral tender inguinal lymphadenopathy; she is unable to sit comfortably because of the pain.

She reports this is the first time she has had genital lesions. No chronic medical conditions, not pregnant, no routine medications, and HIV test two years ago was negative. She describes a systemic prodrome (malaise, low-grade fever) that began 24 hours before the skin lesions appeared.

Complete blood count shows leukocytes 12.4 × 10^9/L. Urinalysis negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Microscopy from a lesion scrape demonstrates multinucleated epidermal giant cells; nucleic-acid amplification from a vesicle swab detects viral DNA of an enveloped double-stranded DNA pathogen that preferentially infects mucocutaneous epithelium.

Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound performed to exclude a deeper pelvic process shows normal adnexa and no fluid collections; inflammatory change is limited to superficial vulvar soft tissues on clinical correlation.

On further questioning she reports a new male sexual partner 12 days before symptom onset; he later told her he had a brief episode of painful penile blisters around that time which he described as resolving after a few days.

explanation: Clinical picture—primary presentation in a young adult with painful clustered genital vesicles, tender regional adenopathy, multinucleated giant cells on lesion smear, and NAAT detection of an enveloped dsDNA mucocutaneous virus—plus recent new sexual contact strongly supports infection with Herpes simplex virus type 2. Imaging excludes deep suppurative disease; management is antiviral therapy and symptomatic care.

hints.text: A 36-year-old man presents to the sexual health clinic with a 48-hour history of painful grouped vesicles on the dorsal glans and proximal shaft. He reports a tingling prodrome 12–24 hours prior to lesion appearance and a tender ipsilateral inguinal lymph node.

Patient is otherwise healthy, HIV-negative, and reports similar outbreaks twice in the past 12 months that resolved over about a week. Episodes are now occurring more frequently and are preceded by the same local paresthesia.

White cell count 7.8 × 10^9/L. Swab of an intact vesicle sent for nucleic-acid amplification returns positive for viral DNA from an enveloped double-stranded DNA virus commonly recovered from genital mucosa; type-specific serology demonstrates IgG against the genital-associated glycoprotein antigen, consistent with prior exposure.
Targeted ultrasound of the penis to exclude a subcutaneous collection shows only superficial mucocutaneous inflammation without abscess or vascular abnormality.

He is started on oral acyclovir and reports marked reduction in pain and cessation of new vesicle formation within 48 hours; lesions crust and begin to heal over the next 5 days with no bacterial superinfection.

explanation: Recurrent, painful grouped genital vesicles with a prodrome of tingling, positive lesion NAAT for an enveloped dsDNA mucocutaneous virus, type-specific IgG to the genital glycoprotein, and rapid clinical response to a nucleoside analogue are consistent with recurrent infection due to Herpes simplex virus type 2.

hints.text: A 24-year-old previously healthy man presents to urgent care with 10 days of fevers, sore throat, diffuse non‑pruritic maculopapular rash on trunk and proximal limbs, myalgias and tender posterior cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Temperature 38.8°C and pulse 110/min; he has several small painless oral ulcers but no respiratory distress.

No chronic medical conditions and no regular medications. He had routine STI screening 3 months ago that was negative. He reports a single new sexual partner about 4 weeks before presentation. Denies recent travel, blood transfusion or injection drug use.

CBC: leukocytes 3.2 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.6 × 10^9/L. ALT 98 U/L. Heterophile antibody (monospot) negative. A combination antigen–antibody screening assay returned reactive; plasma viral RNA measured by nucleic acid amplification was 5.2 × 10^7 copies/L.
Neck ultrasound demonstrated multiple enlarged cervical lymph nodes (largest 2.2 cm) with preserved fatty hilum and increased vascularity. Chest radiograph was normal.

On further questioning he acknowledges unprotected receptive anal intercourse with the new partner three weeks before symptom onset; that partner later developed a febrile illness and tested positive on the same combination antigen–antibody screening assay.

explanation: Presentation and timing fit acute retroviral syndrome. A reactive combination antigen–antibody screen together with a very high plasma viral RNA concentration in the setting of recent high‑risk exposure is diagnostic of infection with human immunodeficiency virus type 1 (HIV‑1). The transitory lymphopenia, elevated ALT and generalized lymphadenopathy are typical in early infection.

hints.text: A 42-year-old woman is admitted with 3 months of progressive weight loss (≈8 kg), night sweats, nonproductive cough and increasing exertional dyspnoea. She has painful white pseudomembranous plaques on the buccal mucosa and multiple violaceous papules on the trunk. Temp 37.8°C, respiratory rate 22/min and SpO2 94% on room air.

History notable for type 2 diabetes on metformin and a 10 pack‑year smoking history. She reports multiple sexual partners over the past decade and has not had regular primary care for several years. No recent travel or blood product exposure reported.

Hemoglobin 95 g/L, leukocytes 4.1 × 10^9/L. CD4 lymphocyte count 85 × 10^6/L. C‑reactive protein 58 mg/L. A combination antigen–antibody screening assay was reactive and plasma viral RNA by nucleic acid amplification was 6.5 × 10^7 copies/L.
High‑resolution CT chest showed diffuse bilateral ground‑glass opacities with perihilar predominance and several small cavitating nodules in the upper lobes.
