She was started on empiric trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole for suspected Pneumocystis pneumonia and, after counselling, commenced combination antiretroviral therapy. At 3 weeks her oral candidiasis had improved and plasma viral RNA fell to 3.9 × 10^4 copies/L with subjective improvement in appetite and energy.

explanation: Chronic constitutional symptoms, oral thrush, violaceous cutaneous lesions and low CD4 count indicate advanced immunodeficiency. A reactive combination antigen–antibody screen with a high plasma viral RNA load and the rapid virologic response to combination antiretroviral therapy confirm infection by human immunodeficiency virus type 1 (HIV‑1); the pulmonary findings reflect opportunistic infection(s) seen with advanced disease.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to a primary-care clinic with 3 months of intermittent postcoital spotting and an increase in malodorous vaginal discharge. She is otherwise well; pelvic examination reveals multiple small, friable, exophytic papular lesions at the cervical os and mild cervical ectropion.

She reports a new sexual partner 6 months ago, inconsistent condom use, smokes 5–10 cigarettes per day, and has never received the recommended adolescent viral vaccination. No history of immunosuppression or prior abnormal cervical cytology.

Liquid-based cervical cytology shows high-grade squamous epithelial abnormalities and numerous squamous cells with perinuclear clearing and irregular nuclear contours. A nucleic acid amplification test on the cervical swab detects high-risk viral DNA sequences (type-specific assay reported as positive for high-risk groups).

Colposcopic inspection demonstrates acetowhite change at the transformation zone with a mosaic vascular pattern and small punctation areas; directed biopsy was taken from the most abnormal area for histology.

On further questioning she acknowledges that her new partner has persistent, flesh–colored papular lesions on the external genitalia and declined evaluation; she has had unprotected intercourse with him since their relationship began.

explanation: Clinical presentation of exophytic cervical lesions, HSIL on cytology with koilocytic change, colposcopic acetowhite transformation, and recent unprotected sexual exposure — together point to infection with Human papillomavirus, including oncogenic high‑risk types implicated in cervical dysplasia.

hints.text: A 24-year-old kindergarten teacher presents to urgent care in midwinter with abrupt onset of high fever, severe myalgias and headache that began 12 hours earlier; she reports dry cough and marked malaise and is unable to work. On exam T 39.4 °C, pulse 110/min, RR 18/min; pharynx mildly erythematous and lungs clear to auscultation without focal crackles.

She is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, does not smoke, and has no recent travel. She reports she did not receive this season’s respiratory virus immunisation. Symptoms progressed rapidly from feeling well to bedridden over half a day. No prior antibiotic treatment.
A portable chest radiograph obtained in clinic shows clear lung fields without lobar consolidation or effusion.

Because of the rapid onset and severity, oral antiviral therapy was started within 36 hours of symptom onset. Within 24 hours of treatment the patient’s fever and myalgias abated and she reported marked improvement in cough and energy level.

A nasopharyngeal swab tested by reverse-transcription PCR returned positive for a single-stranded, negative-sense, segmented RNA respiratory virus that bears haemagglutinin and neuraminidase surface glycoproteins; laboratory subtyping reported the strain to be the type responsible for seasonal human epidemics and known to undergo antigenic shift.

explanation: Clinical picture — abrupt high fever, prominent myalgias, dry cough during seasonal circulation, quick response to neuraminidase-inhibitor therapy — plus RT‑PCR identification of a segmented negative‑sense RNA virus with haemagglutinin and neuraminidase surface proteins indicates Influenza A virus.

hints.text: An 78-year-old woman from a long-term residential facility is brought in with 36 hours of fever, rigors, lower abdominal discomfort and new confusion. She has a Foley catheter in place after recent hip surgery; physical exam shows suprapubic tenderness and blood pressure 95/60 mmHg.

Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and limited mobility after a fractured femur. The indwelling urinary catheter was placed three weeks earlier for urinary retention; she has had a urinary tract infection treated as an outpatient 10 months ago.
Non-contrast CT of the pelvis and lower abdomen demonstrates gas within the bladder wall and lumen consistent with emphysematous cystitis; no obstructing ureteric stone is seen.

Intravenous fluids and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics were started and the Foley catheter was exchanged. Her blood pressure and mental status partially improved over 24 hours, but fever and marked pyuria persisted, prompting urine culture and susceptibility testing.

Urine microscopy: many leucocytes and numerous gram-negative bacilli. Urine culture grows mucoid colonies that ferment lactose (pink on MacConkey), are oxidase negative and non-motile; the isolate reduces nitrate and demonstrates intrinsic resistance to ampicillin.

explanation: Emphysematous cystitis in an older diabetic with an indwelling catheter, together with a mucoid, lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative, non-motile gram-negative rod (pink colonies on MacConkey, ampicillin resistance), indicates Klebsiella pneumoniae as the causative organism.

hints.text: A 68-year-old man presented to the emergency department with 72 hours of fever (peak 39.6°C), productive cough, progressive dyspnoea and two watery bowel motions. He is a current 40-pack-year smoker; on arrival he was confused, tachypnoeic with respiratory rate 28/min and oxygen saturation 88% on room air.

Past medical history notable for COPD treated with inhaled bronchodilators and low-dose oral prednisone for the past month after an acute exacerbation; type 2 diabetes controlled with metformin. No recent antibiotics prior to presentation.

Serum sodium 124 mmol/L, leukocyte count 11.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, CRP 265 mg/L, ALT 78 U/L. Sputum Gram stain showed few neutrophils and no predominant organisms; blood cultures pending.
Chest radiograph demonstrated dense patchy consolidation of the right lower lobe. Chest CT confirmed lobar consolidation with a small right pleural effusion and no cavitation.

He returned three days before symptom onset from a city conference where he stayed at a downtown hotel and used the indoor spa. Hotel management later contacted guests reporting several other attendees with febrile respiratory illness. A urine antigen test returned positive for a single respiratory pathogen; culture of respiratory secretions later grew a fastidious gram-negative bacillus on buffered charcoal yeast extract medium.

explanation: The combination of severe community-acquired pneumonia with diarrhoea and confusion, marked hyponatraemia, exposure to a hotel spa system with other affected guests, positive urine antigen and growth on buffered charcoal yeast extract medium is most consistent with Legionella pneumophila.

hints.text: A 55-year-old woman presented with 4 days of high fevers (up to 40°C), dry cough, myalgia, and several watery bowel movements; family noted new confusion at home. She was tachycardic and hypoxaemic with oxygen saturation 90% on room air and required admission for oxygen therapy.

She had returned 6 days earlier from a 7-day Mediterranean cruise during which she frequently used the ship’s indoor whirlpool and swimming pool; several other passengers reported respiratory illness during the voyage and the vessel reported mid-voyage maintenance on potable water tanks.

Serum sodium 121 mmol/L, leukocyte count 13.0 × 10^9/L, CRP 310 mg/L, AST 92 U/L, ALT 104 U/L. Routine sputum culture was non-diagnostic; urine antigen test was positive for a single respiratory pathogen.
Chest x-ray revealed dense consolidation of the right middle lobe with a small pleural effusion; CT chest confirmed lobar consolidation without cavitation.

She received empirical intravenous amoxicillin/clavulanate on admission but remained febrile and more hypoxaemic at 48 hours. After switching to intravenous levofloxacin she defervesced over the next 24–48 hours and oxygen requirement fell; urine antigen later confirmed the same pathogen.

explanation: Severe pneumonia after a cruise with exposure to warm-water facilities, prominent GI symptoms, hyponatraemia, a positive urine antigen and rapid clinical response to a fluoroquinolone are typical for Legionella pneumophila infection.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-week history of a single enlarging ulcer on his left forearm. The lesion began as a small papule after an insect bite while he was backpacking; it is now roughly 2.5 cm in diameter with a raised indurated border and central granulation tissue. He is afebrile, otherwise well, and topical antibiotic courses prescribed abroad produced no improvement.

He returned 3 weeks ago from a 10-week trip to rural northern Brazil, where he camped in forested areas and slept in hammocks. He has no chronic medical conditions, is not on immunosuppressants, and reports multiple nocturnal bites that acquaintance locals described as 'tiny, itchy' insects. No bleeding disorders, no prior similar lesions.

High-frequency ultrasound of the lesion shows focal dermal and subcutaneous hypoechoic inflammatory thickening extending to but not involving the deep fascia; there is increased vascularity on colour Doppler but no discrete abscess cavity or underlying osteomyelitis.

On direct questioning he emphasizes that bites occurred at night while sleeping without bed netting; a fellow traveller developed multiple papules that resolved slowly. Local health posts in the villages he visited frequently manage chronic skin ulcers after river-side camping.

A punch biopsy touch-prep stained with Giemsa shows numerous intracellular round bodies within macrophages; each body has a distinct rod-shaped kinetoplast adjacent to a nucleus. Culture incubated at ambient laboratory temperature yielded flagellated promastigote forms after several days.

explanation: Cutaneous leishmaniasis. The chronic non-healing papule-to-ulcer after nocturnal sandfly exposure, compatible lesion imaging, and demonstration of intracellular amastigotes with a kinetoplast (and promastigote growth at lower temperature) confirm infection with Leishmania species.

hints.text: A 72‑year‑old woman presents with 48 hours of progressive headache, fever to 38.4°C, confusion and new right‑sided facial weakness; she has lethargy, vomiting and nuchal rigidity on examination and a Glasgow Coma Scale of 13.

Medical history notable for seropositive rheumatoid arthritis treated with oral prednisone 10 mg daily for the past 6 months. She lives independently, has no recent travel, and had no preceding significant respiratory or urinary symptoms.

Blood: WBC 14.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 95 mg/L. Lumbar puncture: CSF WBC 1.5 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, protein 2.8 g/L, glucose 1.8 mmol/L (concurrent plasma glucose 6.3 mmol/L). Initial CSF Gram stain shows small Gram‑positive rod forms.

MRI brain with contrast demonstrates T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and patchy enhancement in the pontine and medullary tegmentum with relative sparing of the cerebellar hemispheres, findings most in keeping with brainstem encephalitis.

She was started on empiric ceftriaxone plus vancomycin but showed no clinical improvement after 48 hours; blood and CSF cultures subsequently grew small Gram‑positive bacilli and high‑dose intravenous ampicillin was added, after which the patient defervesced and cognitive function began to recover over the next 72 hours.

explanation: An older, mildly immunocompromised patient with meningitis/brainstem encephalitis, small Gram‑positive rods on CSF, lack of response to third‑generation cephalosporin empiric therapy and rapid clinical improvement after addition of ampicillin points to Listeria monocytogenes. The organism frequently causes meningitis and rhombencephalitis in older or immunosuppressed adults and is not reliably covered by cephalosporins; targeted therapy with ampicillin is indicated.

hints.text: A 4-year-old previously well boy presents to the emergency department with 72 hours of high fever (max 40.0 °C), cough, coryza and red, tearing eyes. His parents report noticing tiny white spots on the buccal mucosa this morning; a confluent erythematous maculopapular rash began at the hairline and face and is spreading downward. He attends a community daycare and looks uncomfortable but not toxic.

Family declines routine childhood vaccinations; the child received no scheduled live childhood immunisations. Several classmates developed febrile rash illness earlier in the week. No recent travel, no chronic conditions, and no hospitalisations.

Chest radiograph shows perihilar peribronchial cuffing with patchy air‑space opacities in the right middle lobe and perihilar regions, appearances most consistent with viral bronchiolitis/early viral pneumonia.

Supportive care including antipyretics, oral rehydration and two doses of oral vitamin A were given. Over 48 hours the fever and conjunctivitis improved and the rash evolved in a cephalocaudal pattern, beginning to desquamate as lesions faded.

Nasopharyngeal swab PCR detected an enveloped, single‑stranded, negative‑sense RNA virus. Paired sera demonstrated seroconversion with virus‑specific IgM present in convalescent sample, confirming recent infection.

explanation: The combination of prodromal cough, coryza and conjunctivitis; buccal white spots; a feverish maculopapular rash that begins at the hairline and spreads downward; lack of childhood live vaccination; and detection of an enveloped, negative‑sense single‑stranded RNA virus with seroconversion identifies measles virus (rubeola). The chest radiograph and clinical course reflect the well‑recognized risk of viral pneumonitis; vitamin A and supportive care were given per paediatric guidance.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman at 20 weeks' gestation presents with 4 days of fever to 38.8 °C, sore throat, and a severe dry cough. Over the prior 48 hours she developed red eyes and a confluent erythematous maculopapular rash that began at the forehead and behind the ears and is spreading to the trunk. On examination small whitish flecks are visible on the buccal mucosa.

She emigrated from Eastern Europe six months prior and cannot locate childhood immunisation records; she does not recall receiving two doses of a childhood combined live viral vaccine. Her pregnancy until now has been uncomplicated.

She works as a clinician in a community refugee health clinic. Ten days before symptom onset she assessed a febrile toddler with a generalized rash who was later admitted to hospital with progressive illness.

Because of increasing cough and breathlessness, a CT chest was performed and shows multifocal ground‑glass opacities and bronchovascular thickening without lobar consolidation, appearances compatible with viral pneumonitis.

Throat swab PCR identified an enveloped, single‑stranded, negative‑sense RNA virus. Acute serum was negative but convalescent serum 10 days later showed high‑titre virus‑specific IgM, consistent with recent primary infection.

explanation: Her prodrome of cough, coryza and conjunctivitis followed by buccal mucosal spots and a cephalocaudal maculopapular rash, recent exposure in a clinic, lack of documented childhood live vaccine, and PCR plus seroconversion confirm measles virus infection. In pregnancy measles infection carries maternal and fetal risks; the CT findings reflect viral pneumonitis, a recognised complication.

hints.text: A 21-year-old male university student presents with 48 hours of progressive painful swelling in the left preauricular region, fever to 38.9 °C, trismus and difficulty chewing. On examination there is a firm, tender swelling over the angle of the mandible extending to the submandibular area; Stensen’s duct appears nonpurulent.

He returned 5 days ago from an inter‑university rowing regatta. He lives in a crowded dormitory with five roommates. Childhood vaccination records are unavailable and he does not recall receiving an adolescent booster. He denies recent dental work or trauma.
Complete blood count: white cell count 7.2 × 10^9/L. CRP 36 mg/L. Serum amylase is elevated at 880 U/L (reference 25–125 U/L). Throat swab sent for routine bacterial culture; blood cultures obtained.
Ultrasound of the left parotid gland shows enlargement with heterogeneous echotexture and increased Doppler flow; there is no intraglandular stone and no abscess cavity identified.

Campus health notified the ward that several students who attended the same regatta developed febrile parotid swelling over the past 2 weeks; the cluster began with a single student who had acute salivary‑gland swelling followed days later by similar cases among close contacts.

explanation: Presentation and outbreak pattern are most consistent with infection by the mumps virus (a paramyxovirus). The combination of unilateral parotid swelling, markedly elevated serum amylase, supportive ultrasound findings and a dormitory/regatta exposure cluster is characteristic. Diagnosis is confirmed by virus‑specific IgM or PCR; management is supportive and prevention relies on vaccination (MMR).

hints.text: A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of slowly progressive numbness of the right hand and intermittent painless ulcers on the plantar surface of his left foot. Examination shows two well-demarcated hypopigmented macules on the dorsal right hand with loss of light touch and pinprick, weakness of right finger abduction, and a visibly thickened, tender right ulnar nerve palpable at the elbow.

He emigrated from a rural district in eastern India 18 months ago. He has no diabetes, is HIV-negative by prior testing, takes no immunosuppressive medications, and reports that the sensory symptoms began insidiously after he finished agricultural work; symptoms have progressed despite topical emollients. He recalls his mother having long-standing skin plaques when he was a child.

Skin biopsy of a hypopigmented patch shows a granulomatous infiltrate on haematoxylin–eosin stain; Fite-Faraco stain demonstrates numerous acid-fast bacilli within cutaneous nerves. Slit-skin smear returns a bacteriologic index reported as +3. Full blood count is unremarkable (WBC 6.4 × 10^9/L), CRP 2 mg/L.

High-resolution ultrasound of the right ulnar nerve demonstrates focal enlargement with hypoechoic change and increased intraneural blood flow consistent with active neuritis; chest radiograph is normal.

He spent his first 30 years in a district where the condition remains endemic and lived in the same household as his mother, who had untreated chronic nodular skin disease for decades; he reports prolonged, close household contact during childhood and early adulthood.

explanation: The combination of hypopigmented anaesthetic skin patches, thickened peripheral nerve with neuropathy, positive Fite-Faraco stain showing abundant acid-fast bacilli in cutaneous nerves, and prolonged household contact in an endemic region is diagnostic of infection by Mycobacterium leprae.

hints.text: A 34-year-old male presents to an urban clinic with a 3-month history of productive cough, drenching night sweats and progressive weight loss of 6 kg. He reports intermittent streaks of blood in sputum over the last 2 weeks and low-grade fevers. On exam he is thin, afebrile, with dullness and bronchial breath sounds at the right upper chest.

He emigrated from South Asia 18 months ago and works at a large homeless shelter. Past medical history notable for poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (most recent HbA1c 8.9%). No known HIV risk factors; rapid HIV test in clinic was negative.

Posterior–anterior chest radiograph shows a cavitary consolidation in the right upper lobe with adjacent patchy nodular opacities and volume loss. No acute cardiopulmonary oedema; small calcified granuloma is noted in the left hilum.

Colleagues at the shelter report a recent outbreak of chronic cough among clients; one resident had been diagnosed with a smear-positive pulmonary infection three months earlier but left the facility before treatment completion.

Three sputum samples: Ziehl–Neelsen stain shows numerous acid-fast bacilli; culture on egg-based medium produced rough, buff colonies after approximately 3 weeks. A nucleic acid amplification assay targeting a species-specific genomic region returned positive; phenotypic susceptibility testing showed susceptibility to isoniazid and rifampicin.

explanation: Clinical syndrome of chronic cough, weight loss, night sweats, haemoptysis and an upper-lobe cavitary lesion combined with AFB-positive sputum, slow growth on Löwenstein–Jensen (egg-based) medium and a positive species-specific NAAT indicate Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex infection. The work and shelter exposure and diabetes increase risk and help explain progression to active pulmonary disease.

hints.text: A 52-year-old woman reports 4 months of progressively worsening lower back pain radiating to the left flank, night sweats and a 7-kg weight loss. Over the past 3 weeks she developed bilateral leg weakness and difficulty walking. On examination she has focal midline tenderness at the thoracolumbar junction and reduced hip flexion strength.

She has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (most recent HbA1c 9.6%), lives in a multi-generational household and recalls prolonged household exposure years earlier to a relative treated for pulmonary infection. No history of intravenous drug use; no recent surgery.

MRI of the spine demonstrates collapse of the L1 vertebral body with destruction of adjacent endplates, a large anterior paravertebral fluid collection tracking along the psoas muscle and an epidural collection causing spinal canal stenosis and cord compression.

Family members describe that an elderly household contact had a chronic cough and weight loss 8–10 years ago and completed a course of therapy; the patient herself has spent most of her adult life in a region with high prevalence of pulmonary mycobacterial disease.

CT-guided core biopsy of the vertebral lesion showed necrotising granulomatous inflammation; Ziehl–Neelsen stain identified acid-fast bacilli. Culture from the biopsy yielded slow-growing, non-pigmented colonies on egg-based medium after 3–6 weeks; a molecular assay targeting an organism-specific genetic element was positive and baseline susceptibility testing showed sensitivity to first-line agents.

explanation: The combination of chronic focal vertebral osteomyelitis with paravertebral abscess, necrotising granulomas on biopsy and AFB-positive, slow-growing cultures that are confirmed by molecular assay is diagnostic of infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex. Spinal involvement with vertebral collapse and psoas extension (Pott disease) is a well-recognised form of extrapulmonary manifestation, especially in patients with diabetes and prior household exposure.

hints.text: A 19-year-old woman who lives in a university dormitory presents with a 10-day history of gradually worsening dry cough, sore throat, headache and malaise. She reports low‑grade fevers to 38.2°C and intermittent chest discomfort but no dyspnoea at rest. On examination she is alert, T 38.0°C, SpO2 97% on room air; pharynx is erythematous and there are scattered fine inspiratory crackles at the right mid‑lung.

She shares a room with two classmates who developed coughs approximately two weeks earlier. She is otherwise healthy, a non‑smoker, received seasonal influenza vaccine, and was given oral amoxicillin by her general practitioner three days ago with no improvement in symptoms.
Frontal chest radiograph demonstrates patchy, bilateral perihilar and peribronchial interstitial opacities more on the right; there is no dense lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

Peripheral blood: WBC 8.1 × 10^9/L with relative lymphocytosis; CRP 20 mg/L. Cold agglutinin titre 1:128. Enzyme immunoassay returned elevated specific IgM directed against an atypical respiratory pathogen.

Amoxicillin was stopped and oral azithromycin started; fever abated and the cough markedly improved within 48 hours. Clinical follow‑up showed progressive resolution of symptoms and radiographic clearing over the next few weeks.

explanation: Young adult with an outbreak exposure, protracted dry cough, interstitial chest radiograph, elevated cold agglutinins and pathogen‑specific IgM, plus rapid clinical response to a macrolide—most consistent with Mycoplasma pneumoniae.

hints.text: A 28-year-old male recruit from a military training camp presents with a 12-day history of persistent dry cough and progressive fatigue, now noting dark urine and yellowing of the eyes. Temperatures up to 38.5°C; he denies chest pain or significant shortness of breath. On examination he is jaundiced, pulse 108/min, lungs with scattered wheeze and a few crackles, SpO2 96% on room air.

He lives in crowded barracks; multiple platoon members have had prolonged coughs over the past three weeks. No prior medical conditions, not on medications, no recent travel, and no known sick contacts outside the camp.

Haemoglobin 88 g/L with elevated reticulocyte count; LDH 520 U/L, total bilirubin 62 μmol/L with indirect predominance, and low haptoglobin. Direct antiglobulin test positive for complement only; cold agglutinin titre >1:256.
Chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy peribronchial and perihilar reticulonodular infiltrates, greater on the left, without focal lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

He was started on oral doxycycline and measures to keep warm; over several days the urine colour and jaundice improved and haemoglobin stabilised while the cough abated. Clinicians noted the combination of atypical pneumonia, high cold agglutinins and a barracks outbreak when formulating their working diagnosis.

explanation: Atypical community outbreak in young adults with interstitial chest radiograph and extrathoracic cold agglutinin haemolytic anaemia that improves with tetracycline/macrolide therapy is characteristic of Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 48 hours of rapidly worsening frontal headache, fever to 39.5°C, photophobia and neck stiffness. Friends report progressive confusion and one episode of vomiting; on arrival he is febrile, tachycardic, GCS 13, and has marked nuchal rigidity with a subtle left upper‑limb pronator drift.

Previously healthy, no chronic illness, not on immunosuppressive medication, up to date with routine vaccinations. Symptoms began as malaise 3 days earlier and progressed quickly; no recent sinus or neurosurgical procedures and no known sick contacts.

Blood: leukocytes 16.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 120 mg/L, serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 330 mm H2O; CSF WBC 2.3 × 10^9/L with 92% neutrophils, glucose 1.3 mmol/L (paired serum 5.6 mmol/L), protein 3.0 g/L. CSF Gram stain shows no organisms; routine bacterial cultures pending.

MRI brain performed the day after admission shows diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement and cortical swelling, greatest in the frontal lobes, with abnormal T2 signal tracking along the cribriform region and olfactory bulbs.

On re-questioning he reports swimming and repeatedly diving under the surface of a warm inland lake on the weekend before symptom onset. A concentrated CSF wet mount examination demonstrates rapidly motile, uninucleate trophozoite forms; CSF bacterial cultures remain sterile after 48 hours.

explanation: Diagnosis: primary fulminant meningoencephalitis caused by Naegleria fowleri. The rapid onset of meningeal signs and altered mental status after warm freshwater nasal exposure, neutrophil‑predominant CSF with low glucose and high protein, and demonstration of motile trophozoites on CSF wet mount establish the diagnosis. Management requires prompt high‑dose intravenous amphotericin B and other adjunctive measures to control intracranial pressure and support organ function; outcome is often poor despite therapy, so early recognition and cerebrospinal fluid examination for motile trophozoites are critical.

hints.text: A 56-year-old woman presents with 72 hours of severe bifrontal headache, fever to 38.9°C, nausea and two brief episodes of confusion; family witnessed a single generalized tonic–clonic seizure at home. On exam she is drowsy, oriented only to person, has neck stiffness and early papilledema.

Medical history notable for well‑controlled type 2 diabetes (metformin only) and chronic allergic rhinitis managed with daily nasal saline rinses for years. She has no known immunosuppression and no recent dental or ENT procedures. Empiric intravenous ceftriaxone and vancomycin were started in the emergency department for presumed bacterial meningitis.

Blood: leukocytes 12.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 90 mg/L, serum glucose 6.7 mmol/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 280 mm H2O; CSF WBC 3.0 × 10^9/L with 85% neutrophils, glucose 0.9 mmol/L (paired serum 6.7 mmol/L), protein 4.5 g/L. CSF Gram stain negative; bacterial PCR and viral PCR panels pending.

After 24 hours of empiric antibacterial therapy the patient’s level of consciousness declined, requiring intubation for airway protection. Given concern for a non‑bacterial etiology, clinicians added intravenous amphotericin B and an oral azole; intracranial pressure rose despite hyperosmolar therapy and she showed only transient improvement in fever over the next 48 hours.

Family reports she rinsed her nasal passages with unboiled well water six days before symptom onset. Repeat CSF microscopy using a fresh, centrifuged wet mount now shows rapidly motile, uninucleate trophozoites; bacterial and viral studies remain negative.

explanation: Diagnosis: primary meningoencephalitis due to Naegleria fowleri. The patient’s history of nasal irrigation with unboiled well water, rapid neutrophilic CSF pleocytosis with hypoglycorrhachia and high protein, lack of response to broad‑spectrum antibacterials, and demonstration of motile trophozoites in CSF confirm the diagnosis. Treatment efforts center on amphotericin B–based regimens, adjunctive azoles in many centres, aggressive control of intracranial pressure and intensive supportive care; prognosis remains poor and early suspicion in the appropriate exposure setting is essential.

hints.text: A 24-year-old male cacao farm worker presents with 6 months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnoea; he reports intermittent crampy abdominal pain and noticed pruritic erythematous papules on the soles of his feet. On examination he is pale, heart rate 110 min−1, and has faint conjunctival pallor without jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly.

He was raised and continues to work in a rural coastal community in northeastern Brazil, habitually barefoot while tending wet soil and irrigation furrows. He completed a short over-the-counter iron course with minimal improvement and has no prior transfusions or chronic medical conditions.

Haemoglobin 72 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 62 fL, ferritin 7 µg/L; eosinophils 2.4 × 10^9/L. Stool guaiac positive and direct faecal microscopy shows numerous oval thin‑shelled eggs with morulated embryos compatible with a strongyliform nematode.

Chest radiograph obtained for intermittent dry cough several weeks earlier showed patchy perihilar air‑space opacities; repeat film 3 weeks later demonstrated near‑complete clearance of those faint infiltrates.

The patient received a single 400 mg oral dose of albendazole; within 48 hours several thin live nematodes were observed in the stool. Light microscopy of recovered adult worms showed lengths near 8–10 mm and buccal cutting plates without visible teeth. After deworming plus oral iron his symptoms and haemoglobin progressively improved.

explanation: Findings—progressive microcytic iron‑deficiency anaemia, foot pruritic lesions from skin penetration, peripheral eosinophilia, transient pulmonary infiltrates during larval migration, ova on stool microscopy, and recovery of small adult nematodes with buccal cutting plates—are most consistent with infection by Necator americanus. Management includes benzimidazole therapy (albendazole or mebendazole) and iron repletion; differentiation from species with oral 'teeth' is made by adult buccal morphology.

hints.text: A 22-year-old woman presents to the sexual health clinic with a 3-day history of purulent vaginal discharge, dysuria, and mild lower abdominal cramping. She is afebrile. On speculum examination there is mucopurulent cervical discharge and mild cervical friability; bimanual exam elicits minimal cervical motion tenderness.

She reports a new male sexual partner 10 days ago and condomless vaginal intercourse. She had an uncomplicated chlamydia infection 18 months earlier that was treated. The partner described an episode of urethral discharge one week before she became symptomatic.
Transvaginal ultrasound shows normal-appearing ovaries, no tubo-ovarian abscess, and no hydrosalpinx. There is minimal free fluid in the pelvis but no adnexal mass or other focal pathology.

When contacted, the recent partner confirms he sought urgent care for profuse urethral discharge and received a single intramuscular dose of a third‑generation cephalosporin the week prior to the patient’s presentation.

Nucleic acid amplification test (endocervical swab) returned positive for a bacterial sexually transmitted pathogen. Gram stain of cervical discharge shows abundant polymorphonuclear leukocytes and numerous intracellular gram‑negative diplococci within epithelial cells.

explanation: The combination of acute mucopurulent cervicitis after recent unprotected sexual exposure, a partner with purulent urethritis, and a Gram stain demonstrating intracellular gram‑negative diplococci identifies Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Confirmation is commonly by NAAT from mucosal specimens; pelvic imaging without abscess supports localized cervicitis rather than advanced pelvic inflammatory disease.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man is seen in the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever, migratory arthralgias and an acutely painful swollen right wrist. He also has several small pustular lesions on the dorsal hands and fingers and pain along the flexor tendon sheath of the right wrist that limits active motion.

He reports a brief episode of urethral burning and scant clear discharge about 10 days earlier that resolved without treatment. He had unprotected sex with a new partner two weeks ago and has no other chronic medical conditions.

Blood tests show WBC 14.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 11.0 × 10^9/L and C‑reactive protein 110 mg/L. Blood cultures at 48 hours remain negative. Synovial aspiration from the wrist yields turbid fluid with markedly elevated neutrophils; Gram stain shows few organisms and routine synovial fluid cultures are sterile so far.

Point‑of‑care ultrasound of the wrist demonstrates a radiocarpal joint effusion with distension of the extensor and flexor tendon sheaths consistent with tenosynovitis; plain radiographs show no joint space narrowing or bony destruction.
He was started empirically on intravenous ceftriaxone. Within 48 hours his fever abated, the pustular lesions began crusting, and wrist pain and range of motion improved substantially.

explanation: A young adult with preceding urethritis, migratory arthritis with prominent tenosynovitis and pustular skin lesions, sterile or paucibacillary synovial fluid and rapid clinical response to parenteral third‑generation cephalosporin is most consistent with disseminated infection due to Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Diagnostic confirmation can be obtained from NAAT or culture of urogenital or pharyngeal specimens rather than relying on blood or synovial fluid cultures alone.

hints.text: A 42-year-old woman presents to clinic with 6 weeks of worsening cough, daily wheeze and intermittent expectoration of thick brownish plugs. She reports reduced exercise tolerance and two prior courses of oral antibiotics over the past year for presumed chest infections without sustained improvement.

Longstanding moderate asthma diagnosed in childhood, uses inhaled corticosteroid intermittently. Atopic dermatitis since adolescence. No systemic immunosuppression. Works part time at a municipal composting facility and recalls heavy dust exposure from decaying leaves last month.
Peripheral eosinophil count 1.6 × 10^9/L. Total IgE 2 200 kU/L. Sputum Gram stain showed no predominant bacterial pathogen. Serum C-reactive protein 18 mg/L.

High resolution CT chest shows central bronchiectasis most pronounced in the upper lobes, with branching mucus-filled bronchi producing a finger-in-glove appearance. No nodules or cavities identified.

Expectorated mucus plug submitted for mycology grew a thermotolerant mould on culture at 37 °C. Microscopy from the colony revealed septate hyphae with acute-angle branching. Serum and sputum specific IgE to the isolated mould were markedly elevated.

explanation: The history of poorly controlled asthma, marked total IgE elevation, peripheral eosinophilia, central bronchiectasis with mucus plugging, occupational exposure to decaying plant matter and recovery of a thermotolerant septate mould with acute-angle branching together point to hypersensitivity lung disease due to Aspergillus fumigatus. The combination of clinical, immunologic and microbiologic findings supports allergic bronchopulmonary involvement rather than simple colonization.

hints.text: A 58-year-old man is admitted with fever and pleuritic left-sided chest pain that began on day 12 after induction chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukaemia. He has a non-productive cough and a single episode of haemoptysis.

He received cytarabine and daunorubicin 2 weeks ago. For 9 days he has been neutropenic with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.05 × 10^9/L. He received prophylactic antibacterial agents but no mould-active prophylaxis.

C-reactive protein 220 mg/L. Blood cultures remain negative after 48 hours. Serum galactomannan index 1.8. Bronchoalveolar lavage was performed because fever persisted despite broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Contrast CT chest shows multiple peripheral nodules in the left lower lobe. Several nodules have surrounding ground-glass attenuation consistent with a halo sign. One focus shows early cavitation.

Patient did not improve on antibacterial therapy. Intravenous voriconazole was started empirically. Within 72 hours fever and pleuritic pain diminished. BAL culture later grew a thermotolerant mould demonstrating septate hyphae with acute-angle branching.

explanation: Prolonged profound neutropenia after induction chemotherapy, new pleuritic chest pain with haemoptysis, CT nodules with halo sign, positive serum galactomannan, and culture showing a thermotolerant septate mould with acute-angle branching together indicate invasive pulmonary disease caused by Aspergillus fumigatus. Rapid clinical improvement after initiation of voriconazole supports the diagnosis.

hints.text: A 35-year-old man presents with 6 weeks of productive cough, intermittent fevers, night sweats, and a 5 kg weight loss. He also has two enlarging verrucous plaques, one on the left forearm and one on the cheek. He works outdoors as a river restoration technician and reports increasing dyspnea on exertion.

He is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, is HIV negative by recent testing, and has received no immunosuppressive therapy. He completed two courses of oral antibiotics for presumed bacterial pneumonia without clinical improvement.

Blood leukocyte count 14.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophil predominant. C-reactive protein 94 mg/L. Sputum AFB smears negative. Punch biopsy of a skin lesion shows granulomatous inflammation and numerous large yeast forms on PAS stain with broad-based budding.

Chest radiograph shows patchy consolidation in the right upper lobe and multiple bilateral nodular opacities, one with a 2.5 cm cavitary change. CT chest confirms right upper lobe consolidation with scattered nodules.

On further questioning he reports that three weeks before symptoms began he spent several days clearing driftwood and rebuilding a beaver dam along a nearby river valley, frequently disturbing damp soil and rotting wood.

explanation: The combination of chronic pulmonary disease with verrucous cutaneous lesions, histopathology showing large yeast with broad-based budding, and recent exposure to riverbank soil and decaying wood points to Blastomyces dermatitidis. The organism is a dimorphic fungus that produces broad-based budding yeast in tissue and can disseminate to skin and bone. Treatment for moderate or disseminated disease is oral itraconazole after initial amphotericin B for severe presentations.

hints.text: A 32-year-old woman presents to primary care with three days of intense vulvar pruritus and a thick white vaginal discharge. Symptoms began two days after she completed a 10-day course of oral amoxicillin for sinusitis. She reports burning on urination and discomfort with intercourse. On examination the labia are erythematous and there are adherent white curd-like plaques in the vaginal vestibule.

Her medical history is unremarkable. She is not diabetic and not pregnant. She uses combined oral contraceptives. She has had no new sexual partners and no prior similar episodes requiring systemic therapy.

Vaginal pH measured at 4.3. Wet mount with 10% KOH preparation shows numerous budding yeast forms and pseudohyphae. Gram stain does not show clue cells and bacterial culture returned without pathogenic growth.
Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound was performed to exclude anatomic causes. The uterus and adnexa have normal appearance. No fluid collection or tubo-ovarian abnormality is identified.

She received a single 150 mg oral dose of an azole antifungal. Within 48 hours pruritus and dysuria were markedly reduced. At one-week follow-up the discharge and vulvar erythema were nearly resolved.

explanation: The clinical picture is consistent with vulvovaginal candidiasis due to Candida albicans. Recent broad-spectrum antibiotic use, normal vaginal pH, identification of budding yeasts and pseudohyphae on KOH, and rapid response to a single-dose oral azole support this diagnosis.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman from coastal Massachusetts presents to clinic with 6 days of fever, drenching night sweats and dark urine. She reports temperatures up to 39.0 °C. On examination she is icteric and has a palpable spleen 2 cm below the left costal margin.

She is otherwise healthy. She recalls an attached tick after a day hike on Nantucket 12 days before onset. She has not travelled outside New England and has no history of blood transfusion or chronic illness.

Hb 82 g/L, reticulocyte count 8%, WBC 6.4 × 10^9/L, platelets 110 × 10^9/L. Bilirubin 60 μmol/L, LDH 980 U/L, haptoglobin <0.1 g/L. Peripheral blood smear shows intraerythrocytic ring forms with an estimated parasitaemia of 3%. Rapid malaria antigen test is negative.
Abdominal ultrasound shows a spleen length of 14 cm without focal lesion. No hepatomegaly is seen.
Oral atovaquone plus azithromycin was started. Fevers resolved within 48 hours and repeat smear showed parasitaemia <0.5% at 72 hours. PCR for an intraerythrocytic protozoan returned positive.

explanation: Presentation of febrile hemolytic anemia after a tick bite in an endemic area, intraerythrocytic ring forms on smear, and rapid improvement with atovaquone plus azithromycin are characteristic of infection with Babesia microti. PCR confirmation and falling parasitaemia after targeted therapy support the diagnosis.

hints.text: A 69-year-old man comes to the emergency department with 5 days of fever, fatigue and dark urine. Symptoms began three weeks after he received packed red blood cells during a hip replacement. On arrival he is tachycardic and icteric.
His medical history is notable for traumatic splenectomy, hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. He lives in a rural area and denies recent travel outside the region.

Hb 75 g/L, reticulocyte count 12%, WBC 7.1 × 10^9/L, platelets 95 × 10^9/L. Bilirubin 120 μmol/L, LDH 1450 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable. Peripheral smear shows intraerythrocytic ring forms and red cell fragmentation with estimated parasitaemia 12%.
Chest radiograph shows no acute airspace consolidation. Abdominal CT demonstrates postsplenectomy anatomy without abscess or other acute intra-abdominal process.

Because of high parasitaemia and asplenia he underwent urgent red cell exchange and was started on intravenous clindamycin plus quinine. Parasitaemia fell to 3% within 24 hours and he became afebrile. PCR for an intraerythrocytic protozoan returned positive.

explanation: Severe hemolysis with high parasitaemia after a blood transfusion, marked risk in asplenic patients, intraerythrocytic ring forms on smear and response to clindamycin plus quinine with red cell exchange are consistent with Babesia microti. Transfusion-transmitted cases and severe disease in patients without spleens support this diagnosis.

hints.text: A 58-year-old woman presents to clinic with a two-month history of chronic cough, occasional blood-streaked sputum, low-grade night sweats and 4 kg weight loss. She is a current smoker and reports persistent right upper chest discomfort but no severe dyspnoea.

She is otherwise well, takes no immunosuppressive medications, and tested HIV negative three months ago. She recently cleaned a large backyard pigeon coop and reports heavy pigeon droppings there for several years.

Peripheral blood count shows WBC 8.9 × 10^9/L, CRP 24 mg/L. Sputum AFB smears and PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis are negative. Routine bacterial cultures grow oral flora only. Cytology of induced sputum shows occasional extracellular round yeast forms.
Chest computed tomography demonstrates a solitary 2.6 cm cavitary nodule in the right upper lobe with surrounding ground-glass change and no mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

A CT-guided core biopsy was performed. Histology showed round yeasts with a prominent mucopolysaccharide capsule highlighted by mucicarmine stain. Culture of tissue on Sabouraud medium produced mucoid colonies and the isolate was urease positive. She improved clinically on oral fluconazole after partial wedge resection.

explanation: An otherwise immunocompetent patient with pigeon exposure, a solitary cavitary pulmonary nodule, mucicarmine-positive encapsulated yeast on tissue biopsy, mucoid urease-positive culture and response to azole therapy are consistent with pulmonary infection by Cryptococcus neoformans.

hints.text: An 86-year-old woman from a residential care home is brought to hospital with 48 hours of fever, rigors, and new confusion. She reports acute dysuria. Blood pressure 90/60 mmHg and heart rate 110/min. She has an indwelling urinary catheter placed 10 days earlier after a hip fracture.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3, and recurrent urinary tract infections. Two weeks ago she completed a 10-day course of ceftriaxone for a presumed chest infection.

Urinalysis shows pyuria and is nitrite negative. Urine culture yields small, non-haemolytic colonies of Gram-positive cocci. Admission blood cultures grew the same Gram-positive cocci in chains at about 28 hours. White blood cell count 14.8 × 10^9/L. Serum creatinine 160 μmol/L.
Renal ultrasound demonstrates normal renal size without hydronephrosis. The urinary catheter tip projects within the bladder and there is no evidence of perinephric fluid collection or abscess.

Empiric piperacillin-tazobactam was started. After culture and susceptibility results the team switched to intravenous ampicillin. She defervesced within 48 hours and repeat blood cultures became sterile. The isolate was reported resistant to cephalosporins but susceptible to ampicillin.

explanation: Elderly patient with a recent indwelling catheter, prior cephalosporin exposure, and bloodstream and urine isolates showing Gram-positive cocci in chains that are resistant to cephalosporins but ampicillin susceptible indicates Enterococcus faecalis. Clinical improvement after switching to ampicillin supports the identification and guides management of catheter-related enterococcal bacteremia.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents to clinic with 2 weeks of progressive fatigue, anorexia, nausea, dark urine and yellowing of the eyes. He reports mild right upper quadrant discomfort and a low-grade fever. On exam he is jaundiced and tender in the right upper quadrant. He mentions a new sexual partner about 6 weeks ago and says he did not receive childhood immunisation for the relevant infection.

No prior liver disease. Denies intravenous drug use, recent tattoos, travel outside the country, or heavy alcohol intake. No regular medications and denies acetaminophen overdose. HIV test last year was negative.

Abdominal ultrasound shows an enlarged liver with preserved echotexture, no focal hepatic lesions, and no biliary dilatation. Portal and hepatic vein flow appear normal. No gallstones or cholecystitis.

Serum ALT 1 920 U/L, AST 1 080 U/L, total bilirubin 85 µmol/L, INR 1.4, WBC 6.8 × 10^9/L, platelets 230 × 10^9/L. Serum PCR detects viral DNA at 6.2 × 10^7 IU/mL. IgM antibody directed against a viral core antigen is present.

Oral nucleotide analogue therapy was started given symptomatic acute liver injury and high viraemia. Within 3 weeks the patient reports improved appetite and decreasing jaundice. Repeat serum viral DNA decreased by >3 log10 to 4.1 × 10^4 IU/mL and ALT is falling toward the reference range.

explanation: Acute infection with Hepatitis B virus. The presentation of acute icteric hepatitis after recent sexual exposure, marked transaminase elevation with IgM core antibody, high serum viral DNA by PCR, and rapid decline in DNA after a nucleos(t)ide analogue support the diagnosis and distinguish this from other viral hepatitis pathogens.

hints.text: A 62-year-old retired poultry farmer presents with 3 months of productive cough, intermittent haemoptysis and an 8 kg unintentional weight loss. He reports night sweats and reduced exercise tolerance. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history. On exam he appears thin with localized coarse crackles over the right upper chest.
Known chronic obstructive pulmonary disease treated with inhaled bronchodilators. No prior diagnosis of tuberculosis. HIV test negative. No systemic immunosuppressive drugs.

Full blood count shows haemoglobin 105 g/L, WBC 8.1 × 10^9/L, platelets 240 × 10^9/L. ESR 78 mm/h. Three sputum samples negative for acid-fast bacilli by smear and NAAT. Sputum fungal stain reveals small intracellular yeast forms within alveolar macrophages.
Chest CT shows a 4.2 cm thin-walled cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe with surrounding fibrotic scarring. Multiple calcified hilar lymph nodes are present.

He has lived his whole life in a river basin region endemic for cave and bird roost exposures. He routinely cleaned an old chicken coop and the attic of a barn where heavy bird droppings accumulated. Respiratory cultures later grew a dimorphic fungus.

explanation: An older smoker with chronic upper‑lobe cavitation, systemic symptoms, calcified hilar nodes, sputum showing small intracellular yeasts within macrophages, and long‑standing exposure to bird droppings in an endemic river basin is consistent with chronic pulmonary infection due to Histoplasma capsulatum. Such cases can mimic tuberculosis. Treatment for chronic cavitary disease is prolonged oral itraconazole, with amphotericin B reserved for severe presentations.

hints.text: A 46-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-week history of progressive cough, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, night sweats, and a 6 kg weight loss. He reports low-grade fevers and increasing exertional breathlessness. On exam he is afebrile with oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Chest auscultation reveals reduced breath sounds and coarse crackles localized to the right upper lung field.

Past medical history notable only for type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin, recent HbA1c 9.2%. He is a current smoker, 20 pack-years. He completed a short course of oral amoxicillin prescribed by his general practitioner 3 weeks ago with no clinical improvement. He denies known HIV, haematologic malignancy, chronic corticosteroid use, or previous tuberculosis.

Blood tests show WBC 12.0 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.5 × 10^9/L. CRP 92 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures are sterile. Sputum bacterial culture shows normal respiratory flora. Three induced sputum smears for acid fast bacilli are negative. Routine renal and liver function are within reference ranges.

Chest CT demonstrates a 4.5 cm thin-walled cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe with adjacent patchy nodular consolidation and several small peribronchial nodules in the same lobe. No pleural effusion. Mediastinal lymph nodes are mildly enlarged. A comparison chest radiograph from 8 weeks earlier had shown only a small right apical scarring.

He reports a 10-day work assignment two months before symptom onset grading unpaved roads in the San Joaquin Valley. During that period a windstorm produced heavy dust clouds. Two co-workers developed febrile respiratory illnesses. A CT-guided core biopsy of the cavitary lesion was performed. Histopathology shows large nonbudding spherical structures 20–60 μm in diameter filled with numerous uniform endospores.

explanation: The combination of progressive cavitary right upper lobe disease, eosinophilia, recent heavy dust exposure in the San Joaquin Valley, and histology demonstrating large spherules containing endospores is diagnostic for infection with Coccidioides species. Poor glycaemic control increases risk of more extensive pulmonary disease. Culture and serologic testing can support the diagnosis, and antifungal therapy is indicated for significant pulmonary involvement or risk factors for progression.

hints.text: A 34-year-old woman employed at a neighborhood daycare presents with 10 days of progressive fatigue and anorexia, 4 days of dark urine and pale stools, and two days of visible jaundice. She reports mild right upper quadrant discomfort but no vomiting or abdominal distension. On exam she is afebrile at 37.8°C, icteric, and has a 2 cm tender hepatomegaly, no peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease.

She has no known chronic medical conditions, drinks alcohol only socially, and has never used injection drugs. Four weeks earlier several toddlers in her room had an episode of vomiting and diarrhoea while she continued routine diapering and food handling. She does not recall prior immunization against the enteric viral causes of acute hepatitis.

Blood tests show ALT 1 950 U/L, AST 920 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L, total bilirubin 150 μmol/L, INR 1.1. Full blood count is within reference range with WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L and platelets 230 × 10^9/L. Screening tests for chronic viral hepatitis markers and for autoimmune hepatitis were negative.

Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates mild hepatomegaly and homogeneous increased echogenicity. The gallbladder is normal and the common bile duct is not dilated. There are no calculi and no focal hepatic lesions.

She was managed with outpatient supportive care and hydration. Over two weeks her jaundice and transaminases began to fall and clinical recovery followed. Convalescent testing showed detectable IgM directed against the acute enterically transmitted hepatotropic virus and stool PCR detected the corresponding viral RNA, confirming an acute, self-limited infection caused by a non-enveloped, positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus.

explanation: The patient has an acute hepatocellular jaundice following probable fecal-oral exposure in a daycare setting, with very high ALT and ALT>AST, normal bile ducts on imaging, and eventual detection of virus-specific IgM and stool viral RNA. Those features identify an acute, self-limited enterically transmitted hepatitis caused by a non-enveloped positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus. Supportive care with consideration of post-exposure measures for close contacts is the usual management.

hints.text: A 58-year-old man on hospital day 21 presents with a 7-day history of low-grade fevers up to 38.1°C and new pruritic, finely scaly, hypopigmented macules across the upper chest and back. He reports the rash started gradually and is worse with heat. On exam there are multiple 1–3 cm flaky macules and a few tender erythematous papules on the anterior chest. A tunneled central venous catheter is in place.

Three weeks earlier he underwent emergency small bowel resection for mesenteric ischemia and now has short bowel syndrome. He has been receiving total parenteral nutrition including an intravenous lipid emulsion for 18 days. He completed a 10-day course of broad-spectrum antibiotics for intra-abdominal contamination and received a short course of systemic corticosteroids for bronchospasm while in the ICU.

Admission labs show WBC 11.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 145 mg/L, platelets 98 × 10^9/L. Paired peripheral and catheter blood cultures were reported as positive for small budding yeast forms on Gram stain in 3/3 sets. Subculture on routine media produced no colonies. When an agar overlaid with olive oil was used, smooth creamy colonies grew within 48 hours. Scale from the chest on KOH preparation revealed round yeast forms with short hyphae.

Point-of-care ultrasound of the catheter showed echogenic material adherent to the distal lumen suggestive of intraluminal thrombus. Chest radiograph showed a correctly positioned catheter tip and no focal pulmonary consolidation. No intra-abdominal collections were identified on bedside ultrasound.

The catheter was removed and the lipid component of parenteral nutrition was stopped. Fever resolved within 36 hours and repeat blood cultures were sterile at 48 hours. The organism retrieved from the lipid-supplemented culture matched the yeast forms seen on the skin KOH.

explanation: The combination of superficial scaly hypopigmented trunk lesions with yeast and short hyphae on KOH, a lipid-dependent yeast recovered only on oil-supplemented media, catheter-associated fungemia in a patient receiving intravenous lipid emulsion, and rapid clearance after catheter removal and stopping lipids point to Malassezia furfur. Malassezia species are lipophilic yeasts that can colonize skin causing pityriasis versicolor and can seed intravascular catheters in patients on lipid-containing parenteral nutrition. Confirmation in this case relied on the lipid-supplemented culture and the concordant skin KOH appearance, and management required catheter removal and antifungal therapy directed at a lipophilic yeast.

hints.text: A 19-year-old male university student presents to the emergency department 12 hours after onset of fever and severe bifrontal headache. He reports vomiting, photophobia and neck stiffness. During triage he developed scattered petechial lesions on his trunk and lower limbs. Temperature 39.7°C, heart rate 125/min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, Glasgow Coma Scale 14.

Previously healthy, no history of splenectomy or immunodeficiency. He smokes lightly and recalls not having received the adolescent conjugate meningococcal vaccine. Symptoms progressed from malaise to severe headache and rash over a single day.

Bloods show white cell count 18.6 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, platelets 45 × 10^9/L, CRP 320 mg/L and INR 1.8. Lumbar puncture after CT had opening pressure 28 cm H2O. CSF was turbid with leucocytes 2100 × 10^6/L (predominantly neutrophils), protein 2.5 g/L and glucose 0.8 mmol/L with concurrent serum glucose 6.2 mmol/L. Gram stain of CSF demonstrated Gram-negative diplococci.

Contrast CT abdomen performed for ongoing hypotension shows bilaterally enlarged adrenal glands with peripheral high attenuation and central low attenuation, consistent with acute bilateral adrenal haemorrhage.

He shares a 12-person hall of residence. Public health were notified and identified multiple close contacts; single-dose oral fluoroquinolone prophylaxis was given to exposed housemates. Nasopharyngeal PCRs from two roommates were positive for an oxidase-positive, maltose-fermenting Gram-negative diplococcus.

explanation: Presentation of abrupt fever, meningism, petechial rash, CSF with neutrophilic pleocytosis and Gram-negative diplococci, plus bilateral adrenal haemorrhage and need for contact prophylaxis support the diagnosis of Neisseria meningitidis invasive disease. Initial management includes immediate empiric IV third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone (or cefotaxime), aggressive haemodynamic support, and early corticosteroid and mineralocorticoid replacement when adrenal insufficiency is suspected. Notify public health for chemoprophylaxis of close contacts and consider vaccination of exposed, susceptible individuals according to local guidance.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with 18 hours of abdominal cramps, profuse watery diarrhoea 8 times, nausea and two episodes of vomiting. She reported a fever at home of 38.9°C and felt light-headed when standing. On arrival she was febrile 38.7°C, heart rate 108 bpm, blood pressure 98/60 mmHg, and had dry mucous membranes and diffuse abdominal tenderness without peritoneal signs.

She is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, is not pregnant and has no history of chronic gastrointestinal disease. She had not taken antibiotics or acid-suppressing drugs in the prior month and had no recent international travel. She works in childcare and had no known animal exposures at home.

Blood tests showed leukocytosis 14.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 11.0 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 78 mg/L. Creatinine 120 μmol/L consistent with mild pre-renal azotaemia. Stool microscopy positive for fecal leukocytes and faint occult blood. Initial stool culture returned growth of a motile, oxidase-negative, non-lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus that produces hydrogen sulphide on triple sugar iron agar.

CT abdomen and pelvis with oral and IV contrast performed for persistent pain showed circumferential mucosal thickening of the terminal ileum and several mildly enlarged adjacent mesenteric lymph nodes. No perforation or bowel obstruction was identified.

On targeted questioning she disclosed attending a family gathering 36 hours before illness, where multiple attendees ate a homemade chicken liver pâté and several developed similar diarrhoeal illness. Public health cultures later recovered the same motile, hydrogen sulphide–producing, non-lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus from both the leftover pâté and stool samples from affected contacts.

explanation: Presentation of acute onset inflammatory diarrhoea after a poultry-derived food exposure, stool leukocytes, terminal ileitis on imaging and isolation of a motile, H2S-producing, non-lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus point to non-typhoidal Salmonella species. Illness is often self-limited in immunocompetent adults, with fluid resuscitation and avoidance of antimotility agents. Antibiotics are reserved for severe disease, high-risk hosts, or invasive infection.

hints.text: A 72-year-old woman, 18 hours after repair of a displaced femoral neck fracture, develops sudden profuse non-bloody vomiting with lightheadedness and one episode of watery diarrhea. On arrival to the ward she is tachycardic at 112 beats/min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, afebrile, with dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor.

Past medical history notable for hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. She ate the ward meal the evening before symptom onset and sat in the communal day room. Nursing staff report that two other postoperative patients on the same ward and one healthcare worker reported vomiting within the same 24 hour period.

Laboratory testing shows white cell count 6.4 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 18 mg/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L and creatinine 200 μmol/L, from a baseline of 85 μmol/L. Stool microscopy shows no fecal leukocytes. Routine stool culture and toxin testing for Clostridioides difficile are negative. A stool sample has been submitted for molecular testing.

Non-contrast abdominal CT performed to exclude postsurgical complications shows diffusely fluid-filled stomach and proximal small bowel without transition point, no free air, and no focal bowel wall enhancement to suggest ischemia.

In an outbreak investigation public health staff link 38 of 118 attendees at a hospital fundraiser to a single catered buffet item handled by a food worker who became symptomatic the morning after the event. RT-PCR performed at the public health laboratory detected a small non-enveloped positive-sense RNA virus in stools from the index food worker and from this patient.

explanation: The clinical picture is an acute, vomiting-predominant gastroenteritis with rapid onset after a shared meal, low inflammatory markers, absence of fecal leukocytes, and point-source transmission from a symptomatic food handler. Molecular detection of a small, non-enveloped positive-sense RNA virus from patient and food handler identifies the pathogen as norovirus. Management is supportive with fluid and electrolyte repletion, and outbreak control requires strict contact precautions, exclusion of ill food handlers, and environmental cleaning with an agent active against non-enveloped viruses.

hints.text: A 42-year-old primary school teacher presents with 3 days of nasal congestion and hoarseness followed by 24 hours of a harsh 'barking' cough. He has low-grade fever to 38.0°C and progressive noisy breathing, worse at night. On examination he has biphasic stridor at rest, mild intercostal retractions, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air.

Past medical history notable only for well controlled asthma treated with a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid. He received this season's influenza vaccine. He reports being otherwise well until this illness and has had no recent intubation, facial trauma, or dental procedure.

Blood count shows WBC 7.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 4.8 × 10^9/L and lymphocytes 1.9 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 28 mg/L. Rapid influenza antigen and respiratory syncytial virus PCR are negative. Throat bacterial culture pending. A nasopharyngeal multiplex PCR has been sent.

Anteroposterior neck radiograph obtained because of persistent stridor shows subglottic narrowing producing a focal tapering of the upper trachea consistent with laryngotracheobronchitis. Frontal chest radiograph is clear without focal consolidation.

Occupational review reveals a small cluster of children at a nearby preschool had abrupt onset barking cough over the prior week and two of his teacher colleagues developed similar symptoms. Subsequent testing of several exposed children identified an enveloped negative-sense single-stranded RNA virus that agglutinates red blood cells and produces syncytia in cell culture. The patient's nasopharyngeal PCR returned positive for the same agent.

explanation: The adult presents with croup-like laryngotracheobronchitis after close exposure to preschool-aged children and an outbreak among colleagues. Radiography shows subglottic narrowing and standard viral testing excluded influenza and RSV. The combination of an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus that causes hemagglutination, induces syncytium formation, and is frequently implicated in paediatric croup points to parainfluenza virus. Management focuses on airway monitoring, nebulised epinephrine and systemic corticosteroids for symptomatic relief, with supportive care for lower airway involvement if it develops.

hints.text: A 28-year-old primigravida at 22 weeks' gestation presents to the antenatal clinic with 5 days of low-grade fever and new symmetric joint pain in her wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints. She notes mild facial flushing but no pruritus. She teaches in a primary school. Fetal movements are reported as normal. On examination temperature is 37.8°C and the rest of the maternal exam is unremarkable.

No chronic medical conditions, no known haemoglobinopathy, and no recent transfusions. Pregnancy to date had been uncomplicated with normal first trimester screens. She is not on immunosuppressive medication and has no history of frequent infections.

Maternal blood tests show haemoglobin 98 g/L, white cell count 6.5 × 10^9/L, platelets 220 × 10^9/L. Reticulocyte percentage is 0.4%. C-reactive protein 3 mg/L. Liver transaminases are within reference limits. Routine prenatal serology pending.

Targeted obstetric ultrasound demonstrates fetal ascites, scalp oedema and a moderate pericardial effusion consistent with hydrops fetalis. Middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity is 1.8 multiples of median for gestation, consistent with moderate to severe fetal anaemia.

Public health follow up reveals an outbreak in her class the previous week, several pupils had abrupt onset facial erythema described by parents as 'slapped cheeks' followed by a reticular truncal rash. Maternal serology later returned positive IgM and PCR on amniotic fluid detected high copy numbers of a small single-stranded DNA virus known to infect erythroid precursors.

explanation: The combination of a pregnant woman with recent febrile illness and arthralgia, occupational exposure to children with 'slapped-cheek' rash, maternal IgM and PCR positivity for a small single-stranded DNA virus, and fetal hydrops with elevated MCA-PSV indicating anemia points to infection with Parvovirus B19. The virus infects erythroid progenitors and can cause profound fetal anaemia leading to hydrops. Management considerations in this case include fetal monitoring, consideration of intrauterine transfusion for fetal anaemia, and serial maternal and fetal testing.

hints.text: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 48 hours of high fevers, severe frontal headache, and vomiting. She reports dark urine and increasing lethargy. On arrival she is febrile 39.5°C, tachycardic 120/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, mildly jaundiced, and intermittently disoriented to time.

No chronic medical conditions except elective splenectomy after a motor vehicle collision five years ago. She is not pregnant. She left work early because of progressive weakness over two days, and had no recent antibiotics. She has no known allergies and takes no routine medications.

Laboratory results show haemoglobin 68 g/L, white cell count 6.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 38 × 10^9/L, total bilirubin 68 µmol/L, creatinine 150 µmol/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, and LDH 980 U/L. Peripheral blood film reported marked haemolysis and frequent red cell inclusions; urgent parasitological studies were requested.
Non-contrast CT head shows mild diffuse cerebral oedema without discrete focal infarct or intracranial haemorrhage. Abdominal ultrasound confirms absence of spleen and mild hepatomegaly.

She returned nine days ago from a two-week visit to relatives in coastal West Africa where she slept without bed netting and did not take any chemoprophylaxis. Thick and thin blood films prepared in the ED show high-level intraerythrocytic parasitaemia measured at 12%, numerous small ring forms with multiple rings in single erythrocytes, and crescent-shaped sexual stages on the thin film.

explanation: Recent travel to West Africa, asplenia, severe haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, elevated lactate and creatinine indicate severe malaria. The high parasitaemia, multiple ring forms per erythrocyte, and crescent-shaped gametocytes on thin film identify Plasmodium falciparum as the cause. This clinical picture meets criteria for severe falciparum malaria with cerebral involvement and high parasite burden, requiring urgent parenteral antimalarial therapy and supportive care.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency clinic with a 2-week history of progressive exertional breathlessness and a dry cough. He reports low-grade fevers and a 1.5 kg weight loss. On arrival SpO2 is 89% on room air, respiratory rate 22/min. Chest auscultation reveals fine bilateral crackles, no wheeze, no peripheral oedema.

He has known HIV infection diagnosed 2 years earlier but reports stopping antiretroviral therapy 3 months ago after relocating. He has not been taking any prophylactic antibiotics. Six months ago his CD4 count was 120 × 10^6/L. He smokes 5 cigarettes daily and has no recent travel or occupational inhalational exposures.

Arterial blood gas on room air shows PaO2 7.0 kPa. Serum lactate dehydrogenase 820 U/L. White cell count 4.8 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 35 mg/L. Routine sputum cultures and a nasal viral PCR are negative.

High-resolution chest CT shows diffuse bilateral ground-glass attenuation, greatest centrally with relative subpleural sparing. There is mild interlobular septal thickening and no pleural effusion or lobar consolidation.

Empiric high-dose trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole with adjunctive oral corticosteroids was started for presumed opportunistic pneumonia. Over 72 hours his PaO2 rose to 10.8 kPa and dyspnea improved. Bronchoalveolar lavage subsequently yielded foamy proteinaceous material and organisms visualised on methenamine silver stain.

explanation: The subacute dry cough and progressive hypoxaemia in a patient with HIV who stopped antiretroviral therapy, elevated LDH, diffuse ground-glass changes on CT, clinical improvement with high-dose trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus corticosteroids, and cystic organisms on methenamine silver stain are most consistent with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.

hints.text: An 18-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of progressive weakness in his right leg. Two days before the weakness he had fever and sore throat. On exam he is afebrile, alert, with flaccid paresis of the right lower limb, reduced tone, absent deep tendon reflexes on the right, preserved pinprick and light touch, and no sensory level.

He is a university student with no chronic illnesses. Childhood immunisations were incomplete; he received only two infant doses of the standard paralytic disease vaccine series and no later booster. He reports no recent vaccinations since childhood, no intravenous drug use, and no preceding trauma.

CSF shows 40 × 10^6/L leucocytes with 80% lymphocytes, protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L with paired serum glucose 5.0 mmol/L. Bacterial culture of CSF is sterile. Routine blood count is 9.8 × 10^9/L with normal differential. PCR panel for herpesviruses in CSF is negative. Nerve conduction studies show reduced compound muscle action potentials in the affected limb with preserved sensory nerve action potentials.

MRI of the spine demonstrates T2 hyperintense signal and restricted diffusion confined to the anterior horn cell region of the lumbar enlargement, without a compressive lesion or meningeal enhancement.

Two weeks before illness he spent 10 days volunteering at a refugee camp where local public health reported multiple recent cases of acute flaccid paralysis in children. Stool testing from that cluster yielded a small, non-enveloped single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus that matched one of the three serotypes targeted by routine inactivated and oral childhood immunisation regimens.

explanation: The combination of a brief febrile prodrome followed by acute asymmetric flaccid paralysis with motor neuron pattern on exam and electrophysiology, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with modest protein elevation, and MRI showing anterior horn cell involvement localises the process to lower motor neurons. Incomplete childhood immunisation and recent exposure in a camp with reported acute flaccid paralysis point to infection with the poliomyelitis virus. Confirmatory testing is stool viral RNA detection and serotyping, which was reported positive for a vaccine-targeted poliomyelitis serotype from the local cluster.

hints.text: A 66-year-old man presents to the emergency clinic with 10 days of left ear pain, progressive hearing loss, and purulent, malodorous discharge. He is febrile to 38.3°C. On examination the external auditory canal is erythematous and tender, with friable granulation tissue deep in the canal and severe pain on manipulation of the tragus.

He has a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes with poor control, recent HbA1c 9.0%. He reports cleaning his ear with cotton buds and had been swimming daily at a local hotel pool for the week before symptoms began. He used over-the-counter topical drops without improvement.

Blood tests show white cell count 15.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and C-reactive protein 112 mg/L. Capillary glucose is 16 mmol/L. Gram stain of canal discharge demonstrates gram-negative rods. Routine blood cultures remain negative at 48 hours.

Contrast CT of the temporal bones shows focal erosion of the posterolateral external auditory canal with soft tissue extension into the adjacent temporal bone and early medially directed bone involvement, findings concerning for skull base osteomyelitis.

Environmental cultures were obtained from the hotel pool and the patient’s home shower. Isolates from the ear discharge and from the pool grew an oxidase positive, non-fermenting gram-negative rod that produced blue-green pigment, emitted a distinct fruity odour on culture plates, and grew at 42°C.

explanation: Elderly patient with poorly controlled diabetes, refractory external ear infection and imaging consistent with skull base osteomyelitis points to an organism that commonly colonises moist environments. The isolate is an oxidase positive, non-fermenting gram-negative rod that produces a blue-green pigment and tolerates growth at 42°C. These features identify the pathogen as Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Management for skull base osteomyelitis requires targeted anti-pseudomonal therapy guided by susceptibility testing and prolonged intravenous treatment.

hints.text: A 29-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by family with 4 days of fever, malaise, and headache followed by 24 hours of increasing anxiety, confusion, and agitation. He has intermittent choking and gagging when offered water and appears to drool more than usual. On arrival he is febrile 38.6 °C, tachycardic, diaphoretic, and intermittently disoriented to time. A small healed puncture wound is visible on the dorsum of his right hand.

He returned 3 weeks ago from visiting rural villages where he slept in simple wooden houses. He recalls being awakened one night by something landing on his hand but did not seek care because the wound bled little and healed. He has no relevant chronic medical conditions and reports no recent vaccinations before travel and no wound care or immunisations after the incident.

Admission blood count shows leukocytosis 13.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance. Lumbar puncture on day 1 of neurologic decline: opening pressure normal, CSF white cell count 72 × 10^6/L with 88% lymphocytes, protein 0.95 g/L, glucose 3.8 mmol/L. Gram stain and routine bacterial cultures of CSF are negative. PCR assays for herpes simplex virus and enteroviruses are negative.
