Brain MRI performed on hospital day 2 shows bilateral hyperintense signal on T2/FLAIR within the hippocampi and dorsal medulla. There is no focal mass lesion and no abnormal parenchymal enhancement after contrast. Findings are most prominent in limbic structures and the brainstem.

On further questioning the family confirm that the nocturnal contact three weeks earlier was with a small sleeping mammal that fell onto his hand while he slept; they later found the animal dead in the corner of the room and describe it as bat-sized. No post-exposure prophylaxis or immunisations were obtained after that event.

explanation: Progression from nonspecific prodrome to encephalopathy with autonomic signs, hypersalivation, and painful swallowing plus MRI limbic and brainstem involvement and lymphocytic CSF points toward infection from a neurotropic virus acquired by mammal bite. The history of an unprotected nocturnal bite from a small bat-like mammal with no post-exposure immunisation is highly suggestive of rabies virus infection. Definitive confirmation is by demonstration of viral antigen or RNA in saliva, nuchal skin biopsy nerve endings, or brain tissue and by detection of neutralising antibodies in serum or CSF at later stages.

hints.text: A 68-year-old retired preschool teacher presents to the emergency department with 7 days of progressive dyspnoea and dry cough following a brief runny nose. She reports increased work of breathing and new wheeze. On arrival she is tachypnoeic, uses accessory muscles, and oxygen saturation is 90% on room air, falling to 86% with minimal exertion. Past medical history includes COPD and compensated heart failure.

Two weeks before presentation she completed a short course of oral corticosteroids for a COPD flare. Family history on review reveals that a 7-month-old grandchild stayed in the house during the previous week and was admitted to hospital with bronchiolitis two days before the patient's symptoms began.

Blood tests show white cell count 6.1 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.8 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 4.7 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 28 mg/L. Blood cultures and sputum cultures show no growth after 48 hours. A nasopharyngeal viral PCR panel was submitted on admission and remained pending at 36 hours.

Chest CT without contrast demonstrates diffuse peribronchial thickening, small scattered centrilobular nodules, patchy peribronchial ground-glass opacities and minimal subsegmental atelectasis. There is no focal lobar consolidation to suggest bacterial lobar pneumonia.

Infection‑control flagged a paediatric cluster earlier that week. The grandchild's nasopharyngeal PCR returned positive for the same virus identified by the hospital's paediatric lab. The patient's nasopharyngeal PCR subsequently returned positive for that single pathogen, the same virus for which palivizumab is used as prophylaxis in high‑risk infants.

explanation: Clinical picture: elderly patient with COPD and recent systemic corticosteroids, progressive lower respiratory symptoms, imaging showing airway-centred viral bronchiolitis rather than lobar consolidation, low CRP and negative bacterial cultures all point toward a viral lower respiratory tract infection. Exposure history of a hospitalised infant with bronchiolitis and the positive nasopharyngeal PCR for the same pathogen that is the target of palivizumab identify the agent as Respiratory syncytial virus. RSV commonly causes severe disease in infants and can cause significant lower respiratory disease and exacerbations in older adults and the immunocompromised.

hints.text: A 34-year-old female preschool teacher presents in early autumn with 4 days of nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhoea, sore throat and daytime cough. Over the last 24 hours she developed increased wheeze and dyspnoea on minimal exertion. She reports no high fevers. On examination temperature 37.8 °C, respiratory rate 22/min, oxygen saturation 95% on room air. There is diffuse expiratory wheeze and nasal mucosal oedema.

She has atopic asthma since childhood, on inhaled corticosteroid and short acting bronchodilator. No systemic corticosteroids in the past year. Non-smoker. Influenza vaccination this season. Takes no other regular medicines. She works in a nursery caring for 2–5 year olds. No recent travel, no known sick contacts at home.

Full blood count WBC 7.1 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 4.3 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes 2.0 × 10^9/L, eosinophils 0.3 × 10^9/L. CRP 6 mg/L. Multiplex nasopharyngeal PCR negative for influenza, respiratory syncytial virus, metapneumovirus, adenovirus and seasonal coronaviruses. A single positive PCR signal was detected for a small non-enveloped positive-sense single-stranded RNA respiratory virus. Viral culture from the same swab produced cytopathic effect when incubated at 33 °C but no growth at 37 °C.

Chest radiograph shows peribronchial cuffing and no focal lobar consolidation. Peak expiratory flow measured in clinic 240 L/min, down from her personal best of 420 L/min. No radiographic evidence to suggest bacterial pneumonia.

Nursery records show a cluster of brief upper respiratory illnesses among children 48–72 hours before the teacher became symptomatic. Parents reported runny nose and sneezing without significant fever and rapid recovery. The combination of an adult with asthma exacerbation following a preschool outbreak, a single positive respiratory PCR for a small non-enveloped ssRNA virus, and culture growth only at cooler incubation temperature points to the responsible pathogen.

explanation: The clinical picture is an upper respiratory infection precipitating asthma exacerbation in a preschool teacher after a childcare cluster. Multiplex PCR isolated a single small non-enveloped positive-sense single-stranded RNA respiratory virus and culture grew only at lower incubation temperature. These features identify the agent as a rhinovirus. Negative tests for influenza, RSV and adenovirus make those causes unlikely. The growth preference at approximately 33 °C and the epidemiology in young children distinguish rhinovirus from enteroviruses and from systemic respiratory viruses.

hints.text: A 36-year-old man presents to the emergency clinic with 8 days of progressive left-sided facial pain, nasal congestion, and purulent nasal discharge. Over 48 hours he developed increasing periorbital swelling and left-sided facial numbness. He reports low-grade fevers and difficulty chewing because of pain. On examination he has left periorbital oedema, reduced sensation over the maxillary distribution, and mild proptosis with preserved extraocular movements.

He has transfusion dependent haemoglobinopathy and receives regular red cell transfusions. He has been on iron chelation therapy with an injectable iron chelator for the last 9 months. He denies diabetes, corticosteroid use, recent travel, and HIV risk factors. He had a dental extraction on the same side 3 weeks ago without immediate complications.

Contrast CT of the paranasal sinuses shows opacification of the left maxillary and ethmoid sinuses with irregular erosion of the medial maxillary wall and extension of soft tissue into the inferomedial orbit. There is suggestion of cisternal enhancement along the skull base but no discrete abscess.
He works part time as a community gardener. In the week before symptom onset he spent several days turning an old compost pile and clearing rotting vegetation at a community allotment.

At endoscopic sinus debridement tissue was sent for histology and culture. Routine blood tests showed haemoglobin 72 g/L and white cell count 9.6 × 10^9/L. Histology of necrotic sinus mucosa demonstrates broad, ribbonlike hyphae, largely without septa, branching at irregular, wide angles and invading vessel walls. Fungal culture grew a rapidly expanding woolly colony within 48 hours.

explanation: The combination of invasive sinusitis with rapid angioinvasion on histology, tissue necrosis with orbital extension, occupational exposure to soil and decaying vegetation, and iron chelation therapy points to an infection by a Mucorales organism, most commonly a species in the Rhizopus group. The histopathology finding of broad, pauciseptate hyphae with wide-angle branching and vascular invasion is the key diagnostic clue. Management requires urgent surgical debridement and high-dose amphotericin B along with reversal of predisposing factors, including stopping iron chelation if possible and correcting any metabolic derangements.

hints.text: A 29-year-old man presented in late June with 4 days of high fever to 39.6°C, frontal headache, severe myalgias and progressive rash. The rash began 2 days after fever onset on his wrists and ankles and has spread centrally to involve the forearms and palms. He reports nausea and photophobia. On examination he is febrile 39.2°C, heart rate 110 min−1, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, and has a maculopapular eruption with several petechial lesions on the wrists and palms.

He is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, and works on a forestry crew. Symptoms progressed despite 48 hours of outpatient symptomatic treatment and a single prescription for amoxicillin. He has no recent international travel and no known sick contacts. He does not recall any recent vaccinations beyond childhood schedule.

Routine blood tests show white blood cell count 8.5 × 10^9/L, platelet count 72 × 10^9/L, sodium 130 mmol/L, AST 156 U/L, ALT 98 U/L, and C-reactive protein 118 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures drawn on admission remain sterile at 24 hours. Prothrombin time is within reference range.

Chest radiograph is clear. Non-contrast CT of the head performed for severe headache shows no acute intracranial abnormality. Photographs of the rash taken on admission show early petechial change most prominent at distal extremities.

He remembered removing an attached tick from his right ankle five days before fever onset after an overnight camping trip in a wooded area of the southeastern United States. The rash began at the wrists and ankles within days of the bite and progressed to involve the palms and soles.

explanation: This presentation is most consistent with an acute febrile illness following a recent tick bite, with an eruption that began on wrists and ankles and involved the palms and soles, plus thrombocytopenia, hyponatraemia and elevated transaminases. Rapid clinical improvement with doxycycline is expected. The clinical picture fits infection with Rickettsia rickettsii, the agent responsible for the described tick-borne spotted fever syndrome; diagnosis can be confirmed by paired serology or PCR from blood or tissue.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man five months after allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation presents with four weeks of high-volume watery diarrhoea, up to eight watery stools daily, with crampy periumbilical pain and 4 kg weight loss. He is afebrile, mildly tachycardic, and exam shows dry mucous membranes and diffusely soft, non-tender abdomen without organomegaly.

Immunosuppression includes tacrolimus and low-dose prednisone for chronic graft-versus-host disease. He has not received recent broad-spectrum antibiotics and denies recent travel. He lives with his partner and a 2-year-old child who had an episode of profuse vomiting followed by watery diarrhoea two weeks before the patient's symptoms began.

Blood tests show lymphopenia 0.4 × 10^9/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, creatinine 120 µmol/L and C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Repeated stool studies are negative for bacterial culture and Clostridioides difficile toxin. Multiplex stool PCR returned positive for a non-enveloped, segmented double-stranded RNA virus commonly included on paediatric viral gastroenteritis panels.

Contrast CT abdomen shows diffuse small-bowel wall thickening and luminal fluid without colonic wall thickening, no free air, and only small mesenteric lymph nodes. Findings were felt more consistent with small-intestinal enteritis than with predominant colonic graft-versus-host disease.

The household 2-year-old was unvaccinated for the routine infant vaccine that prevents this pathogen. The toddler's clinic performed a rapid stool enzyme immunoassay which was strongly positive for the same viral antigen detected on the patient's multiplex PCR, and sequencing showed identical viral genotype in both stools.

explanation: Pathogen: Rotavirus. Prolonged, high-volume small-bowel diarrhoea in an immunocompromised adult with lymphopenia, a preceding household paediatric gastroenteritis, positive multiplex PCR for a non-enveloped segmented double-stranded RNA virus, small-bowel predominant imaging, and a positive stool enzyme immunoassay in the toddler together point to rotavirus infection transmitted from the unvaccinated child. Rotavirus can cause prolonged, severe disease in immunocompromised hosts and is commonly detected by antigen EIA and PCR on paediatric panels.

hints.text: A 29-year-old primigravida presents to the antenatal clinic with 48 hours of low-grade fever, sore throat, conjunctival injection and a faint erythematous maculopapular rash that began on the trunk and limbs. She is at estimated 9 weeks' gestation and reports new stiffness and aching in the small joints of both hands.

She has no chronic medical problems. She emigrated from Eastern Europe at age 16 and is unsure of childhood booster vaccines. First-trimester antenatal records contain no documented serology proving immunity to the relevant vaccine-preventable exanthems.

Complete blood count shows WBC 3.9 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.9 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 5 mg/L. Maternal serum returned positive for virus-specific IgM with low-avidity IgG, a pattern consistent with recent primary infection on acute and convalescent serology.

Targeted fetal ultrasound at 13 weeks demonstrates bilateral lens opacities suspicious for cataracts and a prominent arterial duct with abnormal flow pattern. Fetal biometric measurements are at the 10th centile for gestational age.

On review of contacts she reports caring for an unvaccinated 3-year-old niece two weeks before symptom onset. The child had fever and a rash that began on the face and was noted to have enlarged postauricular nodes. Local public health later confirmed the child had a positive PCR for the same RNA virus implicated in the maternal serology.

explanation: This presentation is most consistent with maternal infection by rubella virus in early pregnancy. The clinical picture of a mild febrile exanthem with arthralgia in an adult, recent primary infection pattern on serology (positive IgM with low-avidity IgG), and characteristic fetal findings of cataracts and ductal cardiac abnormality point to congenital infection with rubella virus. The reported exposure to an unvaccinated toddler from an outbreak strengthens the epidemiologic link. Management includes urgent notification of public health, confirmatory testing with RT-PCR if not already done, counselling about high risk of congenital disease, and discussion of options with obstetrics and maternal-fetal medicine.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 7 days of fever, frontal headache, diffuse abdominal pain and constipation. He reports dry cough and anorexia. Temperature 39.0°C with pulse 78 beats per minute. Examination shows a coated tongue and mild right lower quadrant tenderness without peritonism.

He returned 10 days before presentation from a 3-week visit to relatives in South Asia. He has no chronic medical conditions, is not immunosuppressed and declined pretravel immunisations. He took no antibiotics while abroad.

Blood tests show white cell count 3.4 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 110 g/L, platelets 140 × 10^9/L, CRP 86 mg/L and ALT 78 U/L. Blood cultures drawn on admission became positive at 48 hours for a motile, oxidase-negative Gram-negative bacillus that produced hydrogen sulphide on selective media.
Contrast CT of the abdomen demonstrates circumferential thickening of the terminal ileum and ileocaecal valve with several enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes. There is no free air or focal abscess.

During travel he frequently ate street-vendor food including raw salads and ice made with tap water. A household contact in the town works as a food handler and had an episode of febrile gastroenteritis months earlier followed by intermittent diarrhoea.

explanation: Presentation is consistent with enteric fever due to Salmonella Typhi. The subacute febrile illness with headache, abdominal pain and constipation, relative bradycardia, coated tongue, leukopenia and mild transaminitis fits the syndrome. Ileocecal thickening on imaging and a blood culture growing a motile, H2S-producing Gram-negative bacillus after 48 hours support the diagnosis. Recent travel to an endemic area with ingestion of potentially contaminated food and a household contact who handles food raise the likelihood of exposure and possible carrier transmission. Management is targeted antibiotic therapy guided by susceptibility testing, notification of public health authorities, and assessment of close contacts and food-handling practices.

hints.text: A 47-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath that began three hours earlier. He reports a preceding 10-day history of low-grade fever, dry cough and malaise. On arrival he is tachycardic at 112/min, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 89% on room air and temperature 37.9°C. Lung auscultation is unremarkable and there is no unilateral leg swelling.

Medical history notable only for well controlled hypertension. He works in an office. He visited his mother in an assisted living facility 7 days before presentation where several residents developed respiratory symptoms. He completed a two-dose primary vaccine series with an mRNA platform about 10 months ago and has not received a recent booster.

Blood tests show lymphocyte count 0.8 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 5.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 220 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 120 mg/L, ferritin 450 µg/L, D-dimer 3.4 mg/L FEU. Arterial blood gas on room air shows PaO2 7.8 kPa. High-sensitivity troponin is within reference range.

CT pulmonary angiography demonstrates bilateral segmental pulmonary emboli. The study also shows multifocal, bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities with posterior and lower-lobe predominance, without lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

Public health testing at the assisted living facility identified a cluster of residents with sudden loss of smell and taste and positive real-time reverse transcription PCR for the pandemic single-stranded RNA respiratory virus that emerged in 2019. The patient’s nasopharyngeal swab later returned positive by the same RT-PCR assay with a low cycle threshold, consistent with high viral load.

explanation: This patient has an acute pulmonary embolism in the setting of a viral respiratory illness with lymphopenia, raised inflammatory markers and peripheral ground-glass opacities on CT. The exposure history, cluster with anosmia and a positive RT-PCR that identifies the pandemic respiratory virus first recognized in late 2019 point to infection with SARS-CoV-2. COVID-19 can present with modest respiratory symptoms and prominent thromboinflammatory complications such as venous thromboembolism despite minimal focal lung findings on auscultation.

hints.text: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with 48 hours of fever to 39.0°C, crampy lower abdominal pain, urgent sensation with frequent small-volume bowel movements and tenesmus. Parents report 8 to 12 stools since symptoms began, described as bloody and mucoid. On exam he is febrile, mildly tachycardic, has dry mucous membranes and focal tenderness over the left lower quadrant without peritonism.

Previously healthy, developmentally normal, fully immunised, no chronic illness and no recent antibiotics. He attends a community daycare where three other children in his class have been absent this week with diarrhoea. There was no recent travel and no family members with a similar illness until his symptoms began.

Blood tests show white cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and C-reactive protein 68 mg/L. Serum sodium 133 mmol/L. Stool microscopy shows numerous neutrophils and gross blood. Multiplex PCR panel for common viral enteropathogens is negative. Stool culture was requested and processing is underway.

Abdominal ultrasound is obtained because of persistent pain, and shows segmental thickening of the rectosigmoid colon with preserved peristalsis and no free fluid. Findings were reported as consistent with acute colitis, most marked in the distal colon.

Stool culture from the patient and two symptomatic classmates grew a non-lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative gram-negative bacillus. Biochemical testing showed absence of hydrogen sulfide production and the isolate was non-motile on motility medium. The public health team notes a high attack rate among toddlers in the same classroom, consistent with person-to-person transmission in a daycare setting.

explanation: The child presents with acute small-volume bloody mucoid stools, tenesmus, neutrophil-predominant stool and distal colitis on imaging, in the setting of a daycare outbreak. The culture profile of a non-lactose-fermenting, non-motile, H2S-negative Enterobacterales organism recovered from multiple class members points to Shigella species as the cause. Management focuses on rehydration and targeted antimicrobial therapy when indicated, and public health measures to limit person-to-person spread in the daycare.

hints.text: A 48-year-old man presents to a general medicine clinic with a 9-month history of intermittent watery diarrhoea and crampy abdominal pain. He reports 7 kg weight loss and episodic pruritus around the buttocks and perianal region. For the last 3 weeks he has had a dry cough and low-grade fevers. On exam he appears thin, temperature 37.8°C, abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness, and there is faint erythema with excoriation in the perianal area.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes treated with metformin and inhaled corticosteroid for asthma. He received a 7-day course of oral corticosteroids 8 weeks ago for an exacerbation. He was born and raised in a rural province of Southeast Asia and worked barefoot in rice paddies until emigrating 18 years ago. He recalls similar mild gastrointestinal complaints intermittently over many years.

Complete blood count shows white cell count 13.6 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 3.2 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein is 6 mg/L. Three separate standard stool microscopy examinations for ova and cysts were reported as negative.

Chest radiograph obtained for cough shows patchy bilateral peripheral air‑space opacities that are not lobar and appear migratory on comparison with imaging performed 2 weeks earlier. CT thorax demonstrates small bilateral ground-glass nodules without consolidation.

Given eosinophilia, chronic GI symptoms and migratory pulmonary findings, he received a course of antihelmintic therapy with ivermectin. Within 10 days his diarrhoea and cough resolved, eosinophil count fell to 0.2 × 10^9/L, and an agar-plate stool culture obtained after treatment demonstrated motile larvae identified on microscopy consistent with rhabditiform larvae; repeat stool testing became negative.

explanation: This presentation is most consistent with a chronic helminth infection that can autoinfect and cause pulmonary migration and a pruritic perianal eruption, with eosinophilia and a clear worsening after systemic corticosteroids. Recovery after ivermectin and demonstration of rhabditiform larvae on agar-plate culture confirm the diagnosis of Strongyloides stercoralis. Key teaching points are the possibility of decades-long infection in persons from endemic rural areas, intermittent GI and cutaneous symptoms, peripheral eosinophilia despite earlier negative routine stool microscopy, migratory pulmonary infiltrates during larval migration, and the risk of clinical deterioration after systemic corticosteroids.

hints.text: A 52-year-old woman presents to clinic with a 5-week history of a slowly enlarging papule on the dorsal aspect of her right index finger that ulcerated and intermittently drains serous fluid. Over the past 10 days she noticed two tender subcutaneous nodules ascending the forearm. She has no fever and is otherwise well.

She is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, and is not diabetic. Primary care treated the finger lesion with two courses of oral beta-lactam antibiotics without improvement. Symptoms progressed despite wound care, and she denies recent travel or known insect bites.

Blood tests show white blood cell count 7.6 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 4 mg/L. Bacterial swab and routine cultures from the finger are sterile. Punch biopsy of the primary lesion shows chronic granulomatous inflammation with occasional small yeastlike structures on periodic acid Schiff and silver stains; fungal culture was submitted and is pending.

Ultrasound of the forearm demonstrates multiple small, well defined, hypoechoic subcutaneous nodules in a linear arrangement following the superficial lymphatic distribution from the hand toward the elbow. There is no fluid collection and no involvement of tendon sheaths.

On further questioning she recalls a puncture to the right finger from a rose thorn while pruning at the plant nursery where she works about two weeks before the lesion appeared. After 7 days of incubation at 25 °C submitted fungal culture produced mouldy colonies; subculture at 37 °C converted to yeast. Microscopy of the mould phase showed slender septate hyphae with conidia borne in rosettelike clusters.

explanation: The combination of an indolent ulcerating papule on the hand with proximal nodules in a lymphatic distribution following a thorn injury, granulomatous histology with sparse yeast forms, and a dimorphic fungus that produces mould-phase rosette conidia strongly indicates Sporothrix schenckii. Clinical differential includes atypical mycobacteria and Nocardia, but negative bacterial cultures, fungal conversion between mould and yeast phases on temperature shift, and the rosette conidial arrangement on microscopy point to this dimorphic environmental fungus. First-line therapy for cutaneous and lymphocutaneous disease is oral itraconazole; amphotericin formulations are reserved for severe disseminated infection.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after two witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizures in 48 hours. She reports three months of progressively worse frontal headaches and intermittent confusion. No prior seizure history. On arrival she is afebrile, oriented but slow to respond, and has no focal motor deficit.

Born and raised in a rural Andean community, emigrated 6 years ago and works as a school kitchen assistant. No chronic medical problems and no immunosuppressive therapy. Family members report one relative had intermittent diarrhoea during the past year. She denies recent international travel and has never been treated for intestinal parasites.

Full blood count: WBC 8.4 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 0.35 × 10^9/L. CRP normal. HIV antigen/antibody negative. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 18 cm H2O, CSF leucocytes 22 × 10^6/L with lymphocyte predominance, protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L. Single stool microscopy for ova and parasites reported as negative.

Non-contrast CT of the head shows multiple small cortical calcifications. Contrast MRI demonstrates numerous parenchymal and some subarachnoid cystic lesions at different stages, with several ring-enhancing lesions and surrounding vasogenic oedema.

Household review revealed that a close relative returned from seasonal work on a pig farm several months before the patient’s symptoms and later noted passage of long, white, ribbon-like segments in stool. The patient frequently ate home-prepared pork dishes at family gatherings and no household member received anti-parasitic treatment.

explanation: The combination of new-onset adult seizures, months of headaches, imaging showing multiple cystic brain lesions in different stages including calcifications, and household exposure to a person who likely harboured an intestinal tapeworm points to central nervous system infection by the larval stage of the pork tapeworm, Taenia solium. Human cystic lesions arise after ingestion of eggs shed by an infected human carrier, which explains the relevance of a household contact with ribbon-like segments in stool. Diagnosis is supported by characteristic CT/MRI findings and by specific serology or antigen detection when available. Management includes seizure control, antiparasitic therapy with albendazole (often combined with praziquantel in selected cases) plus corticosteroids to reduce inflammatory response, and treatment of the intestinal carrier to prevent further transmission.

hints.text: A 38-year-old man presented with three weeks of progressive, dull bifrontal headache, episodic confusion, and new right-sided weakness. He had persistent low-grade fevers to 38.2°C and became increasingly disoriented with impaired attention during the prior week. He is six months after a deceased-donor renal transplant and was brought by his partner for acute deterioration.

Maintenance immunosuppression consists of tacrolimus and mycophenolate. He received basiliximab at transplant and a brief prednisone taper. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis was stopped three months ago after a hypersensitivity rash. He has no history of malignancy and no recent travel beyond the region.

Blood tests showed leucocytes 3.2 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphopenia 0.7 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein was 28 mg/L. Cerebrospinal fluid had 20 × 10^6/L mononuclear cells, protein 1.1 g/L, and glucose 3.2 mmol/L with paired serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L. CSF bacterial cultures and a multiplex viral PCR panel were negative.

MRI brain with gadolinium showed three ring-enhancing lesions measuring 1.5–3.2 cm. Lesions were centred in the basal ganglia and at the corticomedullary junction with surrounding vasogenic oedema and mild mass effect. There was no diffuse meningeal enhancement.

On focused exposure history he reported gardening in a shared allotment where a neighbour had recently added cat faeces to the compost. He also ate undercooked lamb at a family meal about four weeks before symptom onset.

explanation: The clinical picture is most consistent with intracerebral infection by Toxoplasma gondii. A subacute focal encephalopathy with low-grade fever in a solid-organ transplant recipient off trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, multiple ring-enhancing basal ganglia lesions on MRI, CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis, and recent exposures to undercooked meat and cat faeces together support this diagnosis. Differential diagnoses include post-transplant CNS lymphoma, bacterial or fungal abscess, and nocardiosis. Diagnostic confirmation may use CSF PCR for the parasite or brain biopsy when PCR is nondiagnostic. First-line therapy in this setting is a pyrimethamine-based regimen with sulfadiazine and leucovorin, with alternative regimens available for sulfonamide allergy. Empiric anti-parasitic therapy is often started while awaiting confirmatory testing given the risk of rapid neurologic decline.

hints.text: A 26-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent fevers, drenching night sweats, frontal headache, and progressive fatigue. She returned 6 weeks ago from six months of community health work in rural eastern Indonesia. On examination she is febrile to 38.8 °C, tachycardic, pale, and has a spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin.

She recalls a similar self-limited febrile illness at the same site about five months before presentation that resolved without medical care. She used mosquito nets inconsistently and missed several weeks of the oral prophylaxis she had been prescribed while in the field. She has no chronic medical conditions and is not pregnant.

Full blood count shows haemoglobin 86 g/L and platelets 78 × 10^9/L. Total bilirubin 48 μmol/L and lactate dehydrogenase 520 U/L, consistent with haemolysis. Blood cultures are sterile. A point-of-care malaria antigen test is positive for the pan-malarial antigen and negative for the falciparum-specific antigen. Initial thin smear describes low parasitaemia with occasional trophozoites.
Abdominal ultrasound confirms splenomegaly with a splenic length of 14.5 cm. The liver is normal in size and echotexture. No focal hepatic or splenic lesions are identified.

During her deployment she spent long periods at lowland and mid-altitude sites in eastern Indonesia where she was bitten frequently. After receiving an initial blood-stage antimalarial locally she experienced recurrent febrile episodes recurring on an approximate 48-hour rhythm. She was advised that a separate course to eradicate dormant liver stages would be needed but could not start it until screening for a relevant enzyme deficiency was performed.

explanation: This presentation fits relapsing non-falciparum malaria acquired in eastern Indonesia. The pan-antigen positive and falciparum-specific negative result, low peripheral parasitaemia with marked splenomegaly, a prior untreated febrile episode months earlier, and recurrent fevers every 48 hours point to Plasmodium vivax. P. vivax produces dormant hepatic forms that can cause relapses weeks to months after initial infection. Confirmation is by peripheral blood smear morphology and management requires both blood-stage therapy and a liver-stage eradication agent after testing for glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man attends the clinic with a 3-month history of intermittent painless visible haematuria and increased urinary frequency. Episodes produce small clots but no fever. He reports occasional right flank discomfort but denies dysuria. He is a current smoker with a 10 pack-year history. Vital signs are normal and abdominal examination is unremarkable.

Symptoms began insidiously after multiple courses of oral antibiotics for presumed urinary infection that gave transient relief. He has no history of pelvic irradiation, no known bleeding disorder, and is not immunosuppressed. He works in construction and has not had recent urologic procedures.

Blood tests show haemoglobin 116 g/L, white blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.3 × 10^9/L. Serum creatinine 88 μmol/L. Urine dipstick positive for blood and protein, nitrite negative. Midstream urine culture yields no growth on two separate samples. Urine cytology shows no malignant cells.

Renal and bladder ultrasound demonstrates irregular nodular thickening of the posterior bladder wall with punctate and linear echogenic foci consistent with calcification. There is mild right-sided hydronephrosis without an obvious intraluminal stone.

On further questioning he says that from age 8 until age 15 he regularly swam and worked in irrigation canals and a seasonal reservoir in the Nile delta region during school holidays. He recalls episodes of an itchy rash after entering the water as a child.

explanation: Chronic painless haematuria in a young adult, peripheral eosinophilia, bladder wall calcifications and a history of repeated freshwater contact in the Nile region point to urinary schistosomal infection. The next diagnostic steps would be urine filtration microscopy timed to midday to look for characteristic ova, and urine antigen testing or serology if needed. Management is antiparasitic therapy with a single recommended agent active against trematode worms, and urologic follow-up for obstructive changes and long-term risk of bladder pathology.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man is referred to hepatology for 3 months of progressive abdominal distension and early satiety. He describes increasing left upper quadrant fullness and fatigue. He reports occasional small-volume bright red rectal bleeding for several months but denies fever, night sweats, or overt jaundice. On exam he has tense ascites and a firm spleen palpable 8 cm below the left costal margin. He has no spider naevi or palmar erythema.

He emigrated 18 months ago. Childhood history includes recurrent bouts of diarrhoea in adolescence managed as community disease. He drinks alcohol only rarely and has no history of intravenous drug use or blood transfusion. He is HIV negative and otherwise immunocompetent. He reports prior treatment for an unspecified intestinal parasite as a child but has no records.

CBC shows haemoglobin 108 g/L, MCV 76 fL, white cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.5 × 10^9/L, platelets 90 × 10^9/L. Liver tests show ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, total bilirubin 18 µmol/L, albumin 40 g/L, INR 1.0. Serologies for hepatitis B and C are negative. Three separate stool ova and parasite examinations were reported negative.

Ultrasound and contrast CT show marked splenomegaly, large volume ascites, and echogenic linear thickening along portal tracts producing a pipe-stem appearance of the periportal regions. Hepatic contour is smooth, hepatic veins are patent, and there is prominent portosystemic collateralisation. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy demonstrates large oesophageal varices.

On targeted questioning he recalls growing up in a riverside village in north-eastern Brazil, where he swam and fished in slow-moving freshwater canals throughout childhood. A rectal mucosal biopsy obtained during colonoscopy showed granulomatous inflammation surrounding ovoid eggs about 140 µm in length with a single prominent lateral spine on light microscopy.

explanation: The combination of childhood freshwater exposure in an endemic region, chronic portal hypertension with massive splenomegaly and preserved hepatic synthetic function, peripheral eosinophilia, and rectal biopsy showing ovoid eggs with a lateral spine identifies the trematode species responsible. Periportal fibrosis producing presinusoidal portal hypertension and negative stool exams are known features in chronic infection, and tissue demonstration of characteristic eggs is confirmatory.

hints.text: A 38-year-old man presents with six days of fever and progressive, severe muscle pain. He describes diffuse myalgias worst in the thighs and shoulders, new exertional dyspnoea, and intermittent palpitations. He had a brief self-limited episode of diarrhoea about eleven days before presentation. On examination temperature is 38.2°C, pulse 110/min, there is proximal muscle tenderness and mild facial puffiness, but no focal neurological deficit.

Previously well chef, non-smoker, takes no regular medications. He stayed in his home region and reports no recent travel abroad. Symptoms began about eleven days after attending a private weekend gathering. No known sick contacts. No recent procedures or transfusions. He denies intravenous drug use.

Full blood count shows leukocytes 16.8 × 10^9/L with absolute eosinophils 3.6 × 10^9/L. C reactive protein 92 mg/L. Serum creatine kinase 2,400 U/L. High-sensitivity troponin I 280 ng/L. ALT 128 U/L, AST 162 U/L.

Cardiac MRI demonstrates patchy midwall late gadolinium enhancement and mildly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, consistent with myocarditis. MRI of the thighs shows diffuse T2 hyperintensity and contrast enhancement of proximal muscle groups, compatible with active myositis.

On targeted questioning he recalls sharing homemade cured sausages made from wild boar at the gathering three weeks earlier, some slices chewed but not fully cooked. Because symptoms progressed a percutaneous thigh muscle biopsy was obtained. Histology shows coiled larvae located within striated muscle fibres, each larva surrounded by a thin inflammatory capsule.

explanation: The combination of an initial gastrointestinal prodrome followed by fever, prominent myalgias, eosinophilia, markedly elevated CK, and myocarditis points to a parasitic muscle-invasive infection. The exposure to undercooked wild boar and the muscle biopsy showing encysted coiled larvae within myofibres are diagnostic of infection with Trichinella spiralis. Management for significant disease includes anthelmintic therapy such as albendazole and supportive treatment, with corticosteroids used for severe inflammatory complications like myocarditis or marked myositis.

hints.text: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 6 months of progressive fatigue and exertional breathlessness, intermittent crampy lower abdominal pain, and 3 to 4 loose stools daily that sometimes contain fresh blood and mucus. She reports an 8 kg weight loss. On examination she is pale, pulse 96/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, BMI 19 kg/m2, and there is mild perianal excoriation but no fever or organomegaly.

She was raised and still lives in a rural provincial town in Southeast Asia and works as a primary-school teacher. She describes limited latrine availability at the school, frequent contact with children who play in bare soil, and new-onset pica, specifically clay ingestion, developing over the last 4 months. She takes no immunosuppressants and has not received routine mass deworming in recent years.

Flexible sigmoidoscopy performed to evaluate chronic rectal bleeding shows patchy mucosal erythema and friability of the rectum and distal sigmoid. Several slender, white filamentous organisms are seen with their anterior ends embedded in the rectal mucosa, surrounded by mucous and petechiae.

When asked about exposures she notes that many pupils at her school have persistent diarrhoea and that open defecation near the school playground is common. She frequently washes soiled clothes by hand and sometimes handles soil and faecally contaminated material without gloves while supervising play.

Full blood count shows haemoglobin 78 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 68 fL, white cell count 7.5 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.2 × 10^9/L. Concentrated stool microscopy demonstrates brown, barrel-shaped ova approximately 50–55 µm long with clear polar plugs at both ends.

explanation: The combination of chronic bloody mucous diarrhoea, iron-deficiency microcytic anaemia, pica, direct visualization of slender worms anchored in the rectal mucosa, environmental fecal contamination, and stool ova with bipolar plugs identifies infection with the human whipworm. Management includes targeted anthelmintic therapy such as mebendazole or albendazole and community-level deworming plus improved sanitation and iron repletion. Follow-up stool microscopy after treatment confirms cure and guides repeat therapy if heavy infection persists.

hints.text: A 59-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abrupt onset of right arm weakness and slurred speech over 8 hours. She described a lancinating left-sided frontal headache and scalp tenderness that began 6 days earlier, and low grade fever since onset of the headache. On arrival she was afebrile, blood pressure 150/86 mmHg, and examination showed right arm drift and mild expressive dysphasia. No skin lesions were seen on the trunk or limbs on initial exam.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes and polymyalgia rheumatica treated with prednisone 10 mg daily for 3 months. She recalled having a febrile vesicular illness in childhood but could not remember the exact year. No recent travel, no known cohabitants with chronic illness, and no recent vaccinations.

Initial blood tests showed white blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Lumbar puncture on day 2 demonstrated cerebrospinal fluid white cell count 120 × 10^6/L with 80% lymphocytes, protein 1.2 g/L, and glucose 3.4 mmol/L with contemporaneous serum glucose 6.2 mmol/L. CSF bacterial cultures were sterile.

MRI brain on day 3 showed multiple acute cortical and subcortical infarcts in the left temporal and parietal lobes. MR angiography demonstrated segmental narrowing of branches of the left middle cerebral artery. Contrast-enhanced vessel wall imaging showed focal enhancement of the affected arterial segments.

On further questioning the patient reported that her 6-year-old grandchild developed fever and a widespread vesicular rash two weeks before her headache began. The grandchild was cared for in the home during the period immediately preceding the patient’s illness.

explanation: This is a reactivation of a neurotropic herpesvirus causing focal cerebral vasculopathy with multifocal cortical infarcts in an older patient receiving low-dose steroids and with recent household exposure to a child with a febrile vesicular illness. The combination of preceding unilateral head pain, CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein, segmental intracranial arterial narrowing with vessel wall enhancement, and recent exposure to a child with a vesicular rash is most consistent with varicella-zoster virus reactivation causing cerebral vasculopathy. Definitive confirmation would be detection of varicella-zoster virus DNA in CSF by PCR or demonstration of intrathecal antiviral antibody, and management includes intravenous antiviral therapy and consideration of adjunctive corticosteroids depending on clinical context.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of profuse watery diarrhoea and repeated vomiting. She reports passing large volumes of pale, watery stool described by family as 'rice-water' in appearance. On arrival she is tachycardic at 120 min−1, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and reduced skin turgor.

She returned 48 hours ago from a one-week visit to a coastal region after volunteer work following recent seasonal flooding. She has no chronic medical conditions, takes no regular medications, did not receive antibiotics during travel, and reports no blood in the stool or abdominal pain beyond mild cramping.

Initial blood tests show haemoconcentration with haemoglobin 185 g/L, WBC 7.2 × 10^9/L, sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 2.7 mmol/L, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L, creatinine 220 µmol/L. Stool microscopy shows large volumes of watery fluid and mucus but no fecal leukocytes. Stool culture pending.

Contrast CT abdomen performed to evaluate persistent oliguria shows markedly fluid-filled small-bowel loops without wall thickening, no colonic mucosal enhancement or mesenteric fat stranding, and no evidence of obstruction or abscess.

During travel she repeatedly drank untreated well and bottled water from street vendors and ate raw shellfish from a flooded estuary. Local public health notices described a cluster of severe watery diarrhoea among residents after the same exposures. Regional laboratories reported isolation from stool of oxidase-positive, comma-shaped gram-negative bacilli that produce yellow colonies on sucrose-containing selective medium.

explanation: The combination of abrupt, high-volume 'rice-water' diarrhoea causing rapid hypovolaemic shock, marked hypokalaemia and haemoconcentration, absent fecal leukocytes, recent exposure to untreated water and raw shellfish in a coastal flood-affected area, plus isolation of oxidase-positive comma-shaped gram-negative bacilli that ferment sucrose on selective medium, identifies Vibrio cholerae. Management priorities are aggressive volume repletion with oral or intravenous rehydration and electrolyte replacement, with adjunctive single-dose antibiotic therapy in severe cases to shorten duration of illness and fecal shedding.

hints.text: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 48 hours of fever and abdominal pain. Pain began as periumbilical cramping then localized to the right lower quadrant. She reports three watery bowel movements, one with streaks of blood. On exam temperature is 38.4°C, pulse 98/min, there is focal tenderness in the right lower quadrant without peritoneal rebound. Pelvic exam is unremarkable.

She is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no history of haemochromatosis or chronic liver disease. She has not received recent blood transfusions or iron supplements. She denies recent travel, antibiotic use, or new sexual partners. Symptoms developed abruptly while she was otherwise well.

Blood tests show white blood cells 12.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 10.4 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 72 mg/L. Stool microscopy shows fecal leukocytes. Routine stool culture grew small colonies of a gram-negative coccobacillus. Laboratory notes that yield increased after cold enrichment at about 4°C.

Contrast CT abdomen demonstrates concentric thickening of the terminal ileum and multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes up to 2.1 cm. The appendix appears normal. No free fluid or abscess is identified.

History obtained later disclosed attendance at a local market three days before symptom onset, where she ate undercooked pork sausages. A younger sibling who shared the meal had a brief, self-limited diarrhoeal illness the next day.

explanation: This patient has an acute ileitis with mesenteric lymphadenopathy and a stool isolate of a small gram-negative coccobacillus that shows improved recovery after cold enrichment, following ingestion of undercooked pork. Those features point to Yersinia enterocolitica. The presentation can mimic appendicitis because the terminal ileum and mesenteric nodes are inflamed. Invasive disease often requires targeted antibiotic therapy in symptomatic adults. Identification is confirmed by culture characteristics and laboratory methods that include cold enrichment.

hints.text: A 52-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of progressive, dull bifrontal headache, intermittent confusion, and new right upper‑limb weakness. He reports low-grade fevers and increasing gait instability. On examination he is afebrile, alert but disoriented to date, with right arm power 4/5 and a 2 cm crusted ulcer on the left temple noted on skin inspection.

He has been treated with prednisone 40 mg daily and azathioprine for autoimmune vasculitis for 6 months. He is a keen amateur gardener and recalls a minor scalp abrasion while working in compost three months earlier that failed to heal fully. He denies recent freshwater swimming or travel outside the region.

Blood count shows leucocytes 7.8 × 10^9/L. CSF obtained by lumbar puncture has leucocytes 120 × 10^6/L with 80% lymphocytes, protein 1.2 g/L, and glucose 3.0 mmol/L with serum glucose 5.5 mmol/L. Gram stain and routine bacterial cultures are negative. Viral PCR panel is negative. A punch biopsy of the temple ulcer was sent for histology and culture.

MRI brain with contrast shows multiple ill‑defined, irregularly enhancing cortical and subcortical lesions in the frontal lobes with surrounding vasogenic oedema. There is mild leptomeningeal enhancement adjacent to the largest lesion. The pattern suggests multifocal subacute encephalitis rather than a single abscess.

Environmental history reveals he rinses his sinuses occasionally with tap water and runs a warm household humidifier nightly. The dermatopathologist reports large amoeboid trophozoites and spherical double‑walled cysts 10 to 25 µm in diameter in the temple biopsy. Culture of a tap water sample and humidifier condensate yielded the same amoeboid organism by sequencing.

explanation: Key features point to a granulomatous amoebic encephalitis caused by an environmental free‑living amoeboid protozoan. The patient had prolonged T cell suppression from corticosteroids and azathioprine. The subacute course with multifocal enhancing brain lesions, a nonhealing skin lesion with both trophozoites and double‑walled cysts on tissue histology, and isolation of the same amoebae from domestic water sources together identify Acanthamoeba species. Diagnosis is supported by the combination of immunosuppression, cutaneous portal of entry, compatible neuroimaging, and environmental recovery of the organism. Management requires combination anti‑amoebic therapy guided by specialist infectious diseases input and aggressive reduction of immunosuppression when feasible.

hints.text: A 45-year-old woman presented with a 6-week history of progressive left submandibular swelling and intermittent low-grade fevers. The mass is firm and indurated, associated with reduced mouth opening and occasional scant seropurulent discharge onto the skin. She has type 2 diabetes treated with metformin.

She underwent extraction of a left lower molar 11 weeks before presentation. Healing of the extraction site was slow. She reports poor dentition and stopped a short course of oral amoxicillin after three days because the gum pain improved. No corticosteroid use or known immunodeficiency.

Contrast CT of the neck showed a 3.5 cm infiltrative soft tissue mass in the left submandibular space with multiple small low-attenuation foci consistent with microabscesses. A thin linear tract extended to the skin surface. No discrete enlarged cervical lymph nodes were identified.

She wears an ill-fitting removable partial denture for two years and admits minimal denture hygiene. She recalls frequent mucosal irritation along the left mandibular sulcus since the extraction. No intrauterine device, no recent travel, no animal bites.

Aspiration of the draining sinus produced thick yellow granules. Peripheral blood count was 9.8 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 42 mg/L. Gram stain of the granules showed gram-positive filamentous branching organisms embedded in dense material. Anaerobic culture grew slow, small colonies after several days, with retained susceptibility to penicillin.

explanation: Cervicofacial indurating mass with sinus tracts following dental extraction, poor oral hygiene, yellow granules in draining pus, gram-positive filamentous branching bacilli on Gram stain, slow growth under anaerobic conditions and penicillin susceptibility identify Actinomyces israelii as the most likely pathogen. The absence of significant lymphadenopathy and the tendency to cross tissue planes supported this diagnosis. Long courses of high-dose penicillin or amoxicillin are required for definitive therapy.

hints.text: A 54-year-old man presents in late May with 6 days of fever to 39.5°C, severe myalgias, frontal headache and anorexia. He became intermittently disoriented over the previous 24 hours. He returned one week ago from a 4-day deer hunting trip in a rural area and noted a small painful puncture on his left thigh. On examination he is febrile 39.2°C, pulse 110/min, blood pressure 100/62 mmHg, oriented only to person, no rash and no lymphadenopathy.

Medical history notable only for well controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin. He had seen his primary clinician on illness day 3 and was given oral amoxicillin for a presumed skin infection, with no clinical improvement. He reports no recent blood transfusion, no travel outside the region, and no chronic immunosuppression.

Admission blood tests show white cell count 3.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 2.0 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 115 g/L, platelets 78 × 10^9/L. AST 160 U/L, ALT 95 U/L, total bilirubin 12 μmol/L. C reactive protein 140 mg/L. Routine blood cultures remain negative at 24 hours.

Non-contrast CT head is unremarkable. Chest radiograph shows clear lung fields. Focused abdominal ultrasound demonstrates mild splenomegaly with longitudinal diameter 15 cm, no focal lesions or portal venous thrombosis.

He recalls removing an attached tick from the left thigh on day 2 of illness. Local public health reported recent detection of tick-borne organisms in the area. A Wright-Giemsa peripheral blood film submitted on admission later shows intracytoplasmic morulae within neutrophils. PCR on whole blood amplifies bacterial 16S rRNA consistent with an intracellular bacterium that infects neutrophils. Doxycycline started empirically produced defervescence within 48 hours.

explanation: The combination of recent exposure to an attached tick, acute febrile illness with headache and confusion, leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated transaminases, mild splenomegaly, intraneutrophilic morulae on peripheral smear and a positive PCR from whole blood is diagnostic of infection with Anaplasma phagocytophilum. Rapid clinical response to doxycycline supports a rickettsial-type intracellular bacterium that targets neutrophils.

hints.text: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with five days of progressive left lower quadrant abdominal pain, fever and anorexia. He reports one episode of vomiting and fewer than three loose stools. On exam he is febrile 38.6 °C, heart rate 102 beats per minute, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg. Abdomen is tender in the left lower quadrant with a palpable, mildly fluctuant mass.

Past medical history notable for type 2 diabetes treated with metformin and peripheral vascular disease. Two days before presentation his general practitioner started oral ciprofloxacin for presumed diverticulitis after phone assessment. He has no chronic immunosuppression, no recent travel outside the region, and no urinary symptoms.

Laboratory tests show white blood cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 132 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures remain sterile at 48 hours. CT-guided aspiration of the left pericolic collection produced foul-smelling purulent fluid. Gram stain of the fluid shows many neutrophils and pleomorphic Gram-negative bacilli. Routine aerobic plates incubated in air show no growth.

Contrast CT abdomen and pelvis demonstrates a 6.3 cm rim-enhancing pericolic collection with internal gas adjacent to the sigmoid colon, surrounding fat stranding, and sigmoid diverticula. There is no free intraperitoneal air to suggest frank perforation.

Two weeks earlier he underwent outpatient colonoscopy with removal of a 12 mm sessile sigmoid polyp. Anaerobic culture of the aspirate grew a bile-resistant Gram-negative bacillus that hydrolysed esculin on selective agar, and the isolate was resistant to penicillin but susceptible to metronidazole and beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations.

explanation: This patient has a postprocedural colonic-source intra-abdominal abscess. The aspirate yielded an anaerobic, bile-resistant, esculin-positive Gram-negative bacillus that produces beta-lactamase and is metronidazole susceptible. Combined with recent mucosal disruption from colonoscopy and typical localisation to the sigmoid colon, the organism is Bacteroides fragilis. Management requires drainage plus antimicrobial therapy active against anaerobic, beta-lactamase-producing Enterobacteriaceae-colonising organisms, for example metronidazole or a beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor regimen, guided by susceptibility testing.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to an outpatient clinic with 3 weeks of progressive right axillary pain and swelling and intermittent low-grade fevers up to 38.2°C. She reports fatigue and occasional night sweats. On examination there is a single tender, mobile 3.5 cm right axillary lymph node with overlying erythema but no fluctuance. A faint 6 mm erythematous papule is noted on the dorsum of the right hand.

The patient is previously healthy, takes no systemic immunosuppressive medications, and had a negative HIV test two weeks before presentation. She has not travelled recently and denies known tuberculosis exposure. Her general practitioner prescribed a 7-day course of oral cephalexin for presumed cellulitis with no clinical improvement.

Blood tests show WBC 11.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and CRP 36 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures incubated for 5 days remained sterile. Fine-needle aspiration of the lymph node returned serosanguinous fluid; cytology showed necrotizing granulomatous inflammation, but Gram stain and routine aerobic and anaerobic cultures of the aspirate were negative after 5 days.

Ultrasound of the right axilla demonstrates a 3.6 × 2.8 cm hypoechoic complex lymph node with central low-echogenicity suggesting necrosis and prominent peripheral vascularity on Doppler. Chest radiograph is unremarkable.

On further questioning the patient reports adopting a stray kitten about 5 weeks earlier. She recalls a superficial scratch on the dorsal right hand soon after adoption, followed days later by the small papule at that site and then progressive ipsilateral axillary enlargement over the subsequent weeks.

explanation: Regional tender lymphadenitis following a recent kitten scratch, an antecedent papule at the inoculation site, necrotizing granulomatous lymph node with sterile routine cultures, and a subacute course point to infection with Bartonella henselae. Confirmatory testing may include organism-specific serology or PCR on tissue or aspirate. Management for uncomplicated regional disease often includes oral macrolide therapy and consideration of node drainage if fluctuant.

hints.text: A 36-year-old man presents to clinic with 3 weeks of intermittent fevers, drenching night sweats, malaise, and progressive low back pain that now limits his ability to climb stairs. He reports 4 kg weight loss over the interval. Temperature 38.2°C. Exam shows focal lumbar paraspinal tenderness maximal at L4-L5 and restricted forward flexion. No rash or focal neurologic deficit.

He is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and is not immunosuppressed. Symptoms began insidiously and waxed and waned. Two weeks before presentation he completed a 7-day course of amoxicillin prescribed for presumed respiratory infection with only transient symptomatic improvement. He denies IV drug use, recent hospitalization, or international travel.

Blood tests show Hb 110 g/L, MCV 78 fL, WBC 5.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 3.6 × 10^9/L. CRP 46 mg/L, ESR 62 mm/h. ALT 68 U/L, AST 55 U/L. Two sets of blood cultures drawn on presentation reported no growth at 48 hours.

Contrast MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates L4-L5 spondylodiscitis with erosive endplate changes and a 1.5 cm right paravertebral phlegmon. No large epidural collection and no spinal cord compression. Radiologist comments favour an indolent subacute infectious process.

On focused reinterview he recalls attending a rural family slaughter and cheese-making event about three weeks before symptom onset. He handled aborted goat placentas and tasted unpasteurised soft cheese. Several goats in the herd had recently aborted and one relative developed a similar febrile illness.

explanation: The combination of subacute febrile illness with night sweats, modest systemic inflammation, vertebral osteomyelitis, and recent contact with parturient goats plus ingestion of unpasteurised dairy is most consistent with infection due to Brucella species. Blood cultures may be negative early or require prolonged incubation. Diagnosis is supported by culture from blood or tissue and by serology. Management in this patient requires prolonged combination antimicrobial therapy such as doxycycline with an aminoglycoside or doxycycline with rifampicin, and an extended treatment course for vertebral involvement with consideration of image-guided aspiration or surgical review if there is concern for instability or persistent collection.

hints.text: A 71-year-old woman on postoperative day 9 after emergency sigmoid colectomy presents from the ward with new fever to 38.9°C, rigors and systolic blood pressure 85 mmHg despite fluid bolus. Examination shows erythema and tenderness at the right internal jugular central venous catheter site. She has an indwelling urinary catheter placed at surgery.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease stage 3b. Postoperative course included 7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics for intra-abdominal sepsis and initiation of parenteral nutrition on day 2. She reports recurrent mucosal yeast infections treated intermittently with oral azole courses, most recently a 10-day oral course two months earlier.

Admission bloods show white cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 185 mg/L, creatinine 180 µmol/L, lactate 3.0 mmol/L. Paired blood cultures flagged positive; Gram stain from one bottle shows small ovoid budding yeast forms 2–4 µm in diameter. Serum (1→3)-β-D-glucan elevated at 350 pg/mL.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrates no valvular vegetations. Contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis shows small postoperative pelvic fluid collections along the pelvic sidewall without a drainable abscess. Chest radiograph is without focal consolidation.

After 36 hours the blood culture isolate produced small, smooth cream colonies on fungal media, failed to produce germ tubes in serum at 3 hours, and antifungal testing reported a fluconazole MIC of 32 mg/L with low MICs to an echinocandin. Given the patient’s age, recent azole exposure, parenteral nutrition and the laboratory phenotype, these findings point to the non-albicans bloodstream yeast most likely responsible in this setting.

explanation: Diagnosis: Candida glabrata. The patient has postoperative candidemia risk factors including central venous access, parenteral nutrition, broad-spectrum antibiotics and diabetes. Blood culture demonstrated small ovoid yeast and a negative germ tube test, which argues against species that form germ tubes within 3 hours. The isolate’s high fluconazole MIC (32 mg/L) with preserved echinocandin activity and recent azole exposure is characteristic of Candida glabrata, an older-patient non-albicans species with reduced azole susceptibility. Management considerations include removing or replacing intravascular lines and initiating an echinocandin pending species confirmation and susceptibility testing.

hints.text: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic on day 3 of illness with sudden onset of high fever to 39.4°C, severe symmetric pain and swelling of both wrists and ankles that limits walking, and a diffuse non‑pruritic maculopapular rash across the trunk. She describes intense fatigue and photophobia but denies bleeding, vomiting, or jaundice.

Previously healthy office worker, no chronic joint disease, not on regular medications. She returned 5 days ago from a 10‑day holiday on a tropical island where she spent most days on walking tours and markets. No recent vaccinations. She has not been pregnant and has no known sick contacts at home.

Complete blood count shows leukopenia 3.2 × 10^9/L with lymphopenia 0.6 × 10^9/L and platelet count 170 × 10^9/L. C‑reactive protein is 48 mg/L. Alanine aminotransferase 82 U/L. Rapid malaria test negative. A point-of-care test for a flavivirus antigen collected on day 3 is reported negative.
Ultrasound of the right wrist demonstrates tenosynovial thickening with small joint effusion but no erosions. Plain radiographs of hands and feet are unremarkable.

During her trip she had numerous daytime mosquito bites in an urban area that had an ongoing cluster of acute febrile illness characterised by high fever, rash and severe joint pains reported to local clinics. Several fellow travellers on the same tour developed similar syndromes.

explanation: The combination of abrupt high fever, severe symmetric polyarthralgia and tenosynovitis, early leukopenia with relative preservation of platelets, mild transaminitis, and recent travel to a tropical area with daytime mosquito exposure and local clusters of febrile arthralgia points to infection with chikungunya virus. The negative rapid flavivirus antigen test and lack of haemorrhagic features make dengue less likely. Confirmatory diagnosis is by virus detection by PCR in the first week of illness or by virus-specific IgM thereafter.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to the student health clinic with a 12-day history of dry cough and progressive hoarseness. Symptoms began with sore throat and nasal congestion, then developed persistent nocturnal cough and intermittent low-grade fever up to 38.0 °C. She has mild exertional breathlessness but no pleuritic chest pain. On exam respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, chest auscultation with scattered expiratory wheeze and faint bibasilar crackles.

She is a postgraduate student with intermittent asthma treated only with a salbutamol inhaler. She is a non-smoker and otherwise well. Her primary care physician prescribed a 5-day course of amoxicillin two weeks into the illness with no clinical improvement. She reports no recent travel, no known immunosuppression, and no sick contacts at home.

Blood tests show white blood cell count 7.4 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 4.8 × 10^9/L and lymphocytes 1.9 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein is 26 mg/L and procalcitonin 0.08 µg/L. Sputum Gram stain contains scant neutrophils and no predominant organisms. A viral respiratory PCR panel is negative.

Chest radiograph shows patchy peribronchial and perihilar interstitial markings, greater on the left, without lobar consolidation or pleural effusion. High-resolution CT performed because symptoms persisted shows bronchial wall thickening and scattered small peribronchial airspace nodules.

Over the past week three classmates in her seminar developed a similar prolonged cough. A roommate had a nasopharyngeal PCR that was positive for an intracellular atypical respiratory bacterium. The student’s own nasopharyngeal PCR returned positive for the same target, and several classmates had matching PCR results during the cluster investigation.

explanation: The history of a protracted dry cough with hoarseness, minimal systemic inflammation, lack of response to a beta-lactam, patchy peribronchial interstitial imaging, and a cluster among young adults points to an atypical community respiratory bacterium. Normal or mildly elevated white count, low procalcitonin, negative routine sputum Gram stain and positive nasopharyngeal PCR for an intracellular organism from multiple cases support the diagnosis of Chlamydophila pneumoniae. Confirmation in practice rests on PCR or a compatible rise in specific antibody titres. Treatment options effective for this pathogen include macrolides or tetracyclines, which are appropriate when beta-lactam therapy fails in an outpatient with this presentation.

hints.text: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 7 days of fever, frontal headache, dry cough and progressive dyspnoea. She reports profound fatigue and several rigors. She has known type 2 diabetes, well controlled. On arrival T 38.6°C, pulse 84 bpm, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 94% on room air. Auscultation reveals bronchial breath sounds and coarse crackles at the right lower lung field.

She was evaluated by her primary care physician four days ago and given a 5-day course of oral amoxicillin with no improvement. No recent travel outside the region. No chronic immunosuppressive therapy. She is an ex-smoker with a 10 pack-year history. No household sick contacts.

Blood tests show WBC 9.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 7.1 × 10^9/L, CRP 120 mg/L, ALT 78 U/L. Blood cultures pending and currently negative. Urinary antigen tests for pneumococcus and Legionella are negative. Sputum Gram stain demonstrates many neutrophils and few epithelial cells but no predominant organism.

Chest radiograph shows dense consolidation confined to the right lower lobe with a small right pleural effusion. CT thorax confirms focal lobar consolidation in the right lower lobe with adjacent ground-glass change and no significant lymphadenopathy.

On further questioning she reports that two weeks before onset she spent several days cleaning cages and drying feather debris while admitting a group of rescued parakeets and cockatiels to the wildlife rehabilitation centre where she volunteers. A co-worker who helped with the same animals developed conjunctivitis and fever shortly afterward.

explanation: The combination of subacute febrile respiratory illness with headache, poor response to a beta-lactam, mild transaminitis, focal lobar consolidation, negative common urinary antigens and a clear recent exposure to psittacine birds points to Chlamydophila psittaci infection. Diagnosis is supported by PCR of respiratory specimens or rising specific serology. Recommended treatment is a tetracycline such as doxycycline, with clinical and laboratory improvement expected within 48 to 72 hours of appropriate therapy.

hints.text: A 47-year-old man is seen in hepatology clinic for six months of intermittent right upper quadrant pain, progressive pruritus and dark urine. He reports two short hospital admissions last year for febrile episodes treated as cholangitis. He has lost 5 kg over three months. On exam he is jaundiced with mild hepatomegaly and scattered excoriations from scratching.

Medical history notable for well controlled type 2 diabetes and a laparoscopic cholecystectomy four years earlier for symptomatic gallstones. No heavy alcohol use. He immigrated from a rural area in Southeast Asia eight years ago and has not had recent intravenous drug use or blood transfusions.

Blood tests show white cell count 9.2 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.3 × 10^9/L. Alkaline phosphatase 620 U/L, gamma-glutamyltransferase 410 U/L, total bilirubin 48 µmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 94 U/L. Serum tumour marker CA 19-9 mildly elevated at 220 U/mL. Blood cultures from his recent admission were sterile after antibiotics.

MRCP demonstrates irregular, multifocal dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts, most pronounced in the right lobe, with periductal thickening. The extrahepatic duct is not obstructed and there is no discrete hepatic mass. Endoscopic cholangiography shows irregularity of small intrahepatic ducts without a single obstructing stone.

On further questioning he recalls frequent consumption of raw freshwater fish at family gatherings during childhood in his home village and again while visiting relatives two months before his symptoms worsened.

explanation: The combination of chronic cholestatic liver enzyme pattern, peripheral eosinophilia, recurrent cholangitic episodes, and imaging that shows diffuse intrahepatic ductal dilatation in a patient with a history of eating raw freshwater fish from an endemic area points to infection by a fish-borne biliary trematode. Diagnosis is established by finding characteristic operculated eggs in stool or bile or by demonstration of the trematode on cholangiography or sampling. Recommended therapy is a single recommended antihelminthic course targeted to hepatic flukes, with endoscopic management for complications such as obstructing stones or cholangitis when needed. The most likely pathogen is Clonorchis sinensis.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to a travel clinic with 10 days of profuse watery diarrhoea, six to eight loose stools daily, nocturnal urgency, crampy abdominal pain and 4 kg weight loss. She is ambulatory but mildly dehydrated on exam, heart rate 102/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, mucous membranes dry. There is no visible blood in the stool and she is afebrile at presentation.

She returned 3 weeks ago from a 3-week volunteer placement in coastal South America. Symptoms began about one week into her return. She has no chronic medical conditions, is not on immunosuppressants, and is not pregnant. She self-treated abroad with a 3-day course of ciprofloxacin with no clinical improvement.

Routine bloods show white cell count 8.9 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 128 g/L, CRP 18 mg/L, sodium 135 mmol/L and potassium 3.3 mmol/L. Stool bacterial culture is negative. A concentrated stool ova and parasite examination shows spherical structures about 8–10 µm in diameter that stain variably with modified acid-fast techniques and were initially reported as possible artefact.

Contrast CT abdomen performed to exclude intra-abdominal complication shows mild mucosal thickening of the terminal ileum and proximal ascending colon, without perforation, abscess, or high-grade bowel obstruction.

On further questioning she reports eating a prepackaged mixed salad and fresh raspberries from a supermarket chain while travelling. Local public health later notified a cluster of travellers who ate the same imported produce and developed prolonged watery diarrhoea with similar stool microscopy findings.

explanation: This clinical picture is most consistent with infection by a small coccidian parasite that causes prolonged, relapsing watery diarrhoea in travellers and in outbreaks linked to fresh imported produce. The key clues are the incubation after travel, lack of response to fluoroquinolone therapy, stool concentrated O&P demonstrating variably acid-fast oocysts measuring about 8–10 µm, and a common-vehicle exposure to fresh raspberries/salad. Differentiation from other enteric protozoa rests on oocyst size and the epidemiologic link. Confirmatory features (not given in earlier hints) include bright autofluorescence of oocysts and therapeutic response to trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents to a city clinic with a three-week history of profuse watery diarrhoea. He reports up to 10 loose stools per day, frequent nocturnal stools that wake him, crampy periumbilical pain, and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss. He feels lightheaded when standing. On exam he is afebrile, pulse 102/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, mucous membranes dry.

He was diagnosed with HIV five years earlier but has taken antiretroviral therapy intermittently because of unstable housing. He denies recent travel outside the region or antibiotic use. He reports sleeping rough and drinking untreated water from a river occasionally over the past two months.

Haemoglobin 95 g/L, white blood cell count 3.8 × 10^9/L, platelets 160 × 10^9/L. CD4 count 58 × 10^6/L, plasma HIV RNA 200 000 copies/mL. Sodium 129 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, albumin 28 g/L. Routine stool culture and stool antigen testing for Giardia were negative. Direct immunoassay for Cryptosporidium was negative.
Contrast CT of the abdomen shows diffuse small-bowel wall thickening and mild mesenteric fat stranding. The colon is grossly unremarkable. There is no obstruction, abscess, or biliary dilatation.

High-dose oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole was started on hospital day 4 after initial investigations. Stool frequency fell from 10 to 2 watery stools per day within 48 hours and orthostatic symptoms resolved. Electrolytes and appetite began to improve. Concentrated stool examined with a modified acid-fast stain later demonstrated ovoid, variably sized acid-fast oocysts.

explanation: The patient is an advanced HIV case with prolonged watery diarrhoea, small bowel imaging abnormalities, and low CD4 count. Negative routine stool studies and a modified acid-fast stain showing ovoid variably sized oocysts point to a coccidian parasite of the small intestine. Rapid clinical response to high-dose trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus the acid-fast oocysts is diagnostic for infection by Cystoisospora belli.

hints.text: A 45-year-old woman presented to primary care with 3 months of progressive fatigue, intermittent paresthesias in her hands and feet, and an unsteady gait. She reports reduced exercise tolerance and new pallor but no abdominal pain, melena, fevers, or weight loss. On examination she is pale, has decreased vibration sense at the toes, and a mildly wide-based gait.

Medical history includes type 2 diabetes treated with metformin for 6 years and well controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. She has no history of gastric surgery, no known autoimmune disease, and she does not follow a vegan diet. She has not previously received vitamin injections and takes no chronic NSAIDs or anticoagulants.

Blood work showed haemoglobin 87 g/L, MCV 110 fL, leukocytes 6.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 260 × 10^9/L. Serum cobalamin 95 pmol/L with an elevated methylmalonic acid. Serum folate was normal. Anti-intrinsic factor and anti-parietal cell antibodies were negative. Faecal occult blood testing was negative.
