Transabdominal ultrasound performed for intermittent bloating captured an elongated, linear, mobile echogenic structure within the lumen of the proximal small intestine that moved with peristalsis. No bowel wall thickening or free fluid was seen.

On targeted questioning she recalled a backpacking trip to northern Europe 4 months earlier during which she ate several servings of raw or lightly pickled freshwater fish purchased from local markets and served at informal stalls.

explanation: The combination of progressive macrocytic anaemia with neurologic symptoms, low serum cobalamin and elevated methylmalonic acid, negative intrinsic factor antibodies, visualisation of a long intraluminal linear parasite on ultrasound, and a history of eating raw freshwater fish points to infection with the broad freshwater fish tapeworm. Diagnosis is confirmed by identification of characteristic eggs or proglottids in stool. Management includes antiparasitic therapy plus cobalamin replacement and monitoring of neurological recovery.

hints.text: A 29-year-old female aid worker presents to a regional hospital 4 days after onset of fever and severe myalgia. She reports headache, sore throat, profuse watery diarrhoea and persistent vomiting, and one brief syncopal episode at home. On arrival temperature 39.4°C, pulse 120 min−1, blood pressure 86/52 mmHg. Examination shows conjunctival injection, dry mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal tenderness without peritonism, and scattered petechial spots on the trunk.

She returned from a 3-week assignment in a remote province of a West African country 6 days before presentation. She worked at a small health post providing basic nursing care. She has well controlled asthma. Six days before her symptoms she sustained an ungloved needlestick while inserting an intravenous line in a patient who was bleeding. She reports no chronic medications other than an inhaler and no pre-departure live-virus immunization for this deployment.

Initial labs show leukocytes 2.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 1.1 × 10^9/L, platelets 45 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 104 g/L. Serum sodium 128 mmol/L, potassium 4.9 mmol/L, creatinine 165 μmol/L. ALT 890 U/L, AST 2 100 U/L. Coagulation testing shows INR 2.3. Blood cultures obtained and sent. Malaria rapid test negative.

Chest radiograph shows mild bilateral interstitial shadowing and small bilateral pleural effusions. Focused abdominal ultrasound demonstrates mild gallbladder wall oedema and a small-volume free fluid in the pelvis. No abscess or obstructing lesion identified.

Local public health investigators report a recent cluster of unexplained fatal febrile illnesses in the nurse's field site. She attended a community funeral about 10 days before symptom onset and assisted in washing and dressing the body. Community contacts describe several attendees who developed high fever, vomiting and bleeding then died within days.

explanation: The clinical picture is a viral haemorrhagic febrile illness after travel to West Africa with high fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, mucocutaneous bleeding, hypotension, leukopenia, marked thrombocytopenia, transaminitis and coagulopathy. The history of caring for ill patients, an unprotected percutaneous exposure and participation in corpse preparation during a local cluster of fatal febrile disease strongly suggest infection with Ebola virus. Confirmatory testing is by real-time PCR for viral RNA at a reference laboratory. Management centres on strict isolation, barrier nursing, aggressive supportive care for volume depletion and coagulopathy, and discussion with public health authorities about contact tracing and experimental or approved antibody therapies where available.

hints.text: A 24-year-old female primary school teacher presents to the emergency department with 48 hours of severe frontal headache, photophobia, fever to 38.8 °C and several episodes of non-bloody vomiting. She reports neck stiffness and new diffuse blanching maculopapular rash across her trunk. She is alert with no focal neurological deficits on examination.

She is previously healthy, takes no regular medications, and is not pregnant. She works in a daycare where over the past week several toddlers had febrile illness with coryza and rash. She has not travelled recently and denies tick or mosquito exposures, recent sexual contacts of concern, or recent antibiotic use.

Blood leukocytes 11.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 6 mg/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 18 cm H2O, CSF white cells 220 × 10^6/L with 90% lymphocytes, protein 0.9 g/L, CSF glucose 3.6 mmol/L with paired serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L. Gram stain and bacterial culture of CSF are negative. PCR panel for common herpesviruses is negative. Viral culture and typing from stool and CSF sent to the reference laboratory.
Non-contrast CT of the head is normal. Contrast MRI shows mild leptomeningeal enhancement over the cerebral convexities, without parenchymal lesions or diffusion restriction.

Local public health reports several linked cases among the daycare cohort. Reference laboratory recovered a small non-enveloped RNA virus from both stool and CSF and identified it by neutralisation testing as serotype 30, matching strains recently implicated in regional summer outbreaks of aseptic meningitis.

explanation: Presentation and CSF findings are consistent with viral (aseptic) meningitis. The epidemiology of a daycare outbreak, recovery of the same non-enveloped RNA virus from stool and CSF, and serotype 30 neutralisation typing indicate an echovirus strain, a well-recognised cause of summer aseptic meningitis outbreaks in adults and children. Management is supportive; bacterial causes were excluded and antiviral therapy for herpesviruses was not indicated after negative PCR results.

hints.text: A 52-year-old male municipal groundskeeper presents in late spring with 6 days of fever to 39.0 °C, severe frontal headache, diffuse myalgias, and progressive lethargy. He now has new confusion and two episodes of non-bloody diarrhoea. On exam he is febrile, mildly tachycardic, and has scattered petechial macules over both lower legs; no throat erythema or neck stiffness is present.

Medical history includes well controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He is not on chronic immunosuppressants. He was given a 5-day course of oral amoxicillin by his primary clinician after day 3 without improvement. He denies recent overseas travel and has been working outdoors on municipal parks for the last month.

Blood tests show leukopenia with white blood cell count 2.4 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 1.4 × 10^9/L, platelets 62 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 130 g/L. ALT 210 U/L, AST 160 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, CRP 118 mg/L. Blood cultures remain negative at 48 hours. Thick and thin malaria smears were negative.

Chest radiograph shows mild bilateral peribronchial cuffing without focal lobar consolidation. Abdominal ultrasound performed for persistent fever shows mild splenomegaly, largest diameter 13.8 cm, without focal lesions.

On targeted questioning he recalls removing an attached hard-bodied tick from his forearm 10 days before symptom onset while clearing an overgrown lot frequented by deer. Whole-blood PCR for bacterial 16S rRNA returned positive for an intracellular organism; repeat peripheral smear demonstrated intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies within circulating mononuclear cells. He began doxycycline and defervesced with marked clinical improvement within 48 hours.

explanation: The combination of recent removal of an attached hard tick, several-day febrile illness with headache and myalgias, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis, splenomegaly, and intramonocytic inclusion bodies on blood smear points to infection with Ehrlichia chaffeensis. Rapid clinical response to doxycycline and a positive whole-blood PCR for an intracellular bacterium that forms morulae in mononuclear cells support the diagnosis and distinguish it from agents that produce neutrophilic inclusions or primary viral illnesses.

hints.text: A 72-year-old man from a long-term care facility presents with 48 hours of fever, rigors and new confusion. He reports suprapubic pain and cloudy urine. On examination he is febrile 38.9°C, heart rate 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. A long-term indwelling urinary catheter is in place.

Three weeks earlier he underwent sigmoid colectomy for perforated diverticulitis with a 10-day postoperative stay in hospital. During that admission he received a 7-day course of a third-generation cephalosporin. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and stage 3 chronic kidney disease. He had a central venous catheter during the ICU stay that was removed before discharge.

Laboratory tests show white cell count 15.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 120 mg/L, creatinine 150 µmol/L. Urinalysis shows pyuria. Blood cultures flagged positive at 36–48 hours revealing Gram-positive cocci in short chains and pairs, catalase negative on preliminary testing.

Transthoracic echocardiography performed because of persistent bacteremia shows a 7 mm oscillating vegetation on the mitral valve and mild mitral regurgitation. Contrast CT of the abdomen shows no intra-abdominal abscess.

Empiric ceftriaxone and a glycopeptide antibiotic were started. Bacteremia persisted after 48 hours on that regimen. The isolate was reported resistant to ampicillin and to the glycopeptide tested, and bacteremia cleared within 72 hours after switching therapy to linezolid.

explanation: This hospital-acquired urinary-source bacteremia progressing to native valve endocarditis in an elderly patient with prior GI surgery and recent broad-spectrum cephalosporin exposure points to a gut-derived, Gram-positive coccus. The culture morphology of cocci in short chains, ampicillin resistance, and resistance to glycopeptide therapy with clinical response to linezolid indicate vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium as the most likely pathogen.

hints.text: A 29-year-old man presents to a dermatology clinic with a 3-month history of an itchy, slowly expanding rash in his groin and persistent discomfort between his toes. The groin lesion is a well demarcated, erythematous, scaly plaque along the medial thigh with peripheral scale and central pallor. Both feet show maceration and fissuring between toes, and the right great toenail is thickened with subungual debris.

He is an amateur weightlifter who showers daily at a commercial gym. He has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and reports intermittent high fasting glucose readings. There has been no recent systemic antibiotic use. A housemate developed a chronic scaly foot rash several weeks before his symptoms began.

Bedside dermoscopy of the groin plaque shows peripheral scaling with sharply advancing edge and sparse dotted vessels. High-frequency ultrasound of the affected toenail demonstrates subungual hyperkeratotic material and nail plate thickening without evidence of bone involvement on foot radiograph.

He previously applied a potent topical corticosteroid prescribed by a primary clinician and noted transient reduction in pruritus but then progressive enlargement of the lesion and more prominent central clearing. After stopping the steroid and using over-the-counter topical antifungal cream for 6 weeks his skin lesions improved slowly, but the nail abnormality persisted.

KOH preparation of skin scrapings shows long, branching septate hyphae. Culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar incubated at 25 °C yielded within 7 days a flat, suede-like, yellowish tan colony. Lactophenol cotton blue mount of the culture reveals numerous smooth, thin-walled, club-shaped multicellular conidia with 2 to 5 cells. No small single-celled conidia are observed.

explanation: The clinical picture—treatment-modified itchy annular groin plaque with concurrent interdigital maceration and onychopathy in a person using communal showers—points to a dermatophyte infection. KOH shows septate hyphae, and the culture morphology is decisive: colonies producing abundant smooth, multicellular club-shaped macroconidia with absence of single-celled microconidia identify the dermatophyte requested in this case. The distribution and culture findings are consistent with the organism that commonly causes tinea cruris, tinea pedis, and nail infection and does not invade hair. Management for skin disease can be topical allylamines or azoles; persistent nail involvement usually requires systemic therapy such as terbinafine.

hints.text: A 47-year-old male park warden presents with 6 days of fever, drenching sweats, malaise and a painful lesion on the dorsal aspect of his right hand. The lesion began as a small papule and developed into a shallow ulcer with a tense, erythematous border. He has a tender, 4 cm ipsilateral axillary lymph node that is warm and fluctuant on palpation. Temperature is 39.1°C and heart rate 100/min.

Previously healthy man with no chronic medical conditions and not taking immunosuppressive drugs. He first noticed the skin papule 3 days after a thorn-like puncture while working outdoors. He was prescribed oral beta-lactam antibiotics for presumed cellulitis three days before presentation with minimal clinical improvement. No recent foreign travel, no sexual risk factors, and no intravenous drug use.

Blood tests show leukocytosis 13.5 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and CRP 188 mg/L. Gram stain of material aspirated from the axillary node shows scant, faintly staining small coccobacillary forms. Routine blood and wound cultures remain sterile at 48 hours. After 72 hours a few tiny grey colonies grow only on enriched media; isolates are oxidase negative and weakly catalase positive.

Ultrasound of the right axilla demonstrates a 4.2 × 2.8 cm complex lymph node with central hypoechoic area consistent with liquefactive necrosis and peripheral vascularity. Chest radiograph is normal without focal consolidation or effusion.

On further questioning he recounts that 10 days before symptom onset he field-dressed several wild hares found dead along a creek and sustained a small skin puncture while skinning one animal. He did not wear gloves and recalls aerosolized splatter during the process.

explanation: The combination of an ulcerative skin lesion with ipsilateral suppurative lymphadenitis, scant faintly staining small coccobacilli on aspirate, poor growth on routine media but growth on enriched media, and a history of handling wild lagomorphs after finding dead animals is most consistent with infection by Francisella tularensis. This pathogen is fastidious and can require cysteine-enriched or chocolate-type media and prolonged incubation. Management for severe local disease includes aminoglycoside therapy such as streptomycin or gentamicin, or doxycycline for less severe disease, and surgical drainage when abscesses are present. Microbiology laboratories should be alerted because of the organism's infectivity and special culture requirements.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents with 72 hours of fever, severe myalgias and headache followed by 12 hours of progressive dyspnoea and a dry cough. He is an outdoor guide who is otherwise healthy. On arrival he is febrile 38.6°C, tachypnoeic at 28/min, heart rate 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg and oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Chest auscultation reveals diffuse bilateral crackles.

No chronic illnesses, no cardiovascular disease and no regular medications. He had a prodrome with nausea and brief vomiting three days before respiratory symptoms. He was seen at urgent care two days earlier and given a short course of oral antibiotics for presumed community-acquired pneumonia but symptoms progressed. He has not travelled outside the region and reports being immunocompetent.

Admission blood tests show leukocytes 18.2 × 10^9/L with a left shift, platelet count 62 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 172 g/L with haematocrit 0.52 L/L, creatinine 140 μmol/L and ALT 85 U/L. Arterial blood gas on room air reveals pO2 7.2 kPa and lactate 3.2 mmol/L.

Chest radiograph shows bilateral, diffuse interstitial and patchy alveolar opacities with small bilateral pleural effusions. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size. CT pulmonary angiography excludes embolus and confirms a pattern consistent with non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

On further questioning he recalls spending three days clearing out an enclosed, disused barn two weeks before illness, sweeping large volumes of dry rodent droppings and notes heavy dust exposure. He also remembers a small rodent bite to his hand during that clean-up.

explanation: The combination of a brief flu-like prodrome followed by rapid-onset respiratory failure with non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, thrombocytopenia, haemoconcentration and a history of heavy aerosolised exposure to rodent excreta is most consistent with hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome. Diagnosis is confirmed by detection of specific IgM or rising IgG antibodies or by PCR on blood. Management is supportive with early intensive care, close haemodynamic monitoring and consideration of extracorporeal membrane oxygenation for refractory respiratory failure. Avoidance of further aerosol exposure and notification of public health are important.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 10 days of progressive jaundice, anorexia, nausea, dark urine and pale stools. She reports low-grade fevers and right upper quadrant discomfort but no vomiting or diarrhoea. Examination shows scleral icterus, mild tender hepatomegaly and no encephalopathy. Temperature 38.1°C.

She returned 3 weeks ago from a 4-week volunteer placement in a rural region of South Asia. While there she drank untreated well water and ate at family homes and market stalls. She takes no regular medications, has no history of chronic liver disease, does not use injected drugs and is not pregnant.

Liver tests show ALT 1 850 U/L, AST 1 320 U/L, total bilirubin 180 µmol/L, alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L. INR is 1.5. Serologies negative for acute hepatitis A IgM and surface antigen of hepatitis B. Autoimmune markers are negative. Serologic testing for an enterically transmitted hepatotropic virus returned positive IgM and PCR detected viral RNA in serum.

Abdominal ultrasound shows a mildly enlarged liver without focal lesions, a nonthickened gallbladder and no biliary duct dilation. There is mild periportal echogenicity consistent with acute hepatocellular injury. No portal hypertension stigmata.

Local health services notified an outbreak of acute jaundice among residents using the same communal well. Environmental testing found faecal contamination of the well and viral RNA matching the patient’s serum PCR was recovered from the water sample.

explanation: This is acute viral hepatitis acquired by faecal-oral transmission after travel, confirmed by positive specific IgM and detection of viral RNA and linked to contaminated drinking water. The clinical timing, outbreak linked to a communal well and exclusion of hepatitis A and B support infection with Hepatitis E virus. Imaging excluded obstructive causes. Pregnancy and immunosuppression increase risk of severe disease, but this patient is immunocompetent and not pregnant.

hints.text: A 42-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with an 18-month history of progressive stiffness and weakness in both legs, frequent falls, and new urinary urgency. She now needs a cane for short distances. Examination shows spastic paraparesis with increased tone in the lower limbs, bilateral ankle clonus, extensor plantar responses, and distal vibration sense mildly reduced.

She emigrated from Jamaica to the United Kingdom 12 years ago. Past medical history is unremarkable. She recalls a postpartum blood transfusion 20 years earlier and says she breastfed each child for about 18 months. She denies intravenous drug use and tests for HIV during immigration screening were negative.

CSF analysis shows 20 × 10^6/L white cells, 90% mononuclear, protein 0.9 g/L, glucose normal. Oligoclonal bands are absent. Peripheral blood lymphocyte count is 4.8 × 10^9/L. Serum autoimmune panels including anti-aquaporin-4 and anti-MOG are negative.

MRI of the thoracic spinal cord demonstrates a long segment T2 hyperintensity affecting the lateral corticospinal tracts with mild cord atrophy. Brain MRI is largely unremarkable without periventricular lesions or enhancing plaques.

Family screening reveals that her older sister, who remains in Jamaica, was recently treated for an aggressive peripheral T-cell leukaemia. Two of the patient’s adult nieces tested positive on serologic screening performed during contact tracing. Paired serum and CSF testing later returned high‑titre antibodies and proviral DNA consistent with a retrovirus endemic to her region of origin.

explanation: The combination of a slowly progressive spastic paraparesis with urinary dysfunction, thoracic cord long-tract MRI changes, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with elevated protein, Caribbean origin, remote transfusion and prolonged breastfeeding, and family clustering of an aggressive T‑cell leukaemia points to infection with Human T-lymphotropic virus 1. This virus can cause a chronic myelopathy, often with mild CSF inflammation and cord atrophy, and it is epidemiologically linked to regions such as the Caribbean. Diagnosis is confirmed by detection of specific antibodies in serum and CSF and by identification of proviral DNA in peripheral blood. Management is largely supportive and immunomodulatory in the inflammatory phase, with limited options to eradicate proviral infection.

hints.text: A 28-year-old primary school teacher presents in midwinter with 36 hours of fever, malaise, sore throat and a dry cough. She also reports sudden onset of severe bilateral calf pain overnight that now prevents her from running. Maximum measured temperature at home was 39.2°C. On arrival she is febrile, pulse 110 beats per minute, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, pharyngeal erythema and scattered bibasilar crackles on chest auscultation.

She has mild intermittent asthma treated with inhaled corticosteroid only. She received the current season's inactivated quadrivalent respiratory vaccine five months earlier. One 8-year-old pupil in her classroom was absent last week with febrile myalgias. No recent travel, no pets or farm animal contact, no new medications.

Bloods show white cell count 3.2 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.8 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 22 mg/L, creatine kinase 4,200 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 34 U/L. Urine dipstick negative for blood. Point-of-care rapid respiratory antigen test on a nasopharyngeal swab was negative.

Chest radiograph demonstrates mild peribronchial cuffing without focal lobar consolidation. Duplex ultrasound of the legs excludes deep venous thrombosis. MRI of both calves shows diffuse symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the gastrocnemius muscles consistent with acute myositis.

Oral neuraminidase inhibitor was started 30 hours after symptom onset. Fever resolved within 36 hours and creatine kinase fell steadily over 72 hours. Reverse transcription PCR from the admission nasopharyngeal swab later amplified a segmented negative-sense RNA virus in the Orthomyxoviridae family whose sequence best matched a human-restricted seasonal lineage that circulates primarily among children rather than through animal reservoirs.

explanation: Diagnosis: Influenza B virus. The case shows an acute seasonal respiratory syndrome with a classroom cluster, prominent skeletal muscle involvement with marked CK elevation, lymphopenia, and an initially negative rapid antigen test but positive RT-PCR for a segmented negative-sense orthomyxovirus that is human-restricted. Clinical improvement after a neuraminidase inhibitor and the sequence matching a human-only seasonal lineage differentiate this from zoonotic orthomyxoviruses and support the diagnosis of Influenza B virus.

hints.text: A 44-year-old woman presented to neurology clinic with three weeks of progressive confusion, word-finding difficulty, and a new right-sided clumsiness. Symptoms began insidiously and worsened over days. She denied fever, headache, or neck stiffness. On exam she was disoriented to time, had expressive aphasia, a right pronator drift and 4/5 strength in the right arm and leg.

She has relapsing-remitting demyelinating disease treated with intravenous monoclonal therapy targeting α4-integrin. Therapy started 20 months ago after inadequate control with interferon. She received several short courses of high-dose corticosteroids for relapses in the past two years. No history of organ transplant. She reports no recent travel or sick contacts.

Basic blood tests were unremarkable, C-reactive protein 2 mg/L. HIV antigen/antibody screen was negative. Lumbar puncture showed clear CSF, WBC 4 × 10^6/L (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 0.7 g/L, glucose 3.6 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.4 mmol/L). CSF bacterial culture and fungal stains were negative. CSF PCR returned positive for DNA from a human polyomavirus.

MRI brain demonstrated multiple asymmetric T2/fluid-attenuated inversion recovery hyperintense lesions in the subcortical white matter of the left parietal and occipital lobes and smaller lesions in the frontal white matter. Lesions involved U-fibres, showed minimal or no mass effect, and did not enhance after gadolinium administration.

Review of her records showed she had a positive serology for prior exposure to the same human polyomavirus before starting the monoclonal therapy, and treatment was continued for 20 months until symptom onset.

explanation: Subacute focal neurological deficits in a patient on prolonged α4-integrin monoclonal therapy, MRI showing multifocal non-enhancing white matter lesions that spare mass effect, and CSF positive for a human polyomavirus DNA point to progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy due to JC virus. The immunomodulatory therapy and prior seropositivity increase risk, and the absence of enhancement and mass effect help distinguish this from inflammatory demyelination or abscess. Confirmation relies on the compatible MRI pattern plus detection of viral DNA in CSF.

hints.text: A 34-year-old male rice farmer presents to the emergency department with 6 days of fever, severe calf myalgias, frontal headache, nausea, and dark urine. He reports progressive oliguria over 24 hours. On arrival temperature 38.9°C, pulse 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. Exam shows scleral icterus, diffuse muscle tenderness of the calves, and no rash.

Medical history notable only for mild seasonal asthma. He was seen by his primary care physician on day 3 and given oral amoxicillin for presumed pharyngitis without improvement. He has not had prior liver disease, does not drink heavily, and takes no regular medications. Symptoms began abruptly after a week of strenuous field work.

Blood tests show WBC 14.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 85 × 10^9/L, creatinine 320 µmol/L, total bilirubin 260 µmol/L with ALT 110 U/L and AST 220 U/L. CK 1200 U/L. Urinalysis demonstrates 2+ protein, microscopic haematuria and numerous granular casts. Blood cultures are sterile. Acute viral hepatitis serologies are negative.

Chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy airspace opacities. A subsequent CT thorax demonstrates diffuse bilateral alveolar consolidation with small areas of ground glass opacity, greater at the lung bases, without focal lobar consolidation.

On careful questioning he recalls slipping into a muddy irrigation channel while cleaning a flood-damaged barn 10 days before fever onset, and water splashed into his eyes. Local public health laboratory reports blood PCR positive for spirochetal DNA and a microscopic agglutination test returned a high titre to serovars commonly carried by rodents.

explanation: The combination of acute febrile illness with severe myalgias (especially calf tenderness), conjunctival hyperemia progressing to scleral icterus, disproportionate hyperbilirubinaemia with modest transaminase elevation, acute kidney injury with proteinuria and haematuria, thrombocytopenia, pulmonary infiltrates consistent with alveolar haemorrhage, and recent freshwater exposure with mucosal splash strongly indicates infection by Leptospira interrogans. Supportive laboratory confirmation includes detection of spirochetal DNA by PCR and high microscopic agglutination test titres against rodent-associated serovars. Management requires prompt antimicrobial therapy and supportive care for renal and respiratory failure.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presented to clinic with a 3-week history of an enlarging, painful patch on the right parietal scalp and increasing hair loss. The area measures about 4 cm, is erythematous and scaly, and contains multiple broken hairs and superficial crusts. She reports pruritus and low-grade fevers, and she has difficulty brushing her hair over the lesion.

The patient is otherwise healthy and takes no systemic immunosuppressive medications. Two weeks before presentation she applied an over-the-counter medium-potency topical corticosteroid cream for presumed seborrhoea after the lesion appeared. She has no recent travel, no recent systemic antibiotics, and no known household members with a similar scalp problem.

Scalp scrapings and plucked hairs sent for direct microscopy with potassium hydroxide preparation show abundant arthroconidia coating the hair shafts and fragmented hairs, consistent with an ectothrix pattern. Full blood count is 7.9 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein is 16 mg/L. Routine bacterial culture of crusted material is negative.
Examination with a Wood's lamp in a darkened room produces bright green fluorescence of multiple affected hairs and adjacent scale within the lesion.

On further questioning she reports that she adopted a 3-month-old stray kitten from a local shelter 10 days before the scalp lesion began. The kitten has patchy alopecia and crusted lesions on the face. The shelter noted recent clusters of dermatologic disease among young cats.

explanation: The presentation is an inflammatory tinea capitis with broken hairs and crusting. Direct microscopy showing ectothrix arthroconidia and bright green Wood's lamp fluorescence point to a zoophilic dermatophyte that invades the hair shaft from outside. Recent close contact with an infected kitten identifies the most likely pathogen as Microsporum canis. Confirmation is by culture. Management includes systemic antifungal therapy and treating the animal reservoir to prevent reinfection.
